Boris Burkov

3 items
Patches Submitted (2)
[PATCH] btrfs: fallback to transaction csum tree on a commit root csum miss Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies

This patch introduces a fallback mechanism in the Btrfs file system to handle cases where the commit root checksum is missing, which can occur when an extent is relocated within a transaction and its checksum is not updated. The patch allows the system to fall back to searching for the checksum in the transaction csum tree, ensuring that data integrity is maintained even in these edge cases. This change addresses a specific issue related to relocation and commit root checksums, which has been observed in production environments.

Last comment 2026-06-13 [PATCH] btrfs: release extent lock per folio in readahead Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch modifies the btrfs readahead function to release extent locks per folio, rather than holding them for the entire readahead loop. This change is intended to prevent a race condition between readahead and direct IO writes that was introduced in kernel version 6.14. The patch reverts the behavior of locking/unlocking extents as a unit around the readahead loop, which was changed in kernel version 6.12. By releasing extent locks per folio, overlapping reads can proceed without blocking on the extent lock.

2 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Qu Wenruo ↳ replying to Boris Burkov Reviewed-by Neutral LLM
Reviewer Qu Wenruo noted that the patch does not address a potential issue where compressed readahead can expand the RA range, resulting in folios without extent locks, but assured that direct reads on compressed extents will always fallback to buffered reads and thus there is no window to race with direct reads.
Show original comment 
Looks good to me.

Reviewed-by: Qu Wenruo <wqu@suse.com>


Just to mention that Sashiko gave a review with level high, which 
doesn't seem to be caused by this patch itself, and seems to be a false 
alert.

Btrfs compressed readahead can expand the RA range, resulting folios 
that has no extent lock at all.
Thus in theory we still can have some folios without extent lock for 
compressed readahead.

But thankfully direct read on compressed extent will always fallback to 
buffered read, thus there is no window to race with direct reads.

Thanks,
Qu
View on lore ↗
Signals: NEEDS_WORK, POSITIVE
Qu Wenruo Inline Review Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer Qu Wenruo noted that unlocking the extent lock for a folio in readahead can still cause issues if other threads are holding locks on overlapping ranges, and suggested adding an extra check to ensure the unlock is only done within the original readahead range.
Show original comment 
Wait, this is still problematic, that we can clear the EXTENT_LOCK for 
ranges that is held by other threads.

The read itself is protected by folio lock so we won't race, but some 
other readers may got their extent lock unlocked still seems problematic.

Can we do an extra check on the folio range and only unlock if it's 
inside the original RA range?

Thanks,
Qu

Dmitry Ilvokhin

5 items
Patches Submitted (4)
Ongoing Submitted 2026-06-04 Last comment 2026-06-12 ⏱ No recent activity [PATCH v7 0/3] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch series adds a new tracepoint called contended_release that fires on the lock holder's side when a lock is released while there are waiters. This allows for more accurate measurement of hold time and provides additional information about the lock holder's stack at release time. The patch also addresses issues with existing contention_begin/contention_end tracepoints, which only work for certain types of locks.

View on lore ↗
tip-bot2 Ilvokhin Inline Review Needs Work LLM
The reviewer noted that the patch introduces a new function __percpu_up_read() which is called from percpu_down_read_trylock(), but this function does not drop the lock before calling try_to_unmap(). The reviewer requested that the lock be dropped before calling try_to_unmap() to avoid potential issues with reclaim paths.
Show original comment 
The following commit has been merged into the locking/core branch of tip:

Commit-ID:     7cfa62cf9432df67b1c95a59984a03a3bc023f98
Gitweb:        https://git.kernel.org/tip/7cfa62cf9432df67b1c95a59984a03a3bc023f98
Author:        Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
AuthorDate:    Thu, 04 Jun 2026 07:15:06 
Committer:     Peter Zijlstra <peterz@infradead.org>
CommitterDate: Thu, 11 Jun 2026 13:41:25 +02:00

locking/percpu-rwsem: Extract __percpu_up_read()

Move the percpu_up_read() slowpath out of the inline function into a new
__percpu_up_read() to avoid binary size increase from adding a
tracepoint to an inlined function.

Signed-off-by: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
Signed-off-by: Peter Zijlstra (Intel) <peterz@infradead.org>
Acked-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
Link: https://patch.msgid.link/3dd2a1b9ab4f469e1892766cb63f41d6b0f53d29.1780506267.git.d@ilvokhin.com
---
 include/linux/percpu-rwsem.h  | 15 +++------------
 kernel/locking/percpu-rwsem.c | 18 ++++++++++++++++++
 2 files changed, 21 insertions(+), 12 deletions(-)

diff --git a/include/linux/percpu-rwsem.h b/include/linux/percpu-rwsem.h
index c8cb010..39d5bf8 100644
--- a/include/linux/percpu-rwsem.h
+++ b/include/linux/percpu-rwsem.h
@@ -107,6 +107,8 @@ static inline bool percpu_down_read_trylock(struct percpu_rw_semaphore *sem)
 	return ret;
 }
 
+extern void __percpu_up_read(struct percpu_rw_semaphore *sem);
+
 static inline void percpu_up_read(struct percpu_rw_semaphore *sem)
 {
 	rwsem_release(&sem->dep_map, _RET_IP_);
@@ -118,18 +120,7 @@ static inline void percpu_up_read(struct percpu_rw_semaphore *sem)
 	if (likely(rcu_sync_is_idle(&sem->rss))) {
 		this_cpu_dec(*sem->read_count);
 	} else {
-		/*
-		 * slowpath; reader will only ever wake a single blocked
-		 * writer.
-		 */
-		smp_mb(); /* B matches C */
-		/*
-		 * In other words, if they see our decrement (presumably to
-		 * aggregate zero, as that is the only time it matters) they
-		 * will also see our critical section.
-		 */
-		this_cpu_dec(*sem->read_count);
-		rcuwait_wake_up(&sem->writer);
+		__percpu_up_read(sem);
 	}
 	preempt_enable();
 }
diff --git a/kernel/locking/percpu-rwsem.c b/kernel/locking/percpu-rwsem.c
index ef23446..f3ee7a0 100644
--- a/kernel/locking/percpu-rwsem.c
+++ b/kernel/locking/percpu-rwsem.c
@@ -288,3 +288,21 @@ void percpu_up_write(struct percpu_rw_semaphore *sem)
 	rcu_sync_exit(&sem->rss);
 }
 EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL(percpu_up_write);
+
+void __percpu_up_read(struct percpu_rw_semaphore *sem)
+{
+	lockdep_assert_preemption_disabled();
+	/*
+	 * slowpath; reader will only ever wake a single blocked
+	 * writer.
+	 */
+	smp_mb(); /* B matches C */
+	/*
+	 * In other words, if they see our decrement (presumably to
+	 * aggregate zero, as that is the only time it matters) they
+	 * will also see our critical section.
+	 */
+	this_cpu_dec(*sem->read_count);
+	rcuwait_wake_up(&sem->writer);
+}
+EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL(__percpu_up_read);
View on lore ↗

The following commit has been merged into the locking/core branch of tip:

Commit-ID:     76080d81e511f66eeb094b204e1e217bff674922
Gitweb:        https://git.kernel.org/tip/76080d81e511f66eeb094b204e1e217bff674922
Author:        Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
AuthorDate:    Thu, 04 Jun 2026 07:15:05 
Committer:     Peter Zijlstra <peterz@infradead.org>
CommitterDate: Thu, 11 Jun 2026 13:41:24 +02:00

tracing/lock: Remove unnecessary linux/sched.h include

None of the trace events in lock.h reference anything from
linux/sched.h. Remove the unnecessary include.

Signed-off-by: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
Signed-off-by: Peter Zijlstra (Intel) <peterz@infradead.org>
Acked-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
Link: https://patch.msgid.link/85e14a0685c1d46c3e80de2dc01358a48169b263.1780506267.git.d@ilvokhin.com
---
 include/trace/events/lock.h | 1 -
 1 file changed, 1 deletion(-)

diff --git a/include/trace/events/lock.h b/include/trace/events/lock.h
index 8e89baa..da978f2 100644
--- a/include/trace/events/lock.h
+++ b/include/trace/events/lock.h
@@ -5,7 +5,6 @@
 #if !defined(_TRACE_LOCK_H) || defined(TRACE_HEADER_MULTI_READ)
 #define _TRACE_LOCK_H
 
-#include <linux/sched.h>
 #include <linux/tracepoint.h>
 
 /* flags for lock:contention_begin */

The reviewer noted that the contended_release tracepoint fires after the lock is released and before the waiter is woken for some lock types, which may not be consistent across all lock types.
Show original comment 
The following commit has been merged into the locking/core branch of tip:

Commit-ID:     4f070ccb4dc4692e3b6757819fb80655f58b4f12
Gitweb:        https://git.kernel.org/tip/4f070ccb4dc4692e3b6757819fb80655f58b4f12
Author:        Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
AuthorDate:    Thu, 04 Jun 2026 07:15:07 
Committer:     Peter Zijlstra <peterz@infradead.org>
CommitterDate: Thu, 11 Jun 2026 13:41:25 +02:00

locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

Add the contended_release trace event. This tracepoint fires on the
holder side when a contended lock is released, complementing the
existing contention_begin/contention_end tracepoints which fire on the
waiter side.

This enables correlating lock hold time under contention with waiter
events by lock address.

Add trace_contended_release()/trace_call__contended_release() calls to
the slowpath unlock paths of sleepable locks: mutex, rtmutex, semaphore,
rwsem, percpu-rwsem, and RT-specific rwbase locks.

Where possible, trace_contended_release() fires before the lock is
released and before the waiter is woken. For some lock types, the
tracepoint fires after the release but before the wake. Making the
placement consistent across all lock types is not worth the added
complexity.

For reader/writer locks, the tracepoint fires for every reader releasing
while a writer is waiting, not only for the last reader.

Signed-off-by: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
Signed-off-by: Peter Zijlstra (Intel) <peterz@infradead.org>
Acked-by: Paul E. McKenney <paulmck@kernel.org>
Acked-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
Link: https://patch.msgid.link/02f4f6c5ce6761e7f6587cf0ff2289d962ecddd4.1780506267.git.d@ilvokhin.com
---
 include/trace/events/lock.h   | 17 +++++++++++++++++
 kernel/locking/mutex.c        |  4 ++++
 kernel/locking/percpu-rwsem.c | 11 +++++++++++
 kernel/locking/rtmutex.c      |  1 +
 kernel/locking/rwbase_rt.c    |  6 ++++++
 kernel/locking/rwsem.c        | 10 ++++++++--
 kernel/locking/semaphore.c    |  4 ++++
 7 files changed, 51 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)

diff --git a/include/trace/events/lock.h b/include/trace/events/lock.h
index da978f2..1ded869 100644
--- a/include/trace/events/lock.h
+++ b/include/trace/events/lock.h
@@ -137,6 +137,23 @@ TRACE_EVENT(contention_end,
 	TP_printk("%p (ret=%d)", __entry->lock_addr, __entry->ret)
 );
 
+TRACE_EVENT(contended_release,
+
+	TP_PROTO(void *lock),
+
+	TP_ARGS(lock),
+
+	TP_STRUCT__entry(
+		__field(void *, lock_addr)
+	),
+
+	TP_fast_assign(
+		__entry->lock_addr = lock;
+	),
+
+	TP_printk("%p", __entry->lock_addr)
+);
+
 #endif /* _TRACE_LOCK_H */
 
 /* This part must be outside protection */
diff --git a/kernel/locking/mutex.c b/kernel/locking/mutex.c
index 0953462..43b7f7e 100644
--- a/kernel/locking/mutex.c
+++ b/kernel/locking/mutex.c
@@ -1023,6 +1023,9 @@ static noinline void __sched __mutex_unlock_slowpath(struct mutex *lock, unsigne
 		wake_q_add(&wake_q, next);
 	}
 
+	if (trace_contended_release_enabled() && waiter)
+		trace_call__contended_release(lock);
+
 	if (owner & MUTEX_FLAG_HANDOFF)
 		__mutex_handoff(lock, next);
 
@@ -1220,6 +1223,7 @@ EXPORT_SYMBOL(ww_mutex_lock_interruptible);
 
 EXPORT_TRACEPOINT_SYMBOL_GPL(contention_begin);
 EXPORT_TRACEPOINT_SYMBOL_GPL(contention_end);
+EXPORT_TRACEPOINT_SYMBOL_GPL(contended_release);
 
 /**
  * atomic_dec_and_mutex_lock - return holding mutex if we dec to 0
diff --git a/kernel/locking/percpu-rwsem.c b/kernel/locking/percpu-rwsem.c
index f3ee7a0..f7e152c 100644
--- a/kernel/locking/percpu-rwsem.c
+++ b/kernel/locking/percpu-rwsem.c
@@ -263,6 +263,9 @@ void percpu_up_write(struct percpu_rw_semaphore *sem)
 {
 	rwsem_release(&sem->dep_map, _RET_IP_);
 
+	if (trace_contended_release_enabled() && wq_has_sleeper(&sem->waiters))
+		trace_call__contended_release(sem);
+
 	/*
 	 * Signal the writer is done, no fast path yet.
 	 *
@@ -293,6 +296,14 @@ void __percpu_up_read(struct percpu_rw_semaphore *sem)
 {
 	lockdep_assert_preemption_disabled();
 	/*
+	 * After percpu_up_write() completes, rcu_sync_is_idle() can still
+	 * return false during the grace period, forcing readers into this
+	 * slowpath. Only trace when a writer is actually waiting for
+	 * readers to drain.
+	 */
+	if (trace_contended_release_enabled() && rcuwait_active(&sem->writer))
+		trace_call__contended_release(sem);
+	/*
 	 * slowpath; reader will only ever wake a single blocked
 	 * writer.
 	 */
diff --git a/kernel/locking/rtmutex.c b/kernel/locking/rtmutex.c
index 9147d6a..28beae7 100644
--- a/kernel/locking/rtmutex.c
+++ b/kernel/locking/rtmutex.c
@@ -1470,6 +1470,7 @@ static void __sched rt_mutex_slowunlock(struct rt_mutex_base *lock)
 		raw_spin_lock_irqsave(&lock->wait_lock, flags);
 	}
 
+	trace_contended_release(lock);
 	/*
 	 * The wakeup next waiter path does not suffer from the above
 	 * race. See the comments there.
diff --git a/kernel/locking/rwbase_rt.c b/kernel/locking/rwbase_rt.c
index 82e078c..2835c9e 100644
--- a/kernel/locking/rwbase_rt.c
+++ b/kernel/locking/rwbase_rt.c
@@ -174,6 +174,8 @@ static void __sched __rwbase_read_unlock(struct rwbase_rt *rwb,
 static __always_inline void rwbase_read_unlock(struct rwbase_rt *rwb,
 					       unsigned int state)
 {
+	if (trace_contended_release_enabled() && rt_mutex_owner(&rwb->rtmutex))
+		trace_call__contended_release(rwb);
 	/*
 	 * rwb->readers can only hit 0 when a writer is waiting for the
 	 * active readers to leave the critical section.
@@ -205,6 +207,8 @@ static inline void rwbase_write_unlock(struct rwbase_rt *rwb)
 	unsigned long flags;
 
 	raw_spin_lock_irqsave(&rtm->wait_lock, flags);
+	if (trace_contended_release_enabled() && rt_mutex_has_waiters(rtm))
+		trace_call__contended_release(rwb);
 	__rwbase_write_unlock(rwb, WRITER_BIAS, flags);
 }
 
@@ -214,6 +218,8 @@ static inline void rwbase_write_downgrade(struct rwbase_rt *rwb)
 	unsigned long flags;
 
 	raw_spin_lock_irqsave(&rtm->wait_lock, flags);
+	if (trace_contended_release_enabled() && rt_mutex_has_waiters(rtm))
+		trace_call__contended_release(rwb);
 	/* Release it and account current as reader */
 	__rwbase_write_unlock(rwb, WRITER_BIAS - 1, flags);
 }
diff --git a/kernel/locking/rwsem.c b/kernel/locking/rwsem.c
index bf64709..b9c180a 100644
--- a/kernel/locking/rwsem.c
+++ b/kernel/locking/rwsem.c
@@ -1387,6 +1387,8 @@ static inline void __up_read(struct rw_semaphore *sem)
 	rwsem_clear_reader_owned(sem);
 	tmp = atomic_long_add_return_release(-RWSEM_READER_BIAS, &sem->count);
 	DEBUG_RWSEMS_WARN_ON(tmp < 0, sem);
+	if (trace_contended_release_enabled() && (tmp & RWSEM_FLAG_WAITERS))
+		trace_call__contended_release(sem);
 	if (unlikely((tmp & (RWSEM_LOCK_MASK|RWSEM_FLAG_WAITERS)) ==
 		      RWSEM_FLAG_WAITERS)) {
 		clear_nonspinnable(sem);
@@ -1413,8 +1415,10 @@ static inline void __up_write(struct rw_semaphore *sem)
 	preempt_disable();
 	rwsem_clear_owner(sem);
 	tmp = atomic_long_fetch_add_release(-RWSEM_WRITER_LOCKED, &sem->count);
-	if (unlikely(tmp & RWSEM_FLAG_WAITERS))
+	if (unlikely(tmp & RWSEM_FLAG_WAITERS)) {
+		trace_contended_release(sem);
 		rwsem_wake(sem);
+	}
 	preempt_enable();
 }
 
@@ -1437,8 +1441,10 @@ static inline void __downgrade_write(struct rw_semaphore *sem)
 	tmp = atomic_long_fetch_add_release(
 		-RWSEM_WRITER_LOCKED+RWSEM_READER_BIAS, &sem->count);
 	rwsem_set_reader_owned(sem);
-	if (tmp & RWSEM_FLAG_WAITERS)
+	if (tmp & RWSEM_FLAG_WAITERS) {
+		trace_contended_release(sem);
 		rwsem_downgrade_wake(sem);
+	}
 	preempt_enable();
 }
 
diff --git a/kernel/locking/semaphore.c b/kernel/locking/semaphore.c
index 74d4143..233730c 100644
--- a/kernel/locking/semaphore.c
+++ b/kernel/locking/semaphore.c
@@ -230,6 +230,10 @@ void __sched up(struct semaphore *sem)
 		sem->count++;
 	else
 		__up(sem, &wake_q);
+
+	if (trace_contended_release_enabled() && !wake_q_empty(&wake_q))
+		trace_call__contended_release(sem);
+
 	raw_spin_unlock_irqrestore(&sem->lock, flags);
 	if (!wake_q_empty(&wake_q))
 		wake_up_q(&wake_q);

Ongoing Submitted 2026-06-04 ⏱ No comments yet [PATCH v7 1/3] tracing/lock: Remove unnecessary linux/sched.h include Neutral LLM

This patch series removes an unnecessary include of linux/sched.h from the tracing/lock code. The change is made by simply removing the offending line, as none of the trace events in lock.h reference anything from linux/sched.h.

Ongoing Submitted 2026-06-04 ⏱ No comments yet [PATCH v7 2/3] locking/percpu-rwsem: Extract __percpu_up_read() Neutral LLM

This patch series extracts the percpu_up_read() slowpath into a new __percpu_up_read() function to avoid binary size increase from adding a tracepoint to an inlined function.

Ongoing Submitted 2026-06-04 Last comment 2026-06-12 ⏱ No recent activity [PATCH v7 3/3] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch adds a new tracepoint called contended_release to the Linux kernel's locking mechanism. It fires when a lock is released under contention, allowing for correlation of lock hold time with waiter events by lock address. The patch modifies the slowpath unlock paths of various sleepable locks, including mutexes, rtmutexes, semaphores, and rwsems, to enable this tracepoint. The placement of the tracepoint varies depending on the lock type, but it generally fires before the lock is released or after the release but before the wake. This patch also adds support for reader/writer locks, firing the tracepoint for every reader releasing while a writer is waiting.

Usama Arif ↳ replying to Dmitry Ilvokhin Acked-by Positive Heuristic
Gave Acked-by

tip-bot2 Ilvokhin ↳ replying to Dmitry Ilvokhin Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
The reviewer noted that the contended_release tracepoint fires after the lock is released for some lock types, which may not be consistent across all lock types as mentioned in the patch description.

The following commit has been merged into the locking/core branch of tip:

Commit-ID:     4f070ccb4dc4692e3b6757819fb80655f58b4f12
Gitweb:        https://git.kernel.org/tip/4f070ccb4dc4692e3b6757819fb80655f58b4f12
Author:        Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
AuthorDate:    Thu, 04 Jun 2026 07:15:07 
Committer:     Peter Zijlstra <peterz@infradead.org>
CommitterDate: Thu, 11 Jun 2026 13:41:25 +02:00

locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

Add the contended_release trace event. This tracepoint fires on the
holder side when a contended lock is released, complementing the
existing contention_begin/contention_end tracepoints which fire on the
waiter side.

This enables correlating lock hold time under contention with waiter
events by lock address.

Add trace_contended_release()/trace_call__contended_release() calls to
the slowpath unlock paths of sleepable locks: mutex, rtmutex, semaphore,
rwsem, percpu-rwsem, and RT-specific rwbase locks.

Where possible, trace_contended_release() fires before the lock is
released and before the waiter is woken. For some lock types, the
tracepoint fires after the release but before the wake. Making the
placement consistent across all lock types is not worth the added
complexity.

For reader/writer locks, the tracepoint fires for every reader releasing
while a writer is waiting, not only for the last reader.

Signed-off-by: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
Signed-off-by: Peter Zijlstra (Intel) <peterz@infradead.org>
Acked-by: Paul E. McKenney <paulmck@kernel.org>
Acked-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
Link: https://patch.msgid.link/02f4f6c5ce6761e7f6587cf0ff2289d962ecddd4.1780506267.git.d@ilvokhin.com
---
 include/trace/events/lock.h   | 17 +++++++++++++++++
 kernel/locking/mutex.c        |  4 ++++
 kernel/locking/percpu-rwsem.c | 11 +++++++++++
 kernel/locking/rtmutex.c      |  1 +
 kernel/locking/rwbase_rt.c    |  6 ++++++
 kernel/locking/rwsem.c        | 10 ++++++++--
 kernel/locking/semaphore.c    |  4 ++++
 7 files changed, 51 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)

diff --git a/include/trace/events/lock.h b/include/trace/events/lock.h
index da978f2..1ded869 100644
--- a/include/trace/events/lock.h
+++ b/include/trace/events/lock.h
@@ -137,6 +137,23 @@ TRACE_EVENT(contention_end,
 	TP_printk("%p (ret=%d)", __entry->lock_addr, __entry->ret)
 );
 
+TRACE_EVENT(contended_release,
+
+	TP_PROTO(void *lock),
+
+	TP_ARGS(lock),
+
+	TP_STRUCT__entry(
+		__field(void *, lock_addr)
+	),
+
+	TP_fast_assign(
+		__entry->lock_addr = lock;
+	),
+
+	TP_printk("%p", __entry->lock_addr)
+);
+
 #endif /* _TRACE_LOCK_H */
 
 /* This part must be outside protection */
diff --git a/kernel/locking/mutex.c b/kernel/locking/mutex.c
index 0953462..43b7f7e 100644
--- a/kernel/locking/mutex.c
+++ b/kernel/locking/mutex.c
@@ -1023,6 +1023,9 @@ static noinline void __sched __mutex_unlock_slowpath(struct mutex *lock, unsigne
 		wake_q_add(&wake_q, next);
 	}
 
+	if (trace_contended_release_enabled() && waiter)
+		trace_call__contended_release(lock);
+
 	if (owner & MUTEX_FLAG_HANDOFF)
 		__mutex_handoff(lock, next);
 
@@ -1220,6 +1223,7 @@ EXPORT_SYMBOL(ww_mutex_lock_interruptible);
 
 EXPORT_TRACEPOINT_SYMBOL_GPL(contention_begin);
 EXPORT_TRACEPOINT_SYMBOL_GPL(contention_end);
+EXPORT_TRACEPOINT_SYMBOL_GPL(contended_release);
 
 /**
  * atomic_dec_and_mutex_lock - return holding mutex if we dec to 0
diff --git a/kernel/locking/percpu-rwsem.c b/kernel/locking/percpu-rwsem.c
index f3ee7a0..f7e152c 100644
--- a/kernel/locking/percpu-rwsem.c
+++ b/kernel/locking/percpu-rwsem.c
@@ -263,6 +263,9 @@ void percpu_up_write(struct percpu_rw_semaphore *sem)
 {
 	rwsem_release(&sem->dep_map, _RET_IP_);
 
+	if (trace_contended_release_enabled() && wq_has_sleeper(&sem->waiters))
+		trace_call__contended_release(sem);
+
 	/*
 	 * Signal the writer is done, no fast path yet.
 	 *
@@ -293,6 +296,14 @@ void __percpu_up_read(struct percpu_rw_semaphore *sem)
 {
 	lockdep_assert_preemption_disabled();
 	/*
+	 * After percpu_up_write() completes, rcu_sync_is_idle() can still
+	 * return false during the grace period, forcing readers into this
+	 * slowpath. Only trace when a writer is actually waiting for
+	 * readers to drain.
+	 */
+	if (trace_contended_release_enabled() && rcuwait_active(&sem->writer))
+		trace_call__contended_release(sem);
+	/*
 	 * slowpath; reader will only ever wake a single blocked
 	 * writer.
 	 */
diff --git a/kernel/locking/rtmutex.c b/kernel/locking/rtmutex.c
index 9147d6a..28beae7 100644
--- a/kernel/locking/rtmutex.c
+++ b/kernel/locking/rtmutex.c
@@ -1470,6 +1470,7 @@ static void __sched rt_mutex_slowunlock(struct rt_mutex_base *lock)
 		raw_spin_lock_irqsave(&lock->wait_lock, flags);
 	}
 
+	trace_contended_release(lock);
 	/*
 	 * The wakeup next waiter path does not suffer from the above
 	 * race. See the comments there.
diff --git a/kernel/locking/rwbase_rt.c b/kernel/locking/rwbase_rt.c
index 82e078c..2835c9e 100644
--- a/kernel/locking/rwbase_rt.c
+++ b/kernel/locking/rwbase_rt.c
@@ -174,6 +174,8 @@ static void __sched __rwbase_read_unlock(struct rwbase_rt *rwb,
 static __always_inline void rwbase_read_unlock(struct rwbase_rt *rwb,
 					       unsigned int state)
 {
+	if (trace_contended_release_enabled() && rt_mutex_owner(&rwb->rtmutex))
+		trace_call__contended_release(rwb);
 	/*
 	 * rwb->readers can only hit 0 when a writer is waiting for the
 	 * active readers to leave the critical section.
@@ -205,6 +207,8 @@ static inline void rwbase_write_unlock(struct rwbase_rt *rwb)
 	unsigned long flags;
 
 	raw_spin_lock_irqsave(&rtm->wait_lock, flags);
+	if (trace_contended_release_enabled() && rt_mutex_has_waiters(rtm))
+		trace_call__contended_release(rwb);
 	__rwbase_write_unlock(rwb, WRITER_BIAS, flags);
 }
 
@@ -214,6 +218,8 @@ static inline void rwbase_write_downgrade(struct rwbase_rt *rwb)
 	unsigned long flags;
 
 	raw_spin_lock_irqsave(&rtm->wait_lock, flags);
+	if (trace_contended_release_enabled() && rt_mutex_has_waiters(rtm))
+		trace_call__contended_release(rwb);
 	/* Release it and account current as reader */
 	__rwbase_write_unlock(rwb, WRITER_BIAS - 1, flags);
 }
diff --git a/kernel/locking/rwsem.c b/kernel/locking/rwsem.c
index bf64709..b9c180a 100644
--- a/kernel/locking/rwsem.c
+++ b/kernel/locking/rwsem.c
@@ -1387,6 +1387,8 @@ static inline void __up_read(struct rw_semaphore *sem)
 	rwsem_clear_reader_owned(sem);
 	tmp = atomic_long_add_return_release(-RWSEM_READER_BIAS, &sem->count);
 	DEBUG_RWSEMS_WARN_ON(tmp < 0, sem);
+	if (trace_contended_release_enabled() && (tmp & RWSEM_FLAG_WAITERS))
+		trace_call__contended_release(sem);
 	if (unlikely((tmp & (RWSEM_LOCK_MASK|RWSEM_FLAG_WAITERS)) ==
 		      RWSEM_FLAG_WAITERS)) {
 		clear_nonspinnable(sem);
@@ -1413,8 +1415,10 @@ static inline void __up_write(struct rw_semaphore *sem)
 	preempt_disable();
 	rwsem_clear_owner(sem);
 	tmp = atomic_long_fetch_add_release(-RWSEM_WRITER_LOCKED, &sem->count);
-	if (unlikely(tmp & RWSEM_FLAG_WAITERS))
+	if (unlikely(tmp & RWSEM_FLAG_WAITERS)) {
+		trace_contended_release(sem);
 		rwsem_wake(sem);
+	}
 	preempt_enable();
 }
 
@@ -1437,8 +1441,10 @@ static inline void __downgrade_write(struct rw_semaphore *sem)
 	tmp = atomic_long_fetch_add_release(
 		-RWSEM_WRITER_LOCKED+RWSEM_READER_BIAS, &sem->count);
 	rwsem_set_reader_owned(sem);
-	if (tmp & RWSEM_FLAG_WAITERS)
+	if (tmp & RWSEM_FLAG_WAITERS) {
+		trace_contended_release(sem);
 		rwsem_downgrade_wake(sem);
+	}
 	preempt_enable();
 }
 
diff --git a/kernel/locking/semaphore.c b/kernel/locking/semaphore.c
index 74d4143..233730c 100644
--- a/kernel/locking/semaphore.c
+++ b/kernel/locking/semaphore.c
@@ -230,6 +230,10 @@ void __sched up(struct semaphore *sem)
 		sem->count++;
 	else
 		__up(sem, &wake_q);
+
+	if (trace_contended_release_enabled() && !wake_q_empty(&wake_q))
+		trace_call__contended_release(sem);
+
 	raw_spin_unlock_irqrestore(&sem->lock, flags);
 	if (!wake_q_empty(&wake_q))
 		wake_up_q(&wake_q);
View on lore ↗
Signals: inconsistency, tracepoint placement

No activity
Reviews Given (1)
Re: [PATCH v6 5/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch adds a contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock, which is a type of lock in the Linux kernel. The goal is to measure and understand the performance impact of inlining unlock operations on modern CPUs with shared L1 instruction caches. Initial benchmarks showed that inlining unlock can cause measurable kernel i-cache pressure, leading to a 1.27% throughput decrease on a workload-bound system. To mitigate this issue, the patch adds a tracepoint to collect data on contended releases, allowing for further analysis and optimization of lock performance.

Acks / Tags Given (0)
No activity

Gregory Price

Show 9 individual patches
  • [1/9] mm/memory: add memory_block_aligned_range() helper Neutral Submitted 2026-06-05 David Hildenbrand (Arm)
  • [2/9] mm/memory_hotplug: pass online_type to online_memory_block() via arg Submitted 2026-06-05
  • [3/9] mm/memory_hotplug: export mhp_get_default_online_type Neutral Submitted 2026-06-05 David Hildenbrand (Arm)
  • [4/9] mm/memory_hotplug: add __add_memory_driver_managed() with online_type arg Neutral Submitted 2026-06-05Last comment 2026-06-09 David (Arm)
  • [5/9] mm/memory_hotplug: add multi-range hotunplug Neutral Submitted 2026-06-05Last comment 2026-06-09 David (Arm) Gregory Price (author)
  • [6/9] dax: plumb hotplug online_type through dax Submitted 2026-06-05
  • [7/9] dax/kmem: extract hotplug/hotremove helper functions Submitted 2026-06-05
  • [8/9] dax/kmem: add sysfs interface for atomic hotplug Neutral Submitted 2026-06-05Last comment 2026-06-09 David (Arm) Gregory Price (author)
  • [9/9] selftests/dax: add dax/kmem hotplug sysfs regression test Submitted 2026-06-05
Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch series adds atomic whole-device hotplug control via sysfs, allowing for transitions to be atomic across entire device ranges. The patch addresses a race condition between offlining and removing memory blocks, which was discussed in the LPC2025 device memory sessions. It introduces a 'hotplug' attribute and updates mm and dax plumbing to support it. The state names mirror the per-block memoryX/state ABI with modifications for atomic whole-device control. The patch also includes mm preparation changes, such as adding helpers for online type and offline+remove of multiple ranges under a single lock.

to disallow MMOP_UNPLUGGED as a valid input (otherwise you could
permanently unplug memory without the ability to get it back... unless

[RFC PATCH 0/3] mm/numa: reserve standby NUMA nodes for runtime claiming 3 patches
Show 3 individual patches
  • [1/3] mm/numa: add exclusive node pool and numa=standby boot parameter
  • [2/3] acpi/numa: add CONFIG_ACPI_NUMA_STANDBY_NODES
  • [3/3] acpi/numa: add CONFIG_ACPI_NUMA_ADD_CFMWS_NODES
Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies

This patch series introduces the ability to reserve empty 'standby' NUMA nodes at boot, allowing drivers to place hotplugged memory on distinct nodes later at runtime without relying on BIOS-provided topology. The reserved nodes are initially offline but can be brought online by a driver and released when done. Three methods are provided to reserve standby nodes: a boot parameter, two kernel configuration options, and the ability to reserve nodes per CXL Fixed Memory Window. The patch series includes testing under various scenarios, including QEMU emulation and fallback cases.

Last comment 2026-06-09 [PATCH] mm: constify oom_control, scan_control, and alloc_context nodemask Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch makes the nodemasks in several Linux kernel structures const, ensuring they are not modified when passed around between contexts. The affected structures include oom_control, scan_control, and alloc_context. This change is motivated by the fact that these nodemasks can come from various sources, including tasks and cpusets, and should be treated as immutable. The patch modifies 11 files across several kernel directories to make this change.

Reviews Given (33)
Last comment 2026-06-11 Re: [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC][RFC PATCH v4 00/27] Private Memory Nodes (w/ Compressed RAM) Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch series introduces Private Memory Nodes, which allows for isolation of memory allocation on a per-node basis. The goal is to enable features like compressed RAM and improve system resource management. However, the patch also raises questions about how to limit page allocations to folio allocations or disallow slab allocations on private nodes. It appears that implementing such restrictions would be complex and may not provide significant benefits.

6 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
David (Arm) ↳ replying to Gregory Price Neutral LLM
Reviewer questioned whether focusing solely on folio allocations to private memory nodes could make the __GFP_THISNODE approach work, suggesting disallowing non-folio paths and allowing only folio allocation with __GFP_THISNODE set.
Show original comment 
Sorry, I haven't been able to follow up so far, and not sure if that's what you
are discussing here ...

After the LSF/MM session, I was wondering, whether if we focus on allowing only
folios allocations to end up on private memory nodes for now: could the
__GFP_THISNODE approach work there?

Essentially, disallow any allocations on non-folio paths, and allow folio
allocation only with __GFP_THISNODE set.

I have to find time to read the other mails in this thread, on my todo list.

-- 
Cheers,

David
View on lore ↗
Signals: question, suggestion
Gregory Price (author) ↳ replying to David (Arm) Needs Work LLM
Author Gregory Price is addressing David's (Arm) feedback on the patch, specifically concerns about locking and isolation in private memory nodes. He acknowledges that he didn't fully understand the request initially but provides a detailed explanation of how __GFP_THISNODE works under the hood, including its interaction with slab allocation and page allocator flags. He concludes that without explicit filtering, the mechanism is fragile and proposes two possible solutions for mm/slub.c to either refuse slab allocations on private nodes or not upgrade private-node slab requests with __GFP_THISNODE.
Show original comment 
So, I remember this being asked, and I didn't fully grok the request.

I'm still not sure I fully understand the question, so apologies if I'm
answer the wrong things here.

I understand this question in two ways:

  1) Can we disallow PAGE allocation and limit this to FOLIO allocation
  2) Can we disallow [Feature] (i.e. slab) allocation targeting the node.


1) Can we disallow page allocation and limit this to folios?

No, I don't think so.

Folio allocations are written in terms of page allocations, we would
have to rewrite folio allocation interfaces and introduce a bunch of
boilerplate for the sake of this.

struct page *__alloc_pages_noprof(gfp_t gfp, unsigned int order,
                int preferred_nid, nodemask_t *nodemask)
{
        struct page *page;

        page = __alloc_frozen_pages_noprof(gfp, order, preferred_nid, nodemask);
        if (page)
                set_page_refcounted(page);
        return page;
}

struct folio *__folio_alloc_noprof(gfp_t gfp, unsigned int order, int preferred_nid,
                nodemask_t *nodemask)
{
        struct page *page = __alloc_pages_noprof(gfp | __GFP_COMP, order,
                                        preferred_nid, nodemask);
	return page_rmappable_folio(page);
}

At the end of the day, this all reduces to `get_pages_from_freelist`,
and at that level we don't really care about folio vs page.

__GFP_COMP is insufficient to differentiate between a non-folio compound
page and a folio, and __GFP_COMP is passed into __alloc_pages_*
interfaces all over the kernel.

Trying to detach these paths things seems like a horrible rats nest /
not feasible / will create a lot of boilerplate for little value.

(I did not fully understand this request when it was asked, I do
 not fully understand this request not, please let me know if I
 have misunderstood what you were asking).



2) Can we disallow SLAB allocation.

Yeah, but I think a better question is whether there's a difference
between alloc_pages_node() and kmalloc_node() when it all just sinks
to the same fundamental code in mm/page_alloc.c

Maybe there's an argument for something like NP_OPT_KMALLOC (allow slab
allocations on the private node w/ __GFP_THISNODE)

On my current set, I don't implement any explicit filtering at all in
mm/page_alloc.c - the filtering is a function of the nodes not being
present in the FALLBACK list and only having a NOFALLBACK list.

What __GFP_THISNODE actually does under the hood is just switch
which zone list (FALLBACK vs NOFALLBACK) is used for the target node.

For isolation w/o __GFP_PRIVATE, we're removing N_MEMORY_PRIVATE nodes
from *their own FALLBACK* list and only adding them to their NOFALLBACK
list.  That means to reach a private node you MUST use __GFP_THISNODE.

I realize this is confusing, but essentially we don't have to modify
mm/page_alloc.c to get the __GFP_THISNODE filtering, we get this from
the fallback/nofallback list construction.


Ok, so how does this flush out in practice - and why do I call this
filtering mechanism fragile?

consider kmalloc_node() and __slab_alloc():

kmalloc_node(...)
  \u2514\u2500 ___slab_alloc()     mm/slub.c:4406   pc.flags |= __GFP_THISNODE
      \u2514\u2500 new_slab(s, pc.flags, node)
          \u2514\u2500 allocate_slab(s, flags, node)
              \u2514\u2500 alloc_slab_page(flags, node, oo, \u2026)
                  \u2514\u2500 __alloc_frozen_pages(flags, order, node, NULL);

Slab silently upgrades the page allocator flags here to include
__GFP_THISNODE - even if the user didn't request that behavior.

This is exactly the kind of "spillage" I said was hard to police at LSF.

Without __GFP_PRIVATE, we have to keep an eye on what around the kernel
is using __GFP_THISNODE and how.

For mm/slub.c we can choose to do one of thwo things

  1) 100% refuse slab allocations on private nodes, i.e.:

     kmalloc_node(..., private_nid, __GFP_THISNODE)

     And will fail (return NULL).

  or

  2) Do not upgrade private-node slab requests w/ __GFP_THISNODE
     
     This allows kmalloc_node() to work the same as folio_alloc()
     or alloc_pages() interfaces (__GFP_THISNODE is the key), with
     the understanding that any __GFP_THISNODE user

We can opt these nodes into slab/kmalloc with a NP_OPT_SLAB
if the owner wants kmalloc_node(), with the understanding that any
caller using __GFP_THISNODE may get access.

That's the kind of fragility I was trying to avoid.


That said, in practice, I have found that basic kernel operations don't
generally target use kmalloc_node() w/ __GFP_THISNODE - there's just
nothing to prevent anyone from doing so.

So this seems promising...
And then theres arch/powerpc/platforms/powernv/memtrace.c

static u64 memtrace_alloc_node(u32 nid, u64 size)
{
	... snip ...
        page = alloc_contig_pages(nr_pages, GFP_KERNEL | __GFP_THISNODE |
                                  __GFP_NOWARN | __GFP_ZERO, nid, NULL);
	... snip ...
}

static int memtrace_init_regions_runtime(u64 size)
{
	... snip ...
        for_each_online_node(nid) {
                m = memtrace_alloc_node(nid, size);
	... snip ...
}

static int memtrace_enable_set(void *data, u64 val)
{
	... snip ...
        if (memtrace_init_regions_runtime(val))
                goto out_unlock;
	... snip ...
}

This is the *exact* pattern I said would be hard to police - and it
doesn't look like a bug, just not informed that private nodes exist.

This is why I'm concerned with trying to depend on __GFP_THISNODE as the
filtering function.

That said, the number of __GFP_THISNODE users is very limited
kernel-wide, so maybe that's an acceptable maintenance burden?

~Gregory
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledges a fix is needed, proposes revisions
David (Arm) ↳ replying to Gregory Price Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer David requested allowing only folio allocations from private memory nodes, citing that page allocations are written in terms of page allocations and rewriting interfaces would introduce boilerplate code. Reviewer David expressed concerns about the feasibility of disallowing page allocation and limiting it to folios, citing that folio allocations are written in terms of page allocations and would require significant boilerplate changes. He also suggested considering alternative approaches such as providing more context to the allocator or using the memalloc_* interface. Reviewer David (Arm) noted that the current implementation of private memory nodes does not differentiate between folio and page allocations, as both ultimately call __get_pages_from_freelist(), which does not care about the distinction. He suggested adding a flag to allow slab allocations on private nodes with __GFP_THISNODE.
Show original comment 
Yes. Can we only allow folios to be allocated from private memory nodes. So let
me reply to that one below.

---

At LSF/MM we talked about how GFP flags are bad and how deriving stuff from the
context might be better. I think there was also talk about how the memalloc_*
interface might be a better way forward. Maybe we would start giving the
allocator more context ("we are allocating a folio").

The following is incomplete (esp. hugetlb stuff I assume), just as some idea:

---

From: "David Hildenbrand (Arm)" <david@kernel.org>
Date: Wed, 10 Jun 2026 20:55:49 +0200
Subject: [PATCH] tmp

Signed-off-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
---
 include/linux/sched.h    |  2 +-
 include/linux/sched/mm.h | 11 +++++++++++
 mm/mempolicy.c           | 14 ++++++++++++--
 mm/page_alloc.c          |  7 ++++++-
 4 files changed, 30 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)

diff --git a/include/linux/sched.h b/include/linux/sched.h
index ee06cba5c6f5..9c850b7be6bf 100644
--- a/include/linux/sched.h
+++ b/include/linux/sched.h
@@ -1778,7 +1778,7 @@ extern struct pid *cad_pid;
 						 * I am cleaning dirty pages from some other bdi. */
 #define PF_KTHREAD		0x00200000	/* I am a kernel thread */
 #define PF_RANDOMIZE		0x00400000	/* Randomize virtual address space */
-#define PF__HOLE__00800000	0x00800000
+#define PF__MEMALLOC_FOLIO	0x00800000	/* Allocating a folio that can end up on
private memory nodes */
 #define PF__HOLE__01000000	0x01000000
 #define PF__HOLE__02000000	0x02000000
 #define PF_NO_SETAFFINITY	0x04000000	/* Userland is not allowed to meddle with
cpus_mask */
diff --git a/include/linux/sched/mm.h b/include/linux/sched/mm.h
index 95d0040df584..2101a447c084 100644
--- a/include/linux/sched/mm.h
+++ b/include/linux/sched/mm.h
@@ -471,6 +471,17 @@ static inline void memalloc_pin_restore(unsigned int flags)
 	memalloc_flags_restore(flags);
 }

+static inline unsigned int memalloc_folio_save(void)
+{
+	return memalloc_flags_save(PF_MEMALLOC_FOLIO);
+}
+
+static inline void memalloc_folio_restore(unsigned int flags)
+{
+	memalloc_flags_restore(flags);
+}
+
+
 #ifdef CONFIG_MEMCG
 DECLARE_PER_CPU(struct mem_cgroup *, int_active_memcg);
 /**
diff --git a/mm/mempolicy.c b/mm/mempolicy.c
index 36699fabd3c2..a78b0e5a1fce 100644
--- a/mm/mempolicy.c
+++ b/mm/mempolicy.c
@@ -2506,8 +2506,13 @@ static struct page *alloc_pages_mpol(gfp_t gfp, unsigned
int order,
 struct folio *folio_alloc_mpol_noprof(gfp_t gfp, unsigned int order,
 		struct mempolicy *pol, pgoff_t ilx, int nid)
 {
-	struct page *page = alloc_pages_mpol(gfp | __GFP_COMP, order, pol,
+	struct page *page;
+	int flags;
+
+	flags = memalloc_folio_save();
+	page = alloc_pages_mpol(gfp | __GFP_COMP, order, pol,
 			ilx, nid);
+	memalloc_folio_restore(flags);
 	if (!page)
 		return NULL;

@@ -2588,7 +2593,12 @@ EXPORT_SYMBOL(alloc_pages_noprof);

 struct folio *folio_alloc_noprof(gfp_t gfp, unsigned int order)
 {
-	return page_rmappable_folio(alloc_pages_noprof(gfp | __GFP_COMP, order));
+	struct folio *folio;
+	int flags;
+
+	flags = memalloc_folio_save();
+	folio = page_rmappable_folio(alloc_pages_noprof(gfp | __GFP_COMP, order));
+	memalloc_folio_restore(flags);
+	return folio;
 }
 EXPORT_SYMBOL(folio_alloc_noprof);

diff --git a/mm/page_alloc.c b/mm/page_alloc.c
index ee902a468c2f..37434b37f7af 100644
--- a/mm/page_alloc.c
+++ b/mm/page_alloc.c
@@ -5345,8 +5345,13 @@ EXPORT_SYMBOL(__alloc_pages_noprof);
 struct folio *__folio_alloc_noprof(gfp_t gfp, unsigned int order, int
preferred_nid,
 		nodemask_t *nodemask)
 {
-	struct page *page = __alloc_pages_noprof(gfp | __GFP_COMP, order,
+	struct page *page;
+	int flags;
+
+	flags = memalloc_folio_save();
+	page = __alloc_pages_noprof(gfp | __GFP_COMP, order,
 					preferred_nid, nodemask);
+	memalloc_folio_restore(flags);
 	return page_rmappable_folio(page);
 }
 EXPORT_SYMBOL(__folio_alloc_noprof);
-- 
2.43.0


-- 
Cheers,

David
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes, alternative approaches
Gregory Price (author) ↳ replying to David (Arm) Needs Work LLM
Author acknowledges that implementing a separate allocation path for private memory nodes would be too invasive and instead proposes using the existing __GFP_THISNODE flag to allow slab allocations on the private node, while also providing a new userland driver (anondax) to test the functionality without specialty hardware.
Show original comment 
Ok, the mental gap I have is not knowing the full context behind
memalloc.  I'll take this and do some reading / prototyping, but
this looks entirely reasonable.

I will still probably send the next RFC version tomorrow or friday,
as I want to get some eyes on the __GFP_PRIVATE-less pattern.

Also, I made a new `anondax` driver which enables userland testing
of this functionality without any specialty hardware.

tl;dr:

fd = open("/dev/anondax0.0", ....);
buf = mmap(fd, ...);
buf[0] = 0xDEADBEEF; /* fault to anondax driver */

static vm_fault_t anon_dax_fault(struct vm_fault *vmf)
{
        struct dev_dax *dev_dax = vmf->vma->vm_file->private_data;
        vm_fault_t ret;
        int id;

        id = dax_read_lock();
        if (!dax_alive(dev_dax->dax_dev))
                ret = VM_FAULT_SIGBUS;
        else
                ret = do_anonymous_page_node(vmf, dev_dax->target_node);
        dax_read_unlock(id);

        if (ret & VM_FAULT_OOM)
                return VM_FAULT_SIGBUS;
        return ret ? ret : VM_FAULT_NOPAGE;
}

With:
  qemu-system-x86_64 -m 5G \
    -object memory-backend-ram,id=m0,size=4G -numa node,nodeid=0,memdev=m0 \
    -object memory-backend-ram,id=m1,size=1G -numa node,nodeid=1,memdev=m1 \
    -append "... memmap=0x40000000!0x140000000"

Voila - buddy-managed private anonymous memory (1G region)

No need to reinvent page_alloc.c or fault handling :]

This can be used to hammer on reclaim/compaction/whatever support
without needing any particular hardware setup, and in fact it gives
some memory devices a path to support in userland while standards
get worked out.

do_anonymous_page_node is a bit of a bodge right now but I just haven't
fleshed it out yet.  The idea is - don't reinvent the fault path, just
provide the appropriate context to memory.c to do the right thing.

If this is acceptable, I imagine whatever interface gets implemented
will carry an in-tree driver export only, similar to hotplug/kmem.
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledges need for further work, proposes alternative solution
Gregory Price (author) ↳ replying to David (Arm) Needs Work LLM
The author addresses David's concern about the inability to prevent hugetlb from spilling into private nodes, explaining that MEMALLOC_PRIVATE is still needed for this purpose and can be repurposed for Brendan's needs as well.
Show original comment 
Ok, this was easier to test than I expected, and hugetlb is indeed a
stickler.  We can't get there 100% with just MEMALLOC_FOLIO, we still
need a MEMALLOC_PRIVATE - specifically because of users like hugetlb.

hugetlb uses __GFP_THISNODE to do its allocations, and all hugetlb
allocations are folio allocations - so the code you shared by itself
does not gate hugetlb from spilling into private nodes.

That means we still need something like this in hugetlb:

  if (node_is_private(nid))
      /* fail allocation */

HOWEVER... if you have MEMALLOC_PRIVATE - you make the allocation
failure a *page allocator* problem, and it serves exactly the same
purpose that __GFP_PRIVATE did.

the resulting code is two lines in my anondax driver:

    unsigned int priv_flags = memalloc_private_save();
    ret = do_anonymous_page_node(vmf, dev_dax->target_node);
    memalloc_private_restore(priv_flags);

No special hugetlb, slab, arch code handling - they all just fail
to allocate / fall back.  If they fail - it means that code is using
a bad nodemask and we need to go fix it (exactly what we want!)

I think additionally, we might be able to repurpose MEMALLOC_PRIVATE
flag for Brendan's needs as well [1].

Their goal (IIRC) was to have a pile of unmapped blocks that could
be opportunistically converted to normal memory, but otherwise left
unmapped and sitting in the buddy.

Same thing - different filter point (blocks vs nodes).

If you set MEMALLOC_PRIVATE - it makes private node allocations
possible, and "private block" access (without conversion) possible.

Otherwise private nodes are unreachable, and private blocks would be
treated like CMA (last-resort stealing, lazy-direct-mapping).

And they stack (private blocks on private nodes :V).

I don't have enough time looking at his proposal, but it seems like we
can kill two birds with one stone on this.

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/agYJcRgOHho8upVv@gourry-fedora-PF4VCD3F/

~Gregory
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledges a fix is needed
Balbir Singh ↳ replying to Gregory Price Reviewed-by Neutral LLM
Reviewer noted that slub.c injects __GFP_THISNODE internally on behalf of kmalloc, causing spillage into private nodes because slub allows private nodes in its mask, and suggested this is fixable.
Show original comment 
That's good progress, thanks for the update!

Balbir
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledgment, positive tone
Last comment 2026-06-10 Re: [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC][RFC PATCH v4 00/27] Private Memory Nodes (w/ Compressed RAM) Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch series introduces Private Memory Nodes, which allows for isolation of memory allocation on a per-node basis. The goal is to enable features like compressed RAM and improve system resource management. However, the patch also raises questions about how to limit page allocations to folio allocations or disallow slab allocations on private nodes. It appears that implementing such restrictions would be complex and may not provide significant benefits.

3 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
David (Arm) ↳ replying to Gregory Price Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer David requested allowing only folio allocations from private memory nodes, citing that page allocations are written in terms of page allocations and rewriting interfaces would introduce boilerplate code. Reviewer David expressed concerns about the feasibility of disallowing page allocation and limiting it to folios, citing that folio allocations are written in terms of page allocations and would require significant boilerplate changes. He also suggested considering alternative approaches such as providing more context to the allocator or using the memalloc_* interface. Reviewer David (Arm) noted that the current implementation of private memory nodes does not differentiate between folio and page allocations, as both ultimately call __get_pages_from_freelist(), which does not care about the distinction. He suggested adding a flag to allow slab allocations on private nodes with __GFP_THISNODE.
Show original comment 
Yes. Can we only allow folios to be allocated from private memory nodes. So let
me reply to that one below.

---

At LSF/MM we talked about how GFP flags are bad and how deriving stuff from the
context might be better. I think there was also talk about how the memalloc_*
interface might be a better way forward. Maybe we would start giving the
allocator more context ("we are allocating a folio").

The following is incomplete (esp. hugetlb stuff I assume), just as some idea:

---

From: "David Hildenbrand (Arm)" <david@kernel.org>
Date: Wed, 10 Jun 2026 20:55:49 +0200
Subject: [PATCH] tmp

Signed-off-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
---
 include/linux/sched.h    |  2 +-
 include/linux/sched/mm.h | 11 +++++++++++
 mm/mempolicy.c           | 14 ++++++++++++--
 mm/page_alloc.c          |  7 ++++++-
 4 files changed, 30 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)

diff --git a/include/linux/sched.h b/include/linux/sched.h
index ee06cba5c6f5..9c850b7be6bf 100644
--- a/include/linux/sched.h
+++ b/include/linux/sched.h
@@ -1778,7 +1778,7 @@ extern struct pid *cad_pid;
 						 * I am cleaning dirty pages from some other bdi. */
 #define PF_KTHREAD		0x00200000	/* I am a kernel thread */
 #define PF_RANDOMIZE		0x00400000	/* Randomize virtual address space */
-#define PF__HOLE__00800000	0x00800000
+#define PF__MEMALLOC_FOLIO	0x00800000	/* Allocating a folio that can end up on
private memory nodes */
 #define PF__HOLE__01000000	0x01000000
 #define PF__HOLE__02000000	0x02000000
 #define PF_NO_SETAFFINITY	0x04000000	/* Userland is not allowed to meddle with
cpus_mask */
diff --git a/include/linux/sched/mm.h b/include/linux/sched/mm.h
index 95d0040df584..2101a447c084 100644
--- a/include/linux/sched/mm.h
+++ b/include/linux/sched/mm.h
@@ -471,6 +471,17 @@ static inline void memalloc_pin_restore(unsigned int flags)
 	memalloc_flags_restore(flags);
 }

+static inline unsigned int memalloc_folio_save(void)
+{
+	return memalloc_flags_save(PF_MEMALLOC_FOLIO);
+}
+
+static inline void memalloc_folio_restore(unsigned int flags)
+{
+	memalloc_flags_restore(flags);
+}
+
+
 #ifdef CONFIG_MEMCG
 DECLARE_PER_CPU(struct mem_cgroup *, int_active_memcg);
 /**
diff --git a/mm/mempolicy.c b/mm/mempolicy.c
index 36699fabd3c2..a78b0e5a1fce 100644
--- a/mm/mempolicy.c
+++ b/mm/mempolicy.c
@@ -2506,8 +2506,13 @@ static struct page *alloc_pages_mpol(gfp_t gfp, unsigned
int order,
 struct folio *folio_alloc_mpol_noprof(gfp_t gfp, unsigned int order,
 		struct mempolicy *pol, pgoff_t ilx, int nid)
 {
-	struct page *page = alloc_pages_mpol(gfp | __GFP_COMP, order, pol,
+	struct page *page;
+	int flags;
+
+	flags = memalloc_folio_save();
+	page = alloc_pages_mpol(gfp | __GFP_COMP, order, pol,
 			ilx, nid);
+	memalloc_folio_restore(flags);
 	if (!page)
 		return NULL;

@@ -2588,7 +2593,12 @@ EXPORT_SYMBOL(alloc_pages_noprof);

 struct folio *folio_alloc_noprof(gfp_t gfp, unsigned int order)
 {
-	return page_rmappable_folio(alloc_pages_noprof(gfp | __GFP_COMP, order));
+	struct folio *folio;
+	int flags;
+
+	flags = memalloc_folio_save();
+	folio = page_rmappable_folio(alloc_pages_noprof(gfp | __GFP_COMP, order));
+	memalloc_folio_restore(flags);
+	return folio;
 }
 EXPORT_SYMBOL(folio_alloc_noprof);

diff --git a/mm/page_alloc.c b/mm/page_alloc.c
index ee902a468c2f..37434b37f7af 100644
--- a/mm/page_alloc.c
+++ b/mm/page_alloc.c
@@ -5345,8 +5345,13 @@ EXPORT_SYMBOL(__alloc_pages_noprof);
 struct folio *__folio_alloc_noprof(gfp_t gfp, unsigned int order, int
preferred_nid,
 		nodemask_t *nodemask)
 {
-	struct page *page = __alloc_pages_noprof(gfp | __GFP_COMP, order,
+	struct page *page;
+	int flags;
+
+	flags = memalloc_folio_save();
+	page = __alloc_pages_noprof(gfp | __GFP_COMP, order,
 					preferred_nid, nodemask);
+	memalloc_folio_restore(flags);
 	return page_rmappable_folio(page);
 }
 EXPORT_SYMBOL(__folio_alloc_noprof);
-- 
2.43.0


-- 
Cheers,

David

Author acknowledges that implementing a separate allocation path for private memory nodes would be too invasive and instead proposes using the existing __GFP_THISNODE flag to allow slab allocations on the private node, while also providing a new userland driver (anondax) to test the functionality without specialty hardware.
Show original comment 
Ok, the mental gap I have is not knowing the full context behind
memalloc.  I'll take this and do some reading / prototyping, but
this looks entirely reasonable.

I will still probably send the next RFC version tomorrow or friday,
as I want to get some eyes on the __GFP_PRIVATE-less pattern.

Also, I made a new `anondax` driver which enables userland testing
of this functionality without any specialty hardware.

tl;dr:

fd = open("/dev/anondax0.0", ....);
buf = mmap(fd, ...);
buf[0] = 0xDEADBEEF; /* fault to anondax driver */

static vm_fault_t anon_dax_fault(struct vm_fault *vmf)
{
        struct dev_dax *dev_dax = vmf->vma->vm_file->private_data;
        vm_fault_t ret;
        int id;

        id = dax_read_lock();
        if (!dax_alive(dev_dax->dax_dev))
                ret = VM_FAULT_SIGBUS;
        else
                ret = do_anonymous_page_node(vmf, dev_dax->target_node);
        dax_read_unlock(id);

        if (ret & VM_FAULT_OOM)
                return VM_FAULT_SIGBUS;
        return ret ? ret : VM_FAULT_NOPAGE;
}

With:
  qemu-system-x86_64 -m 5G \
    -object memory-backend-ram,id=m0,size=4G -numa node,nodeid=0,memdev=m0 \
    -object memory-backend-ram,id=m1,size=1G -numa node,nodeid=1,memdev=m1 \
    -append "... memmap=0x40000000!0x140000000"

Voila - buddy-managed private anonymous memory (1G region)

No need to reinvent page_alloc.c or fault handling :]

This can be used to hammer on reclaim/compaction/whatever support
without needing any particular hardware setup, and in fact it gives
some memory devices a path to support in userland while standards
get worked out.

do_anonymous_page_node is a bit of a bodge right now but I just haven't
fleshed it out yet.  The idea is - don't reinvent the fault path, just
provide the appropriate context to memory.c to do the right thing.

If this is acceptable, I imagine whatever interface gets implemented
will carry an in-tree driver export only, similar to hotplug/kmem.
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledges need for further work, proposes alternative solution
Gregory Price (author) ↳ replying to David (Arm) Needs Work LLM
The author addresses David's concern about the inability to prevent hugetlb from spilling into private nodes, explaining that MEMALLOC_PRIVATE is still needed for this purpose and can be repurposed for Brendan's needs as well.
Show original comment 
Ok, this was easier to test than I expected, and hugetlb is indeed a
stickler.  We can't get there 100% with just MEMALLOC_FOLIO, we still
need a MEMALLOC_PRIVATE - specifically because of users like hugetlb.

hugetlb uses __GFP_THISNODE to do its allocations, and all hugetlb
allocations are folio allocations - so the code you shared by itself
does not gate hugetlb from spilling into private nodes.

That means we still need something like this in hugetlb:

  if (node_is_private(nid))
      /* fail allocation */

HOWEVER... if you have MEMALLOC_PRIVATE - you make the allocation
failure a *page allocator* problem, and it serves exactly the same
purpose that __GFP_PRIVATE did.

the resulting code is two lines in my anondax driver:

    unsigned int priv_flags = memalloc_private_save();
    ret = do_anonymous_page_node(vmf, dev_dax->target_node);
    memalloc_private_restore(priv_flags);

No special hugetlb, slab, arch code handling - they all just fail
to allocate / fall back.  If they fail - it means that code is using
a bad nodemask and we need to go fix it (exactly what we want!)

I think additionally, we might be able to repurpose MEMALLOC_PRIVATE
flag for Brendan's needs as well [1].

Their goal (IIRC) was to have a pile of unmapped blocks that could
be opportunistically converted to normal memory, but otherwise left
unmapped and sitting in the buddy.

Same thing - different filter point (blocks vs nodes).

If you set MEMALLOC_PRIVATE - it makes private node allocations
possible, and "private block" access (without conversion) possible.

Otherwise private nodes are unreachable, and private blocks would be
treated like CMA (last-resort stealing, lazy-direct-mapping).

And they stack (private blocks on private nodes :V).

I don't have enough time looking at his proposal, but it seems like we
can kill two birds with one stone on this.

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/agYJcRgOHho8upVv@gourry-fedora-PF4VCD3F/

~Gregory

3 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Gregory Price ↳ replying to David Hildenbrand (Arm) Needs Work LLM
Raised concerns about adding MMOP_UNPLUGGED as a valid state, suggesting it could lead to permanent unplug without recovery. Proposed special-casing the value instead.
Show original comment 
On Tue, Jun 09, 2026 at 08:11:42PM +0200, David Hildenbrand (Arm) wrote:
> On 6/9/26 17:35, Gregory Price wrote:
> > On Tue, Jun 09, 2026 at 12:26:07PM +0200, David Hildenbrand (Arm) wrote:
> >> On 6/5/26 23:19, Gregory Price wrote:
> >>
> >>>  
> >>> +static int dax_kmem_parse_state(const char *buf)
> >>> +{
> >>> +	if (sysfs_streq(buf, "unplugged"))
> >>> +		return DAX_KMEM_UNPLUGGED;
> >>> +	if (sysfs_streq(buf, "online"))
> >>> +		return MMOP_ONLINE;
> >>> +	if (sysfs_streq(buf, "online_kernel"))
> >>> +		return MMOP_ONLINE_KERNEL;
> >>> +	if (sysfs_streq(buf, "online_movable"))
> >>> +		return MMOP_ONLINE_MOVABLE;
> >>> +	return -EINVAL;
> >>
> >> Should we try making use of mhp_online_type_from_str()/online_type_to_str()
> >> [possibly a nicer exported function for the latter] to avoid duplicating this ...
> >>
> > 
> > In patch 6 response i point out adding MMOP_UNPLUGGED
> > 
> > If we add MMOP_UNPLUGGED as a state that is only use by callers of
> > memory hotplug to represent the current state - but not as a valid input
> > to memory_hotplug.c - then we can simply this as you request.
> > 
> > Although we'll need to add a couple lines to memoryN/state parsing code
> > to disallow MMOP_UNPLUGGED as a valid input (otherwise you could
> > permanently unplug memory without the ability to get it back... unless
> > you want that?  Seems useless to me.)
> 
> I would just special-case your KMEM-special value, and then branch off to the
> core helpers?
> 
> static int dax_kmem_parse_state(const char *buf)
> {
> 	if (sysfs_streq(buf, "unplugged"))
> 		return DAX_KMEM_UNPLUGGED;
> 	return mhp_online_type_from_str(...);
> }
> 
> So there is no need to add a value to MM core?
> 

Was going to propose this, but then I thought... well if CXL detactes
itself from dax and does this same pattern, maybe MMOP_UNPLUGGED makes
sense.

But i'll leave that for another day and do as you suggest.

Thanks!

~Gregory
Signals: NEEDS_WORK
David Hildenbrand (Arm) Positive LLM
Proposed a simpler solution by special-casing the KMEM-special value in dax_kmem_parse_state().
Signals: POSITIVE
Gregory Price ↳ replying to David Hildenbrand (Arm) Neutral LLM
Acknowledged David's proposal and decided to implement it, but also mentioned that MMOP_UNPLUGGED might be useful in other contexts.

The patch aims to use folio_zero_user for user pages in post_alloc_hook, but the discussion is stalled due to disagreements on API design and consensus building.

On Mon, Jun 08, 2026 at 05:46:27PM -0400, Michael S. Tsirkin wrote:
> On Mon, Jun 08, 2026 at 05:33:50PM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:

> Well it's in october, is it not? I don't think I have the patience to
> keep fiddling with that for half a year.
> 

You might be able to find a way forward that doesn't take that long, but
that starts with trying to build consensus on what to build before you
build it.

You're proposing a non-trivial change to the page allocator API, I would
not expect this to move at the speed of claude.

~Gregory

Last comment 2026-06-09 Re: [PATCH splitout] mm: page_reporting: allow driver to set batch capacity Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch addresses an issue where the page reporting capacity is not properly handled when a driver sets it to zero. The code currently forces the capacity up to 32 in such cases, but this change makes it more explicit and obvious that setting capacity to zero will result in PAGE_REPORTING_CAPACITY being used instead. This improves clarity and prevents potential bugs or unexpected behavior.

2 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Michael Tsirkin ↳ replying to Gregory Price Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
reviewer questioned the lack of explicit handling for capacity=0, pointing out that the code intends to default to PAGE_REPORTING_CAPACITY but this is not reflected in the changelog and could lead to unexpected behavior if a driver sets capacity to 0 Reviewer noted that the patch does not explicitly check for a capacity of 0, which could lead to unexpected behavior if a driver sets it to 0 either intentionally or due to a bug, and requested additional documentation in the changelog and code
Show original comment 
exactly ??? since where are we practicing defensive programming in kernel
APIs?

---

So I'll update the commit log to mention PAGE_REPORTING_CAPACITY.
And maybe a comment near capacity field?
Should be enough?

-- 
MST
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes, additional documentation
Gregory Price (author) ↳ replying to Michael Tsirkin Needs Work LLM
Author acknowledges a potential issue with capacity=0 and suggests that either a WARN should be triggered or the default value should be used, which requires documentation.
Show original comment 
I suppose the question is whether capacity=0 should cause a WARN (i.e.
only a bug explains that value), or if capacity=0 means something
special (i.e. use the default) and therefore that should be documented.

I don't know which of these is the case, but if it's the latter than
that deserves a comment yes.

~Gregory

Last comment 2026-06-08 Re: [PATCH v10 07/37] mm: thread user_addr through page allocator for cache-friendly zeroing Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch is part of a larger series that aims to improve memory management in the Linux kernel. Specifically, it uses the folio_zero_user function for user pages in the post_alloc_hook. The goal is to make the code more efficient and easier to understand by breaking down complex changes into smaller, more manageable pieces. The patch set has become unwieldy due to its complexity and scope, so Gregory suggests splitting it up into smaller pull-ahead sets that can be reviewed separately. This patch is one of the core improvements that will eventually lead to a final feature in multiple kernel versions.

5 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Zi Yan ↳ replying to Gregory Price Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer Zi Yan suggested lifting __GFP_ZERO up in the allocation code so that most of it doesn't need to care about zeroing, and doing the zeroing right before the page is returned to callers. Reviewer noted that some callers of the page allocator attempt to zero huge pages differently than intended, causing double zeroing issues when init_on_alloc was introduced and mitigated by user_alloc_needs_zeroing().
Show original comment 
page_alloc.c calling folio_user_zero() is fine, but my question is
whether we should do the zeroing inside post_alloc_hook(), part of
allocation.

What I propose is to lift __GFP_ZERO up as much as possible,
so that most of allocation code does not need to care about it.
We do the zeroing right before the page is returned to callers.

---

Yeah, some callers think they know better about huge page zeroing and want to
do it differently. That caused double zeroing when init_on_alloc was
introduced and was mitigated by user_alloc_needs_zeroing().

Best Regards,
Yan, Zi
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes, double zeroing issue, caller behavior
Gregory Price (author) ↳ replying to Zi Yan Neutral LLM
Author acknowledges that having two interfaces for allocating frozen pages can be error-prone and proposes an alternative approach with separate functions for user-bound allocations, but notes it's not a deal breaker.
Show original comment 
essentially we end up with something like

alloc_frozen_...(..., gfp)
{
  folio = whatever(..., gfp);
  if (gfp & __GFP_ZERO)
    folio_zero(folio, -1); /* don't do cache flush part */
}

alloc_frozen_user_...(..., gfp, user_addr)
{
  folio = whatever(..., gfp);
  if (gfp & __GFP_ZERO)
    folio_zero(folio, user_addr); /* do cache flush part */
}

The downside of this is obvious: it's easy for developers to get this
wrong and call the non-user interface for user-bound allocations and
miss the cache flush (that is only needed on some archs).

Not saying that's a deal breaker, but it's something to chew on.

~Gregory
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledges potential issue, proposes alternative
Zi Yan ↳ replying to Gregory Price Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer noted that the churn approach does not prevent developers from passing user_addr = -1 to alloc_frozen(), which can lead to a page being exported to userspace without proper cache flush, and suggested using alloc_page() + vm_insert() as an example of this vulnerability.
Show original comment 
I agree that misuse can cause trouble. But if we do the churn approach,
what prevents developer from doing alloc_frozen(..., user_addr = -1)
and using the returned page for userspace? It is possible the allocated
page can be exported to userspace later.

BTW, that cache flush thing is fragile even today, you probably can
do alloc_page() + vm_insert() to get a page without doing proper flush
and export it to userspace. There seems to be no mechanism to
prevent that.

Best Regards,
Yan, Zi
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes
Gregory Price (author) ↳ replying to Zi Yan Neutral LLM
Author acknowledged that the increased complexity of having two APIs (alloc_frozen and alloc_frozen_user) carries maintenance burden, but did not indicate plans to simplify or restructure in this patch
Show original comment 
Oh of course, I said that elsewhere.  It leaves us in a spot where we're
not technically worse than we were yesterday - except that the surface
of the buddy has increased (developers need to know about 2 APIs instead
of 1).  That carries maintenance burden (if something in alloc_frozen()
changes, something in alloc_frozen_user() may need to change).

There's a careful dance here.

~Gregory

Reviewer noted that the patch may not work correctly on all architectures, specifically mentioning arm32 where missing the cache flush would result in a performance hit but still produce correct results
Show original comment 
Probably arch dependent. On arm32, I think if you miss the flush, then
PG_dcache_clean will be clear and then you get a perf hit but
it's still correct. Didn't check others.

This patch is part of a larger series that aims to improve memory management in the Linux kernel. Specifically, it uses the folio_zero_user function for user pages in the post_alloc_hook. The goal is to make the code more efficient and easier to understand by breaking down complex changes into smaller, more manageable pieces. Zi Yan ↳ replying to Michael Tsirkin Reviewed-by Neutral LLM

Show original comment

View on lore ↗
Signals: conflicting ideas, need for consensus
David (Arm) ↳ replying to Zi Yan Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer David (Arm) expressed strong disagreement with the patch, calling it 'Simple, and hacky -> unmergable', and suggested a different approach where zeroing is moved further out to callers. Reviewer David (Arm) expressed strong disagreement with the patch, arguing that the existing page/folio zeroing mechanism is a mess and should not be extended with special casing, citing concerns about double-zeroing and the existence of vma_alloc_zeroed_movable_folio() which can be overridden by architectures. Reviewer David (Arm) expressed frustration that the patch series has not received sufficient discussion or feedback, particularly regarding his initial pushback and Michael's question in RFC v3, which he believes would have been a better time to address concerns.
Show original comment 
Simple, and hacky -> unmergable. I tried to push it into a different (no GFP
flags -> IMHO better) direction, but the patch set grew in complexity.

I kept telling to keep it simple (e.g., no folio_put optimization, no hugetlb
optimization, simple wrapper functions), and ideally we would have gotten a
better discussion with other folks here much earlier.

And I still do not consider providing an user address to selected interfaces
while centralizing zeroing a bad idea. The real question is how that could be
done in a cleaner way.

Or as Willy said, if we could move zeroing further out to callers, where they
can special-case. But given that KASAN and friends interact in their own way
with zeroing doesn't make that super straight forward as people might think.

---

I mean, I would hope that we can agree that our existing page/folio zeroing is a
mess and should not be extended by slapping more special casing on top?

Sure, we can try cleaning it up, but conceptually, zeroing happening at two
places in the callchain, with random optimizations to avoid double-zeroing is
just bad.

The fact that a vma_alloc_zeroed_movable_folio() that can be overridden by
architectures even exists makes me angry. user_alloc_needs_zeroing() is jsut the
tip of the ugly iceberg.

---

There was Willy's comment in RFC v3 [1], which had 19 patches. Unfortunately, he
no longer followed up to my initial push back and Michael's question later [2].

That would have probably been the right time to wait for more discussion.

RFC v4 had 22 patches with little replies.
v5 had 28 patches with little replies.
v6 had 30 patches with no replies.
v7 had 31 patches with little replies.
v8 had 37 patches with no replies.

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/lkml/aeu5P1bZW3yEH54t@casper.infradead.org/
[2] https://lore.kernel.org/lkml/20260426165330-mutt-send-email-mst@kernel.org/

-- 
Cheers,

David
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes, disagreement, conceptual issues
Gregory Price (author) ↳ replying to David (Arm) Needs Work LLM
Author acknowledged that the folio_zero_user interface should be internal to mm/, agreed to move its logic into vma_alloc_folio, alloc_hugetlb_folio, and alloc_hugetlb_folio_reserve, but noted a catch related to user_alloc_needs_zeroing().
Show original comment 
Hm, rewinding on this back to v3 here:
https://lore.kernel.org/lkml/016cc5e5-044c-46c6-a668-200f90a64d85@kernel.org/

You said:

  ```
  Exactly, that's why I am saying that vma_alloc_folio() is the only
  external interface people should be using with a user address.
  ```

Going through the list of folio_zero_user references:

Called unconditionally if a folio is acquired:
   fs/hugetlbfs/inode.c:   folio_zero_user(folio, addr);
   mm/hugetlb.c:           folio_zero_user(folio, vmf->real_address);
   mm/memfd.c:             folio_zero_user(folio, 0);

Called when user_alloc_needs_zeroing() and charging passes:
   mm/huge_memory.c:       folio_zero_user(folio, addr);
   mm/memory.c:            folio_zero_user(folio, vmf->address);

No one outside mm/ should know about this interface at all.
Arguably none of these should know about this interface either.

The appropriate place for this logic appears to be:
    vma_alloc_folio
    alloc_hugetlb_folio
    alloc_hugetlb_folio_reserve

The reason to sink it into the post_alloc_hook is to let the buddy
decide whether the page actually needs to be zeroed (like the virtio
situation) based on PG_zeroed or whatever.

It seems like at a minimum moving the logic all the way into
post_alloc_hook lets us actually delete folio_zero_user() as a published
interface and move it entirely within page_alloc.c.

The catch is user_alloc_needs_zeroing() coming along with it.

~Gregory
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledged fix needed, agreed to restructure

This patch is part of a larger series that aims to improve memory management in the Linux kernel. Specifically, it uses the folio_zero_user function for user pages in the post_alloc_hook. The goal is to make the code more efficient and easier to understand by breaking down complex changes into smaller, more manageable pieces. The patch set has become unwieldy due to its complexity and scope, so Gregory suggests splitting it up into smaller pull-ahead sets that can be reviewed separately. This patch is one of the core improvements that will eventually lead to a final feature in multiple kernel versions.

Show original comment 
I agree that misuse can cause trouble. But if we do the churn approach,
what prevents developer from doing alloc_frozen(..., user_addr = -1)
and using the returned page for userspace? It is possible the allocated
page can be exported to userspace later.

BTW, that cache flush thing is fragile even today, you probably can
do alloc_page() + vm_insert() to get a page without doing proper flush
and export it to userspace. There seems to be no mechanism to
prevent that.

Best Regards,
Yan, Zi
