Joanne Koong (author) Neutral LLM
Author acknowledged that the zero-copy patch has a dependency on Jens's io-uring tree patches, which may not be mergeable in time for 7.2. She asked Miklos to decide how to sync between the two trees and suggested co-shipping the bufpool and registered buffer patches with the zero-copy patch.
Show original comment 
Miklos,

The zero-copy patch has a dependency on the bvec patches in Jens's
io-uring tree. Since the merge window opens Monday, it's probably too
tight now to cross-coordinate this for 7.2. What do you prefer for
syncing between the two trees? If Jens stages the patches in a stable
branch, are you fine with pulling that in and then applying the fuse
patches on top?

Patches 7 and 8 (bufpools and registered buffers) don't have this
dependency, but to make the uapi cleaner under one single umbrella
flag, it would be better to co-ship them with the zero-copy patch.

Patches 1-6 are refactoring changes that have been out since October
and have value on their own, independent of the bufpool and zero-copy
work. Could you please pick these up for 7.2 so they don't have to be
carried and repeatedly rebased?

Thanks,
Joanne

View on lore ↗
Signals: no clear signal
[PATCH v2] fuse: end fuse_req on io-uring cancel task work Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies

This patch fixes a bug in the io-uring implementation for FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) where a fuse request is not properly ended when an io-uring task is cancelled. When an io-uring task is cancelled, the fuse request remains linked to the processing queue and its reference count is never decremented, leading to resource leaks and potential crashes. The patch fixes this by releasing the entry directly from the cancel branch, removing it from the queue, completing the io-uring command, ending the fuse request, freeing the entry, and dropping its queue references.

[PATCH v3 0/3] fuse: fix io-uring abort races and cancel null deref 3 patches
Show 3 individual patches
  • [1/3] fuse: fix race between registration and connection abortion
  • [2/3] fuse: check connection abort during ring creation
  • [3/3] fuse: fix moving cancelled entry to ent_in_userspace list
Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies

This patch series fixes two issues in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation for io-uring: a race between abort and fuse ring creation/registration, and a null dereference when cancelling entries. The patches address these problems by reordering commit messages, adding reviewed-by tags, and making changes to the code that handle aborting and cancelling requests. Specifically, the patches ensure that request flushing always happens during abort, and remove a gate that prevented request flushing in certain cases.

Ongoing Submitted 2026-06-05 Last comment 2026-06-08 ⏱ No recent activity [PATCH 0/3] fuse: io-uring request completion and ordering fixes 3 patches
Show 3 individual patches
  • [1/3] fuse: fix EFAULT clobber in fuse_uring_commit Neutral Submitted 2026-06-05Last comment 2026-06-08 Bernd Schubert Miklos Szeredi
  • [2/3] fuse: fix data races on ring->ready Neutral Submitted 2026-06-05Last comment 2026-06-08 Miklos Szeredi
  • [3/3] fuse: end fuse_req on io-uring cancel task work Neutral Submitted 2026-06-05Last comment 2026-06-08 Bernd Schubert Joanne Koong (author)
Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch series fixes three bugs in the io-uring implementation for FUSE file systems. The issues include a potential EFAULT error, data races on the ready ring, and incorrect handling of cancel tasks. The patches were identified by Chris Mason's AI program and are marked for stable inclusion.

Bernd Schubert Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
reviewer noted that the code in fuse_uring_cancel() can race with fuse_uring_stop_queues(), presenting two possible solutions to avoid this issue: either duplicating the logic or sending the request through fuse_uring_entry_teardown() with refactoring
Show original comment 
I think that can race with fuse_uring_stop_queues(), which leaves us two
choices


a) Same logic as fuse_uring_cancel() 
https://lore.kernel.org/r/20260515045541.1171335-4-joannelkoong@gmail.com

(also introduces slight code dup)

with a bit refactoring. I thought I had send an updated patch version

Signals: race condition, code duplication
Joanne Koong (author) ↳ replying to Bernd Schubert Needs Work LLM
Author acknowledged that there is a potential race condition in the request expiration checking logic, agreed to restructure the code in v2 and planned to address it early next week.
Show original comment 
Hmm, I don't think this races with fuse_uring_stop_queues() as
ent->state here is still FRRS_FUSE_REQ and fuse_uring_send_in_task()
can only be called for a registered fuse ent, which means the ent has
already grabbed the queue refcount which will trigger the async
teardown worker to run in the background during abort until the ent is
reclaimed. I think this adds a race though with the request expiration
checking logic which (a) fixed, so I think you're right that we'll
probably need the same cleanup here. I'll look at this early next week
and send a v2.

Thanks for taking a look at this series.

Thanks,
Joanne
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledged a fix is needed, planned to address
Bernd Schubert ↳ replying to Joanne Koong Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
reviewer identified a use-after-free issue in the patch, specifically that an entry is set to FRRS_USERSPACE and then cleaned up during connection abort, only to be released again through fuse_uring_queues or fuse_uring_async_stop_queues reviewer noted that a mean exit does not trigger fuse_dev_release() with a fuse_abort_conn(), and questioned the necessity of patch 3
Show original comment 
Right, actually no race at all, just a plain use-after-free, because the
entry is set to FRRS_USERSPACE and then cleaned up during connection
abort and then released again through fuse_uring_queues or
fuse_uring_async_stop_queues.

I actually do not get this part of the commit message

---

A mean exit does not trigger fuse_dev_release() with a fuse_abort_conn()
from that function?


I do not think we need 3/3 at all.


Thanks,
Bernd
View on lore ↗
Signals: use-after-free issue, commit message unclear, requested change
Joanne Koong (author) ↳ replying to Bernd Schubert Contentious LLM
Author disagreed with the implication that io_uring task death or ring death is equivalent to fuse connection death, explaining that a single worker thread exiting does not drop any reference on /dev/fuse fd but its in-flight task work still drains with tw.cancel Author acknowledged that there are cases where tw.cancel occurs without a subsequent fuse abort, which can cause an application syscall thread to be stuck uninterruptibly in D-state; no explicit fix planned but agrees it's a necessary case
Show original comment 
I don't think tw.cancel implies a connection abort. tw.cancel gets set
on io_uring task death or ring death which is different from fuse
connection death (eg a single worker thread of a multithreaded daemon
exiting doesn't drop any ref on /dev/fuse fd, but that thread's
in-flight task work still drains with tw.cancel)

---

I think this is needed for the cases where tw.cancel occurs without a
subsequent fuse abort, else the application syscall thread is stuck
uninterruptibly in D-state in request_wait_answer() for the
connection's lifetime. tw.cancel with a fuse abort is the common case,
but I think unfortunately we also need to handle the case where this
doesn't occur.

Thanks,
Joanne
View on lore ↗

Show original comment 
I see, the initial code was using IO_URING_F_TASK_DEAD and I had wrongly 
assumed that is related to PF_EXITING. 
Well, I think the fix is clear, although I personally do not like the
exit code dup (or better triple)

In my option fuse_uring_cancel() and canceled fuse_uring_send_in_task() 
should go through fuse_uring_entry_teardown().

Bernd

Show original comment 
I don't feel strongly about this. On cancel, the ent becomes useless
since cancel consumes the cmd, so imo it makes sense to just free and
clean up the ent immediately instead of keeping it around until the
fuse connection gets eventually aborted, but I don't think it really
matters. Happy to go with what you prefer.

Thanks,
Joanne

[PATCH v1] fuse: remove request-less entries from ent_w_req_queue to fix NULL deref Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies

This patch fixes a null pointer dereference issue in the FUSE implementation by removing request-less entries from the ent_w_req_queue list when a copy operation fails, and changing their state to invalid before terminating the request.

Last comment 2026-06-12 [PATCH v7 0/4] io_uring: extend bvec registration 4 patches
Show 4 individual patches
  • [1/4] io_uring/rsrc: rename io_buffer_register_bvec()/io_buffer_unregister_bvec()
  • [2/4] io_uring/rsrc: split io_buffer_register_request() logic
  • [3/4] io_uring/rsrc: add io_buffer_register_bvec()
  • [4/4] io_uring/rsrc: rename and export IO_IMU_DEST / IO_IMU_SOURCE
Neutral LLM

This patch series refactors and extends the io_uring registered buffers infrastructure to allow external subsystems to register pre-existing bvec arrays directly, aiming to make fuse zero-copy possible.

Re: [PATCH v3 07/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools (implemented as buffer ring) Neutral LLM
Re: [PATCH v3 07/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools (implemented as buffer ring) Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies
Last comment 2026-06-11 Re: [PATCH v3 00/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch series adds support for io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy to the FUSE file system, allowing for more efficient handling of I/O operations by reusing buffers instead of constantly allocating new ones.

6 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Miklos Szeredi ↳ replying to Joanne Koong Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer questioned the need for buffer pools, suggesting that if server wants to remove individual buffers, it can register them as separate pools; he also noted that buffer pools introduce extra complexity and overhead
Show original comment 
If server knows that it will want to remove individual buffers, it
will just have to register them as separate pools (containing a single
buffer), right?

Buffer pools are a more generic concept, hence they come with some
extra complexity and overhead.   But the interface should be a
superset of what individual buffers provide.

Am I missing something?

Thanks,
Miklos
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes, questioned design choice
Joanne Koong (author) ↳ replying to Miklos Szeredi Neutral LLM
Author Joanne Koong addressed Miklos Szeredi's concern about the overhead of managing multiple buffer pools, explaining that internally the difference would be between X bufpools with a single buffer versus 1 bufpool with X single buffers. She acknowledged that there would be more overhead for X bufpools but didn't commit to revising the patch.
Show original comment 
The interface is a superset. Internally the difference would be X
bufpools with a single buffer vs 1 bufpool with X single buffers.
There would be more overhead for X bufpools (eg allocating X
fuse_bufpools structs, looking across more bufpools to find a free
buffer). I don't think it would add much additional complexity, given
that multi-bufpool support would also be needed if we wanted to
accommodate different buffer sizes in the same ring.

Does that answer your question or am I misreading what you're asking?

Thanks,
Joanne
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledged concern, provided explanation
Miklos Szeredi ↳ replying to Joanne Koong Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer noted that the current implementation has a potential performance issue due to multiple buffer pools, and suggested using a per-ring freelist for each buffer size instead, which would simplify allocation and reduce overhead. reviewer noted that the bufpool layer would still allow allocating and freeing single buffers, but questioned whether additional complexity or performance overhead for multi-bufpool support would be significant
Show original comment 
I don't think this is an issue.  Allocation should be as simple as
keeping per-ring freelist for each buffer size that would have all
free buffers across an arbitrary number of buf pools.

 I don't think it would add much additional complexity, given

---

To conclude: bufpools allow allocating/freeing multiple buffers
together.  The API is a superset, since allocating/freeing a single
buffer is still possible.   Additional complexity or performance for
the bufpool layer should not be significant.

Is that correct?

If yes, I'd go with bufpools.

Thanks,
Miklos
View on lore ↗
Signals: potential performance issue, suggested alternative solution, requested clarification on potential complexity/performance impact
Bernd Schubert ↳ replying to Joanne Koong Inline Review Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer suggested an alternative implementation for managing multiple buffer pools, where each pool is represented by an array entry in a separate config-defined array, and proposed modifying the fuse_uring_ent_in_out function to set the corresponding indices.
Show original comment 
Really sorry for late replies.

Couldn't we make a config SQE define a max array size and then every
array entry would be something like

struct buf_entry {
	char *payload;
	int buf_pool_idx; /* which buf pool */
	int idx; /* index within the buf pool */	
}


And then in fuse_uring_ent_in_out you would need to set buf_pool_idx and
buf_idx


Avoids which buf pool still has free entries, but buf pools get added to
that array, which then would have its own head and tail. Obviously you
would need to do another pointer lookup to get the actual payload.

Obviously has one more pointer than just a single buf ring.


Thanks,
Bernd
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes
Joanne Koong (author) ↳ replying to Bernd Schubert Positive LLM
Author acknowledged that the freelist concept, which allows for dynamic buffer pool addition and removal, is what Miklos was referring to, and confirmed that the uapi changes in this series are forward-compatible with multi-bufpools.
Show original comment 
I think the max-size array works but if it's backed by a linked list
instead of a fixed array, bufpool add/remove can be more dynamic (eg
no fixed ceiling). I think this is the freelist concept Miklos was
talking about. I don't think the implementation details really matter
right now though - all the kernel-internal stuff could be changed and
figured out after this series.

The uapi changes this series adds are forward-compatible with
multi-bufpools. It adds a uint16_t buf_id to fuse_uring_ent_in_out,
and there's already a uint16_t padding field right after it that a
pool_id can occupy later.

Thanks,
Joanne
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledged a key point, confirmed forward-compatibility

Last comment 2026-06-11 Re: [PATCH v3 00/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch series adds support for io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy to the FUSE file system, allowing for more efficient handling of I/O operations by reusing buffers instead of constantly allocating new ones.

4 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Miklos Szeredi ↳ replying to Joanne Koong Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer noted that the current implementation has a potential performance issue due to multiple buffer pools, and suggested using a per-ring freelist for each buffer size instead, which would simplify allocation and reduce overhead. reviewer noted that the bufpool layer would still allow allocating and freeing single buffers, but questioned whether additional complexity or performance overhead for multi-bufpool support would be significant
Show original comment 
I don't think this is an issue.  Allocation should be as simple as
keeping per-ring freelist for each buffer size that would have all
free buffers across an arbitrary number of buf pools.

 I don't think it would add much additional complexity, given

---

To conclude: bufpools allow allocating/freeing multiple buffers
together.  The API is a superset, since allocating/freeing a single
buffer is still possible.   Additional complexity or performance for
the bufpool layer should not be significant.

Is that correct?

If yes, I'd go with bufpools.

Thanks,
Miklos
View on lore ↗
Signals: potential performance issue, suggested alternative solution, requested clarification on potential complexity/performance impact
Bernd Schubert ↳ replying to Joanne Koong Inline Review Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer suggested an alternative implementation for managing multiple buffer pools, where each pool is represented by an array entry in a separate config-defined array, and proposed modifying the fuse_uring_ent_in_out function to set the corresponding indices.
Show original comment 
Really sorry for late replies.

Couldn't we make a config SQE define a max array size and then every
array entry would be something like

struct buf_entry {
	char *payload;
	int buf_pool_idx; /* which buf pool */
	int idx; /* index within the buf pool */	
}


And then in fuse_uring_ent_in_out you would need to set buf_pool_idx and
buf_idx


Avoids which buf pool still has free entries, but buf pools get added to
that array, which then would have its own head and tail. Obviously you
would need to do another pointer lookup to get the actual payload.

Obviously has one more pointer than just a single buf ring.


Thanks,
Bernd
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes
Joanne Koong (author) ↳ replying to Bernd Schubert Positive LLM
Author acknowledged that the freelist concept, which allows for dynamic buffer pool addition and removal, is what Miklos was referring to, and confirmed that the uapi changes in this series are forward-compatible with multi-bufpools.
Show original comment 
I think the max-size array works but if it's backed by a linked list
instead of a fixed array, bufpool add/remove can be more dynamic (eg
no fixed ceiling). I think this is the freelist concept Miklos was
talking about. I don't think the implementation details really matter
right now though - all the kernel-internal stuff could be changed and
figured out after this series.

The uapi changes this series adds are forward-compatible with
multi-bufpools. It adds a uint16_t buf_id to fuse_uring_ent_in_out,
and there's already a uint16_t padding field right after it that a
pool_id can occupy later.

Thanks,
Joanne

The thread discusses the status of a patchset for fuse and io-uring, specifically the zero-copy feature.

2 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Joanne Koong Neutral LLM
Raised concerns about dependency on bvec patches and suggested coordinating syncing between io-uring and fuse trees. Also requested that refactoring changes be picked up for 7.2.
Signals: dependency on bvec patches, merge window timing
Jens Axboe ↳ replying to Joanne Koong Reviewed-by Positive LLM
Confirmed that the io-uring prep patches are staged and can be merged for 7.2, but suggested carrying them forward if fuse isn't ready yet.
Show original comment 
On 6/12/26 3:46 PM, Joanne Koong wrote:
> On Fri, Jun 12, 2026 at 2:06?PM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>>
>> This series adds buffer pools (implemented internally as a buffer ring) and
>> zero-copy to fuse io-uring.
>>
>> Prior to this series, ents and buffers are tightly coupled where each entry
>> has its own dedicated payload buffer, requiring N buffers for N entries where
>> each buffer must be large enough to accomodate the maximum payload size. This
>> is suboptimal as most request types require vastly less bytes than the maximum
>> payload size and some requests do not require payload buffers at all.
>>
>> This series allows servers to pass in a buffer pool (internally managed as a
>> buffer ring) that the kernel will use for servicing ents/requests. This
>> decoupling reduces the memory usage requirements needed to use fuse-io-uring.
>> It allows for future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption and
>> supporting multiple buffer sizes per ring.
>>
>> This series also allows servers to use io-uring registered buffers as the
>> buffer pool which eliminates the overhead of pinning/unpinning user pages and
>> translating virtual addreses per i/o request.
>>
>> This series additionally adds zero copy to fuse io-uring. The server can
>> directly access client pages or page cache folios without copying data through
>> an intermediary buffer. This requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges and using
>> registered buffers. Zero copy (patch 9) has a dependency on io-uring registered
>> bvec changes in [1].
> 
> Miklos,
> 
> The zero-copy patch has a dependency on the bvec patches in Jens's
> io-uring tree. Since the merge window opens Monday, it's probably too
> tight now to cross-coordinate this for 7.2. What do you prefer for
> syncing between the two trees? If Jens stages the patches in a stable
> branch, are you fine with pulling that in and then applying the fuse
> patches on top?
> 
> Patches 7 and 8 (bufpools and registered buffers) don't have this
> dependency, but to make the uapi cleaner under one single umbrella
> flag, it would be better to co-ship them with the zero-copy patch.
> 
> Patches 1-6 are refactoring changes that have been out since October
> and have value on their own, independent of the bufpool and zero-copy
> work. Could you please pick these up for 7.2 so they don't have to be
> carried and repeatedly rebased?

FWIW, I reviewed the patches again and they still look fine to me. So
the 4 io_uring prep patches are staged, they are in my
for-7.2/io_uring-fuse branch. They can either go in for this coming
merge window, or we can carry them forward if fuse isn't quite ready
yet.

Since there will be conflicts with either the io_uring branches or block
branches, the above branch is for-7.2/io_uring + for-7.2/block to avoid
that.

-- 
Jens Axboe
Signals: reviewed patches again
Last comment 2026-06-09 Re: [PATCH v3 09/10] fuse: add zero-copy over io-uring Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch is a response to another patch that attempts to add zero-copy functionality over io-uring for the fuse filesystem. However, it points out that the current implementation has a flaw: when an abort occurs in userspace while a zero-copy request is still in-flight, the kernel will skip the necessary logic due to the absence of a valid command associated with the entry.

2 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Baokun Li ↳ replying to Joanne Koong Inline Review Reviewed-by Neutral LLM
Reviewer Baokun Li noted that when a zero-copy request is in-flight in userspace and an abort occurs, the entry's cmd field will be NULL due to fuse_uring_send() completing the command before the entry enters FRRS_USERSPACE, causing the if (cmd) guard to skip unregistering the buffer table slot. Baokun agreed that introducing complexity for an independent handle to the ring ctx is not worth it and accepted leaving the buffer table slot occupied until the ring is destroyed as a reasonable tradeoff.
Show original comment 
Hi Joanne,

You're right, I missed that. fuse_uring_send() completes the cmd
and sets ent->cmd = NULL before the entry enters FRRS_USERSPACE.
So when abort hits a zero-copied entry, cmd is already gone \u2014 the
if (cmd) guard skips the unregister entirely.

The timeline is:

 1. Request dispatched \u2192 io_buffer_register_bvec(), zero_copied = true
 2. fuse_uring_send() \u2192 ent->cmd = NULL, io_uring_cmd_done()
 3. Server doing zero-copy IO, ent in FRRS_USERSPACE, no cmd
 4. umount -f \u2192 teardown \u2192 cmd == NULL \u2192 unregister never fires

I now understand the problem you described \u2014 to unregister without
cmd, we'd need an independent handle to the ring ctx, and ensuring
its validity is where the complexity lies. I agree that it's not
worth introducing that complexity for this scenario, and leaving the
buffer table slot occupied until the ring is destroyed is a
reasonable tradeoff.

Thanks for taking the time to walk me through this.


Thanks,
Baokun
View on lore ↗
Signals: agreed with Joanne's explanation, accepted tradeoff
Joanne Koong (author) ↳ replying to Baokun Li Neutral LLM
Author acknowledged that the zero-copy request logic doesn't fire for ents in userspace, and agreed to add clarification to the commit message in v4.
Show original comment 
No worries at all, I think this is very subtle so I will add something
about this to the commit message in v4 to make this more clear.

Thanks,
Joanne
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledged a subtlety, agreed to add clarification
Re: [PATCH v3 09/10] fuse: add zero-copy over io-uring Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies
Re: [PATCH RESEND v3 4/4] fuse: clear ent->fuse_req in commit_fetch error path to prevent UAF Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies
Last comment 2026-06-08 Re: [PATCH 3/3] fuse: end fuse_req on io-uring cancel task work Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch ensures that a fuse request is properly ended when an io-uring cancel task work occurs, even if it doesn't result in a subsequent fuse connection abort. This prevents the application's syscall thread from becoming stuck uninterruptibly in D-state for the lifetime of the connection. The change addresses a specific edge case where tw.cancel happens without a corresponding fuse abort, which is not the common scenario but still needs to be handled.

3 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Bernd Schubert ↳ replying to Joanne Koong Inline Review Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer Bernd Schubert noted that the initial code used IO_URING_F_TASK_DEAD, which he had incorrectly assumed was related to PF_EXITING, and suggested that fuse_uring_cancel() and canceled fuse_uring_send_in_task() should go through fuse_uring_entry_teardown()
Show original comment 
I see, the initial code was using IO_URING_F_TASK_DEAD and I had wrongly 
assumed that is related to PF_EXITING. 
Well, I think the fix is clear, although I personally do not like the
exit code dup (or better triple)
https://lore.kernel.org/r/20260515045541.1171335-4-joannelkoong@gmail.com

In my option fuse_uring_cancel() and canceled fuse_uring_send_in_task() 
should go through fuse_uring_entry_teardown().


Thanks,
Bernd
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes
Joanne Koong (author) ↳ replying to Bernd Schubert Neutral LLM
Author acknowledged that canceling a task work doesn't necessarily imply connection abort and agreed to free the ent immediately on cancel, but expressed neutrality about the approach.
Show original comment 
I don't feel strongly about this. On cancel, the ent becomes useless
since cancel consumes the cmd, so imo it makes sense to just free and
clean up the ent immediately instead of keeping it around until the
fuse connection gets eventually aborted, but I don't think it really
matters. Happy to go with what you prefer.

Thanks,
Joanne
View on lore ↗
Signals: neutral tone, open-ended phrase 'I don't feel strongly about this'

Show original comment 
This fix (as well as [1]) needs to be backported to stable so I think
it'd be better to keep the fix as minimal as possible and do any
entry_teardown deduping as a follow-up cleanup after that. I'll send
out a v2 that fixes it minimally.

Thanks,
Joanne

This patch is not a new solution to the problem, but rather a reference to an existing patch that supposedly fixes the issue of a null-pointer dereference in fuse_readpages_end after an aborted splice read reply.

1 reviewer comment — 0 participants
0 participants
김민서 ↳ replying to Joanne Koong Reviewed-by Neutral LLM
reviewer confirmed that the two-patch series fixes their reproducer and did not observe any KASAN/Oops/panic in the recorded runs, indicating that the issue is resolved
Show original comment 
Hi Joanne,

Thank you for the pointer, and sorry for the duplicate report.

I applied the two-patch series starting at [1] on top of my v7.1-rc7 tree
and rebuilt the kernel with the same KASAN config.

Before applying the patches, the C reproducer triggered:

  KASAN: null-ptr-deref in range [0x0000000000000000-0x0000000000000007]
  RIP: fuse_readpages_end+0xe5/0x650

With the patched kernel, I reran the same C reproducer as uid 1000 with a
user namespace. I did not observe KASAN/Oops/panic in the recorded runs.

Based on my testing, the patch series appears to fix my reproducer.

Thank you for taking the time to check this.

Best regards,
Minseo Kim

On Tue, 9 Jun 2026 at 04:08, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:

> <devnull+bernd.bsbernd.com@kernel.org> wrote:
> >
> > From: Bernd Schubert <bernd@bsbernd.com>
> >
> > Bad userspace might try to trick us and send commit SQEs request
> > unique / commit-id of requests that are not even send to
> > fuse-server (io_uring_cmd_done() not called) yet.
> >
> > fuse_uring_commit_fetch() ends the fuse request when the ring entry
> > has a wrong state, but that could have caused a use-after-free
> > with the memcpy operations in fuse_uring_send_in_task().
> > In order to avoid such races the call of fuse_uring_add_to_pq()
> > is moved after the copy operations and just before completing
> > the io-uring request - malicious userspace cannot find the request
> > anymore until all prepration work in fuse-client/kernel is completed.
> >
> > This also moves fuse_uring_add_to_pq() a bit up in the code to
> > avoid a forward declaration. Also not with a preparation commit,
> > to make it easier to back port to older kernels.
> >
> > Reported-by: xlabai <xlabai@tencent.com>

4 items
Patches Submitted (1)
Ongoing Submitted 2026-05-27 Last comment 2026-06-09 ⏱ No recent activity [PATCH v5 0/9] mm: switch THP shrinker to list_lru 9 patches
Show 9 individual patches
  • [1/9] mm: list_lru: fix set_shrinker_bit() call during race with cgroup deletion Neutral Submitted 2026-05-27Last comment 2026-05-30 Usama Arif Wei Yang
  • [2/9] mm: list_lru: lock_list_lru_of_memcg() cannot return NULL if !skip_empty Submitted 2026-05-27
  • [3/9] mm: list_lru: deduplicate unlock_list_lru() Submitted 2026-05-27
  • [4/9] mm: list_lru: move list dead check to lock_list_lru_of_memcg() Submitted 2026-05-27
  • [5/9] mm: list_lru: deduplicate lock_list_lru() Neutral Submitted 2026-05-27Last comment 2026-05-30 Wei Yang
  • [6/9] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions Submitted 2026-05-27
  • [7/9] mm: list_lru: introduce folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() Submitted 2026-05-27
  • [8/9] mm: memory: flatten alloc_anon_folio() retry loop Neutral Submitted 2026-05-27Last comment 2026-05-30 Dev Jain
  • [9/9] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru Neutral Submitted 2026-05-27Last comment 2026-06-09 SeongJae Park Usama Arif Johannes Weiner (author) Kairui Song Wei Yang Lance Yang Lorenzo Stoakes
Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch series, version 5, aims to switch the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use list_lru instead of the current implementation. The changes include fixing a long-standing issue in set_shrinker_bit() where the bit can be set on a dying child memcg instead of the ancestor that received the item. Other changes include adapting to the new lock_list_lru_of_memcg() signature, propagating folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() failure, and fixing a use-after-free bug in deferred_split_isolate(). The patches have been rebased onto the latest mm-unstable tree.

9 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
SeongJae Park Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer SeongJae Park noted that the patch causes a compilation error in UM mode due to an implicit function declaration of folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() when CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE is unset, and suggested defining the function for this case.
Show original comment 
Hi Johannes,

On Wed, 27 May 2026 16:45:16 -0400 Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org> wrote:

> The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. The
> queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That means
> on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering reclaim
> can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
> 
>         alloc/unmap
>           deferred_split_folio()
>             list_add_tail(memcg->split_queue)
>             set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
>         for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes)
>           mem_cgroup_iter()
>             shrink_slab(node, memcg)
>               shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg)
>                 if test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
>                   deferred_split_scan()
>                     walks memcg->split_queue
> 
> The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
> has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
> by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
> 
> list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
> streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
> widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
> queue as well.
> 
> The list_lru per-memcg heads are instantiated on demand when the first
> object of interest is allocated for a cgroup, by calling
> folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(). Add calls to where splittable pages are
> created: anon faults, swapin faults, khugepaged collapse.
> 
> These calls create all possible node heads for the cgroup at once, so
> the migration code (between nodes) doesn't need any special care.
> 
> Reported-by: Mikhail Zaslonko <zaslonko@linux.ibm.com>
> Tested-by: Mikhail Zaslonko <zaslonko@linux.ibm.com>
> Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>
> Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
> ---
>  include/linux/huge_mm.h    |   7 +-
>  include/linux/memcontrol.h |   4 -
>  include/linux/mmzone.h     |  12 --
>  mm/huge_memory.c           | 364 +++++++++++++------------------------
>  mm/internal.h              |   2 +-
>  mm/khugepaged.c            |   5 +
>  mm/memcontrol.c            |  12 +-
>  mm/memory.c                |   4 +
>  mm/mm_init.c               |  15 --
>  mm/swap_state.c            |  10 +
>  10 files changed, 150 insertions(+), 285 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/include/linux/huge_mm.h b/include/linux/huge_mm.h
> index edece3e26985..f6c2531a27a3 100644
> --- a/include/linux/huge_mm.h
> +++ b/include/linux/huge_mm.h
> @@ -423,10 +423,10 @@ static inline int split_huge_page(struct page *page)
>  {
>  	return split_huge_page_to_list_to_order(page, NULL, 0);
>  }
> +
> +int folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(struct folio *folio);
> +
>  void deferred_split_folio(struct folio *folio, bool partially_mapped);
> -#ifdef CONFIG_MEMCG
> -void reparent_deferred_split_queue(struct mem_cgroup *memcg);
> -#endif
>  
>  void __split_huge_pmd(struct vm_area_struct *vma, pmd_t *pmd,
>  		unsigned long address, bool freeze);
> @@ -664,7 +664,6 @@ static inline int folio_split(struct folio *folio, unsigned int new_order,
>  }
>  
>  static inline void deferred_split_folio(struct folio *folio, bool partially_mapped) {}
> -static inline void reparent_deferred_split_queue(struct mem_cgroup *memcg) {}
>  #define split_huge_pmd(__vma, __pmd, __address)	\
>  	do { } while (0)

I found this patch is now in mm-new and it makes UM mode kunit fails like
below.

    $ ./tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py run --kunitconfig mm/damon/tests/
    [00:00:02] Configuring KUnit Kernel ...
    [00:00:02] Building KUnit Kernel ...
    Populating config with:
    $ make ARCH=um O=.kunit olddefconfig
    Building with:
    $ make all compile_commands.json scripts_gdb ARCH=um O=.kunit --jobs=8
    ERROR:root:../mm/swap_state.c: In function \u2018__swap_cache_alloc\u2019:
    ../mm/swap_state.c:468:26: error: implicit declaration of function \u2018folio_memcg_alloc_deferred\u2019 [-Wimplicit-function-declaration]
      468 |         if (order > 1 && folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
          |                          ^~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
    make[4]: *** [../scripts/Makefile.build:289: mm/swap_state.o] Error 1
    make[4]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....
    make[3]: *** [../scripts/Makefile.build:548: mm] Error 2
    make[3]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....
    make[2]: *** [/home/lkhack/linux/Makefile:2143: .] Error 2
    make[1]: *** [/home/lkhack/linux/Makefile:248: __sub-make] Error 2
    make: *** [Makefile:248: __sub-make] Error 2

Maybe we can define the function for CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE unset case?  I
confirmed the below attaching temporal fix works for at least kunit.


Thanks,
SJ

[...]
=== >8 ===
>From 23b5800dd49085707baee5774b74782c3e424f24 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>
Date: Wed, 27 May 2026 23:58:07 -0700
Subject: [PATCH] mm/huge_mm: define memcg_alloc_deferred() for
 !CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPPAGE
MIME-Version: 1.0
Content-Type: text/plain; charset=UTF-8
Content-Transfer-Encoding: 8bit

Without this, UM mode kunit fails like below.

    $ ./tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py run --kunitconfig mm/damon/tests/
    [00:00:02] Configuring KUnit Kernel ...
    [00:00:02] Building KUnit Kernel ...
    Populating config with:
    $ make ARCH=um O=.kunit olddefconfig
    Building with:
    $ make all compile_commands.json scripts_gdb ARCH=um O=.kunit --jobs=8
    ERROR:root:../mm/swap_state.c: In function \u2018__swap_cache_alloc\u2019:
    ../mm/swap_state.c:468:26: error: implicit declaration of function \u2018folio_memcg_alloc_deferred\u2019 [-Wimplicit-function-declaration]
      468 |         if (order > 1 && folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
          |                          ^~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
    make[4]: *** [../scripts/Makefile.build:289: mm/swap_state.o] Error 1
    make[4]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....
    make[3]: *** [../scripts/Makefile.build:548: mm] Error 2
    make[3]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....
    make[2]: *** [/home/lkhack/linux/Makefile:2143: .] Error 2
    make[1]: *** [/home/lkhack/linux/Makefile:248: __sub-make] Error 2
    make: *** [Makefile:248: __sub-make] Error 2

Fix by implementing the function for CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPPAGE unset
case.

Fixes: https://lore.kernel.org/20260527204757.2544958-10-hannes@cmpxchg.org
Signed-off-by: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>
---
 include/linux/huge_mm.h | 5 +++++
 1 file changed, 5 insertions(+)

diff --git a/include/linux/huge_mm.h b/include/linux/huge_mm.h
index f6c2531a27a35..055de7b8ed487 100644
--- a/include/linux/huge_mm.h
+++ b/include/linux/huge_mm.h
@@ -663,6 +663,11 @@ static inline int folio_split(struct folio *folio, unsigned int new_order,
 	return -EINVAL;
 }
 
+static inline int folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(struct folio *folio)
+{
+	return 0;
+}
+
 static inline void deferred_split_folio(struct folio *folio, bool partially_mapped) {}
 #define split_huge_pmd(__vma, __pmd, __address)	\
 	do { } while (0)
-- 
2.47.3
View on lore ↗
Signals: compiler_error, requested_change
Usama Arif Acked-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer noted that the deferred split queue handles cgroups suboptimally by being per-NUMA node or per-cgroup instead of their intersection, which can lead to large pages being split on other nodes; requested conversion to list_lru, which sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists and streamlines reclaim walks.
Show original comment 
On 27/05/2026 21:45, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> When list_lru_add() races with cgroup deletion, the shrinker bit is set
> on the wrong group and lost. This can cause a shrinker run to miss the
> cgroup that actually has the object.
> 
> When the passed in memcg is dead, the function finds the first non-dead
> parent from the passed in memcg and adds the object there; but the
> shrinker bit is set on the memcg that was passed in.
> 
> This bug is as old as the shrinker bitmap itself.
> 
> Fix it by returning the "effective" memcg from the locking function, and
> have the caller use that.
> 
> Fixes: fae91d6d8be5 ("mm/list_lru.c: set bit in memcg shrinker bitmap on first list_lru item appearance")
> Reported-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
> Reported-by: Sashiko
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
> ---
>  mm/list_lru.c | 26 +++++++++++++++-----------
>  1 file changed, 15 insertions(+), 11 deletions(-)
> 

Acked-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>

---

On 27/05/2026 21:45, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. The
> queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That means
> on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering reclaim
> can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
> 
>         alloc/unmap
>           deferred_split_folio()
>             list_add_tail(memcg->split_queue)
>             set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
>         for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes)
>           mem_cgroup_iter()
>             shrink_slab(node, memcg)
>               shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg)
>                 if test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
>                   deferred_split_scan()
>                     walks memcg->split_queue
> 
> The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
> has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
> by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
> 
> list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
> streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
> widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
> queue as well.
> 
> The list_lru per-memcg heads are instantiated on demand when the first
> object of interest is allocated for a cgroup, by calling
> folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(). Add calls to where splittable pages are
> created: anon faults, swapin faults, khugepaged collapse.
> 
> These calls create all possible node heads for the cgroup at once, so
> the migration code (between nodes) doesn't need any special care.
> 
> Reported-by: Mikhail Zaslonko <zaslonko@linux.ibm.com>
> Tested-by: Mikhail Zaslonko <zaslonko@linux.ibm.com>
> Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>
> Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
> ---
>  include/linux/huge_mm.h    |   7 +-
>  include/linux/memcontrol.h |   4 -
>  include/linux/mmzone.h     |  12 --
>  mm/huge_memory.c           | 364 +++++++++++++------------------------
>  mm/internal.h              |   2 +-
>  mm/khugepaged.c            |   5 +
>  mm/memcontrol.c            |  12 +-
>  mm/memory.c                |   4 +
>  mm/mm_init.c               |  15 --
>  mm/swap_state.c            |  10 +
>  10 files changed, 150 insertions(+), 285 deletions(-)
> 

[...]

> diff --git a/mm/memory.c b/mm/memory.c
> index 135f5c0f57bd..f22e61d8c8de 100644
> --- a/mm/memory.c
> +++ b/mm/memory.c
> @@ -5222,6 +5222,10 @@ static struct folio *alloc_anon_folio(struct vm_fault *vmf)
>  			folio_put(folio);
>  			goto next;
>  		}
> +		if (order > 1 && folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
> +			folio_put(folio);

Ah sorry, should have caught this in the previous version, do we need

count_mthp_stat(order, MTHP_STAT_ANON_FAULT_FALLBACK);

here?

or maybe we just goto next instead of goto fallback and trty next
viable order?


> +			goto fallback;
> +		}
>  		folio_throttle_swaprate(folio, gfp);
>  		/*
>  		 * When a folio is not zeroed during allocation

---

On 28/05/2026 15:02, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> On Thu, May 28, 2026 at 02:32:06PM +0100, Usama Arif wrote:
>>
>>
>> On 27/05/2026 21:45, Johannes Weiner wrote:
>>> The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. The
>>> queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That means
>>> on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering reclaim
>>> can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
>>>
>>>         alloc/unmap
>>>           deferred_split_folio()
>>>             list_add_tail(memcg->split_queue)
>>>             set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
>>>
>>>         for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes)
>>>           mem_cgroup_iter()
>>>             shrink_slab(node, memcg)
>>>               shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg)
>>>                 if test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
>>>                   deferred_split_scan()
>>>                     walks memcg->split_queue
>>>
>>> The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
>>> has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
>>> by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
>>>
>>> list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
>>> streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
>>> widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
>>> queue as well.
>>>
>>> The list_lru per-memcg heads are instantiated on demand when the first
>>> object of interest is allocated for a cgroup, by calling
>>> folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(). Add calls to where splittable pages are
>>> created: anon faults, swapin faults, khugepaged collapse.
>>>
>>> These calls create all possible node heads for the cgroup at once, so
>>> the migration code (between nodes) doesn't need any special care.
>>>
>>> Reported-by: Mikhail Zaslonko <zaslonko@linux.ibm.com>
>>> Tested-by: Mikhail Zaslonko <zaslonko@linux.ibm.com>
>>> Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>
>>> Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>
>>> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
>>> ---
>>>  include/linux/huge_mm.h    |   7 +-
>>>  include/linux/memcontrol.h |   4 -
>>>  include/linux/mmzone.h     |  12 --
>>>  mm/huge_memory.c           | 364 +++++++++++++------------------------
>>>  mm/internal.h              |   2 +-
>>>  mm/khugepaged.c            |   5 +
>>>  mm/memcontrol.c            |  12 +-
>>>  mm/memory.c                |   4 +
>>>  mm/mm_init.c               |  15 --
>>>  mm/swap_state.c            |  10 +
>>>  10 files changed, 150 insertions(+), 285 deletions(-)
>>>
>>
>> [...]
>>
>>> diff --git a/mm/memory.c b/mm/memory.c
>>> index 135f5c0f57bd..f22e61d8c8de 100644
>>> --- a/mm/memory.c
>>> +++ b/mm/memory.c
>>> @@ -5222,6 +5222,10 @@ static struct folio *alloc_anon_folio(struct vm_fault *vmf)
>>>  			folio_put(folio);
>>>  			goto next;
>>>  		}
>>> +		if (order > 1 && folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
>>> +			folio_put(folio);
>>
>> Ah sorry, should have caught this in the previous version, do we need
>>
>> count_mthp_stat(order, MTHP_STAT_ANON_FAULT_FALLBACK);
>>
>> here?
> 
> This isn't an allocation we expect to fail with any sort of routine
> that we'd need to capture it in the event counter. It would warn in
> dmesg if it did. But in practice it can't happen at all, since it's a
> sub-costly-order slab allocation and the allocator would loop and OOM
> kill stuff until it succeeds.
> 
>> or maybe we just goto next instead of goto fallback and trty next
>> viable order?
> 
> Again I don't think it matters, but fallback seems a bit more correct
> because the size of the list_lru allocation doesn't change with lower
> orders (until we hit 0).
> 
> So I think we can just leave it as is.

Ack!

Acked-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes
Johannes Weiner (author) Neutral LLM
The author is addressing a concern about the need to count MTHP statistics for sub-costly-order slab allocations in alloc_anon_folio(). They acknowledge that it's not an allocation expected to fail, and while they agree that fallback seems more correct, they don't think it matters and suggest leaving the code as is.
Show original comment 
On Thu, May 28, 2026 at 02:32:06PM +0100, Usama Arif wrote:
> 
> 
> On 27/05/2026 21:45, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> > The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. The
> > queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That means
> > on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering reclaim
> > can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
> > 
> >         alloc/unmap
> >           deferred_split_folio()
> >             list_add_tail(memcg->split_queue)
> >             set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> > 
> >         for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes)
> >           mem_cgroup_iter()
> >             shrink_slab(node, memcg)
> >               shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg)
> >                 if test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> >                   deferred_split_scan()
> >                     walks memcg->split_queue
> > 
> > The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
> > has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
> > by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
> > 
> > list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
> > streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
> > widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
> > queue as well.
> > 
> > The list_lru per-memcg heads are instantiated on demand when the first
> > object of interest is allocated for a cgroup, by calling
> > folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(). Add calls to where splittable pages are
> > created: anon faults, swapin faults, khugepaged collapse.
> > 
> > These calls create all possible node heads for the cgroup at once, so
> > the migration code (between nodes) doesn't need any special care.
> > 
> > Reported-by: Mikhail Zaslonko <zaslonko@linux.ibm.com>
> > Tested-by: Mikhail Zaslonko <zaslonko@linux.ibm.com>
> > Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>
> > Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>
> > Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
> > ---
> >  include/linux/huge_mm.h    |   7 +-
> >  include/linux/memcontrol.h |   4 -
> >  include/linux/mmzone.h     |  12 --
> >  mm/huge_memory.c           | 364 +++++++++++++------------------------
> >  mm/internal.h              |   2 +-
> >  mm/khugepaged.c            |   5 +
> >  mm/memcontrol.c            |  12 +-
> >  mm/memory.c                |   4 +
> >  mm/mm_init.c               |  15 --
> >  mm/swap_state.c            |  10 +
> >  10 files changed, 150 insertions(+), 285 deletions(-)
> > 
> 
> [...]
> 
> > diff --git a/mm/memory.c b/mm/memory.c
> > index 135f5c0f57bd..f22e61d8c8de 100644
> > --- a/mm/memory.c
> > +++ b/mm/memory.c
> > @@ -5222,6 +5222,10 @@ static struct folio *alloc_anon_folio(struct vm_fault *vmf)
> >  			folio_put(folio);
> >  			goto next;
> >  		}
> > +		if (order > 1 && folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
> > +			folio_put(folio);
> 
> Ah sorry, should have caught this in the previous version, do we need
> 
> count_mthp_stat(order, MTHP_STAT_ANON_FAULT_FALLBACK);
> 
> here?

This isn't an allocation we expect to fail with any sort of routine
that we'd need to capture it in the event counter. It would warn in
dmesg if it did. But in practice it can't happen at all, since it's a
sub-costly-order slab allocation and the allocator would loop and OOM
kill stuff until it succeeds.

> or maybe we just goto next instead of goto fallback and trty next
> viable order?

Again I don't think it matters, but fallback seems a bit more correct
because the size of the list_lru allocation doesn't change with lower
orders (until we hit 0).

So I think we can just leave it as is.

---

On Thu, May 28, 2026 at 12:08:05AM -0700, SeongJae Park wrote:
> From 23b5800dd49085707baee5774b74782c3e424f24 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
> From: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>
> Date: Wed, 27 May 2026 23:58:07 -0700
> Subject: [PATCH] mm/huge_mm: define memcg_alloc_deferred() for
>  !CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPPAGE
> MIME-Version: 1.0
> Content-Type: text/plain; charset=UTF-8
> Content-Transfer-Encoding: 8bit
> 
> Without this, UM mode kunit fails like below.
> 
>     $ ./tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py run --kunitconfig mm/damon/tests/
>     [00:00:02] Configuring KUnit Kernel ...
>     [00:00:02] Building KUnit Kernel ...
>     Populating config with:
>     $ make ARCH=um O=.kunit olddefconfig
>     Building with:
>     $ make all compile_commands.json scripts_gdb ARCH=um O=.kunit --jobs=8
>     ERROR:root:../mm/swap_state.c: In function \u2018__swap_cache_alloc\u2019:
>     ../mm/swap_state.c:468:26: error: implicit declaration of function \u2018folio_memcg_alloc_deferred\u2019 [-Wimplicit-function-declaration]
>       468 |         if (order > 1 && folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
>           |                          ^~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
>     make[4]: *** [../scripts/Makefile.build:289: mm/swap_state.o] Error 1
>     make[4]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....
>     make[3]: *** [../scripts/Makefile.build:548: mm] Error 2
>     make[3]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....
>     make[2]: *** [/home/lkhack/linux/Makefile:2143: .] Error 2
>     make[1]: *** [/home/lkhack/linux/Makefile:248: __sub-make] Error 2
>     make: *** [Makefile:248: __sub-make] Error 2
> 
> Fix by implementing the function for CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPPAGE unset
> case.
> 
> Fixes: https://lore.kernel.org/20260527204757.2544958-10-hannes@cmpxchg.org
> Signed-off-by: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

Whoops, thanks for the fix, SJ. I'll incorporate UM builds into my
final compile test before sending.

Acked-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

---

On Fri, May 29, 2026 at 09:56:28AM +0000, Wei Yang wrote:
> On Wed, May 27, 2026 at 04:45:12PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> >The MEMCG and !MEMCG paths have the same pattern. Share the code.
> >
> >Reviewed-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
> >Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>
> >Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>
> >Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
> >Reviewed-by: Liam R. Howlett (Oracle) <liam@infradead.org>
> >---
> > mm/list_lru.c | 21 +++++++++------------
> > 1 file changed, 9 insertions(+), 12 deletions(-)
> >
> >diff --git a/mm/list_lru.c b/mm/list_lru.c
> >index 7d0523e44010..fdb3fe2ea64f 100644
> >--- a/mm/list_lru.c
> >+++ b/mm/list_lru.c
> >@@ -15,6 +15,14 @@
> > #include "slab.h"
> > #include "internal.h"
> 
> Hi, Johannes
> 
> One very tiny nit below.
> 
> > 
> >+static inline void lock_list_lru(struct list_lru_one *l, bool irq)
> 
> Here we use @irq.
> 
> >+{
> >+	if (irq)
> >+		spin_lock_irq(&l->lock);
> >+	else
> >+		spin_lock(&l->lock);
> >+}
> >+
> > static inline void unlock_list_lru(struct list_lru_one *l, bool irq_off)
> 
> Here we use @irq_off.
> 
> Do you think it would be nicer to unify the parameter name?

Yes, I think it would be nicer.

Note that I inherited this - we had irq on the lock and irq_off on the
unlock before already. I didn't want to mix even more yak shaving prep
patches into this series.

Mind sending a follow-up patch on top of mm-unstable?

---

On Mon, Jun 01, 2026 at 09:21:35PM +0800, Lance Yang wrote:
> 
> On Wed, May 27, 2026 at 04:45:16PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> [...]
> >diff --git a/mm/swap_state.c b/mm/swap_state.c
> >index 04f5ce992401..9c3a5cf99778 100644
> >--- a/mm/swap_state.c
> >+++ b/mm/swap_state.c
> >@@ -465,6 +465,16 @@ static struct folio *__swap_cache_alloc(struct swap_cluster_info *ci,
> > 		return ERR_PTR(-ENOMEM);
> > 	}
> > 
> 
> Shouldn't this be limited to anon swapin?
> 
> e.g. vmf && vma_is_anonymous(vmf->vma)
> 
> >+	if (order > 1 && folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
> 
> __swap_cache_alloc() is also used by shmem direct swapin, so shmem can
> get here too when handling a large swap entry:
> 
> shmem_get_folio_gfp()
>   shmem_swapin_folio()
>     shmem_swap_alloc_folio()
>       swapin_sync()
>         swap_cache_alloc_folio()
>           __swap_cache_alloc()
>             folio_memcg_alloc_deferred()

Good catch, I think you're right. I shouldn't have dismissed that
branch due to "/* Direct swapin skipping swap cache & readahead */"

> @Baolin please correct me if I got it wrong :)
> 
> folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() itself doesn't filter shmem out either; it
> only allocates the memcg list_lru metadata for deferred_split_lru:
> 
> int folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(struct folio *folio)
> {
> 	if (mem_cgroup_disabled())
> 		return 0;
> 	return folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(folio, &deferred_split_lru, GFP_KERNEL);
> }
> 
> Since deferred_split_lru only queues anon large folios, doing this for
> shmem swapin doesn't buy us anything :)

Yes, agreed. I don't think it's a big deal / show stopper in terms of
user-visible effect, but of course still worth fixing.

I'll send a follow-up patch.
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledges a concern, explains reasoning
Wei Yang Inline Review Reviewed-by Neutral LLM
Wei Yang noted that the lock_list_lru() and unlock_list_lru() functions use inconsistent parameter names (irq vs irq_off), suggesting unification for clarity.
Show original comment 
On Wed, May 27, 2026 at 04:45:12PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
>The MEMCG and !MEMCG paths have the same pattern. Share the code.
>
>Reviewed-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
>Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>
>Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>
>Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
>Reviewed-by: Liam R. Howlett (Oracle) <liam@infradead.org>
>---
> mm/list_lru.c | 21 +++++++++------------
> 1 file changed, 9 insertions(+), 12 deletions(-)
>
>diff --git a/mm/list_lru.c b/mm/list_lru.c
>index 7d0523e44010..fdb3fe2ea64f 100644
>--- a/mm/list_lru.c
>+++ b/mm/list_lru.c
>@@ -15,6 +15,14 @@
> #include "slab.h"
> #include "internal.h"

Hi, Johannes

One very tiny nit below.

> 
>+static inline void lock_list_lru(struct list_lru_one *l, bool irq)

Here we use @irq.

>+{
>+	if (irq)
>+		spin_lock_irq(&l->lock);
>+	else
>+		spin_lock(&l->lock);
>+}
>+
> static inline void unlock_list_lru(struct list_lru_one *l, bool irq_off)

Here we use @irq_off.

Do you think it would be nicer to unify the parameter name?

Also the name in callsite and the argument annotation. Would it be cleaner
readers?

-- 
Wei Yang
Help you, Help me

---

On Fri, May 29, 2026 at 09:42:02AM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
>On Fri, May 29, 2026 at 09:56:28AM +0000, Wei Yang wrote:
>> On Wed, May 27, 2026 at 04:45:12PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
>> >The MEMCG and !MEMCG paths have the same pattern. Share the code.
>> >
>> >Reviewed-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
>> >Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>
>> >Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>
>> >Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
>> >Reviewed-by: Liam R. Howlett (Oracle) <liam@infradead.org>
>> >---
>> > mm/list_lru.c | 21 +++++++++------------
>> > 1 file changed, 9 insertions(+), 12 deletions(-)
>> >
>> >diff --git a/mm/list_lru.c b/mm/list_lru.c
>> >index 7d0523e44010..fdb3fe2ea64f 100644
>> >--- a/mm/list_lru.c
>> >+++ b/mm/list_lru.c
>> >@@ -15,6 +15,14 @@
>> > #include "slab.h"
>> > #include "internal.h"
>> 
>> Hi, Johannes
>> 
>> One very tiny nit below.
>> 
>> > 
>> >+static inline void lock_list_lru(struct list_lru_one *l, bool irq)
>> 
>> Here we use @irq.
>> 
>> >+{
>> >+	if (irq)
>> >+		spin_lock_irq(&l->lock);
>> >+	else
>> >+		spin_lock(&l->lock);
>> >+}
>> >+
>> > static inline void unlock_list_lru(struct list_lru_one *l, bool irq_off)
>> 
>> Here we use @irq_off.
>> 
>> Do you think it would be nicer to unify the parameter name?
>
>Yes, I think it would be nicer.
>
>Note that I inherited this - we had irq on the lock and irq_off on the
>unlock before already. I didn't want to mix even more yak shaving prep
>patches into this series.

Reasonable.

>
>Mind sending a follow-up patch on top of mm-unstable?

Thanks, I am glad to.

Since this is trivial, I would wait until everything is settle down.

Looks good to you?

-- 
Wei Yang
Help you, Help me

---

On Wed, May 27, 2026 at 04:45:08PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
>When list_lru_add() races with cgroup deletion, the shrinker bit is set
>on the wrong group and lost. This can cause a shrinker run to miss the
>cgroup that actually has the object.
>
>When the passed in memcg is dead, the function finds the first non-dead
>parent from the passed in memcg and adds the object there; but the
>shrinker bit is set on the memcg that was passed in.
>

This means we just miss to reclaim some obj, but won't crash the kernel.

>This bug is as old as the shrinker bitmap itself.
>
>Fix it by returning the "effective" memcg from the locking function, and
>have the caller use that.
>
>Fixes: fae91d6d8be5 ("mm/list_lru.c: set bit in memcg shrinker bitmap on first list_lru item appearance")
>Reported-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
>Reported-by: Sashiko
>Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

So we don't want to cc stable, right?

The fix looks right, so

Reviewed-by: Wei Yang <richard.weiyang@gmail.com>

-- 
Wei Yang
Help you, Help me

---

On Wed, May 27, 2026 at 04:45:16PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
>The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. The
>queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That means
>on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering reclaim
>can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
>
>        alloc/unmap
>          deferred_split_folio()
>            list_add_tail(memcg->split_queue)
>            set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
>
>        for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes)
>          mem_cgroup_iter()
>            shrink_slab(node, memcg)
>              shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg)
>                if test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
>                  deferred_split_scan()
>                    walks memcg->split_queue
>
>The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
>has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
>by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
>
>list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
>streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
>widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
>queue as well.
>
>The list_lru per-memcg heads are instantiated on demand when the first
>object of interest is allocated for a cgroup, by calling
>folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(). Add calls to where splittable pages are
>created: anon faults, swapin faults, khugepaged collapse.
>
>These calls create all possible node heads for the cgroup at once, so
>the migration code (between nodes) doesn't need any special care.
>
>Reported-by: Mikhail Zaslonko <zaslonko@linux.ibm.com>
>Tested-by: Mikhail Zaslonko <zaslonko@linux.ibm.com>
>Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>
>Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>
>Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
>---
> include/linux/huge_mm.h    |   7 +-
> include/linux/memcontrol.h |   4 -
> include/linux/mmzone.h     |  12 --
> mm/huge_memory.c           | 364 +++++++++++++------------------------
> mm/internal.h              |   2 +-
> mm/khugepaged.c            |   5 +
> mm/memcontrol.c            |  12 +-
> mm/memory.c                |   4 +
> mm/mm_init.c               |  15 --
> mm/swap_state.c            |  10 +
> 10 files changed, 150 insertions(+), 285 deletions(-)
>
[...]
>@@ -1379,6 +1285,14 @@ static struct folio *vma_alloc_anon_folio_pmd(struct vm_area_struct *vma,
> 		count_mthp_stat(order, MTHP_STAT_ANON_FAULT_FALLBACK_CHARGE);
> 		return NULL;
> 	}
>+
>+	if (folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
>+		folio_put(folio);
>+		count_vm_event(THP_FAULT_FALLBACK);
>+		count_mthp_stat(order, MTHP_STAT_ANON_FAULT_FALLBACK);
>+		return NULL;
>+	}
>+

Nit: we have three possible failure point, and some duplicate
count_xxx_event/state().

Maybe we can have a followup cleanup for it.

Others, looks good. Thanks.

-- 
Wei Yang
Help you, Help me
View on lore ↗
Signals: suggested improvement, nit
Kairui Song Inline Review Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer Kairui Song noted that the deferred split queue handles cgroups suboptimally by not considering the intersection of nodes and cgroups, which can lead to large pages being split on other nodes; he suggested converting the deferred split queue to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists and streamlines list operations and reclaim walks.
Show original comment 
On Thu, May 28, 2026 at 4:48 AM Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org> wrote:
>
> The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. The
> queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That means
> on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering reclaim
> can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
>
>         alloc/unmap
>           deferred_split_folio()
>             list_add_tail(memcg->split_queue)
>             set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
>
>         for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes)
>           mem_cgroup_iter()
>             shrink_slab(node, memcg)
>               shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg)
>                 if test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
>                   deferred_split_scan()
>                     walks memcg->split_queue
>
> The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
> has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
> by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
>
> list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
> streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
> widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
> queue as well.
>
> The list_lru per-memcg heads are instantiated on demand when the first
> object of interest is allocated for a cgroup, by calling
> folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(). Add calls to where splittable pages are
> created: anon faults, swapin faults, khugepaged collapse.
>
> These calls create all possible node heads for the cgroup at once, so
> the migration code (between nodes) doesn't need any special care.
>
> Reported-by: Mikhail Zaslonko <zaslonko@linux.ibm.com>
> Tested-by: Mikhail Zaslonko <zaslonko@linux.ibm.com>
> Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>
> Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
> ---
>  include/linux/huge_mm.h    |   7 +-
>  include/linux/memcontrol.h |   4 -
>  include/linux/mmzone.h     |  12 --
>  mm/huge_memory.c           | 364 +++++++++++++------------------------
>  mm/internal.h              |   2 +-
>  mm/khugepaged.c            |   5 +
>  mm/memcontrol.c            |  12 +-
>  mm/memory.c                |   4 +
>  mm/mm_init.c               |  15 --
>  mm/swap_state.c            |  10 +
>  10 files changed, 150 insertions(+), 285 deletions(-)

...

> diff --git a/mm/swap_state.c b/mm/swap_state.c
> index 04f5ce992401..9c3a5cf99778 100644
> --- a/mm/swap_state.c
> +++ b/mm/swap_state.c
> @@ -465,6 +465,16 @@ static struct folio *__swap_cache_alloc(struct swap_cluster_info *ci,
>                 return ERR_PTR(-ENOMEM);
>         }
>
> +       if (order > 1 && folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
> +               spin_lock(&ci->lock);
> +               __swap_cache_do_del_folio(ci, folio, entry, shadow);
> +               spin_unlock(&ci->lock);
> +               folio_unlock(folio);
> +               /* nr_pages refs from swap cache, 1 from allocation */
> +               folio_put_refs(folio, nr_pages + 1);
> +               return ERR_PTR(-ENOMEM);
> +       }
> +

Thanks!

Nit: I think it would be better if we move the error handling under a
label to be shared with the charge failure above, but it's fine this
way, can be simplified later.

Reviewed-by: Kairui Song <kasong@tencent.com>

Lance Yang Needs Work LLM
Lance Yang noted that the new list_lru deferred split queue may lose reclaim in the cgroup.memory=nokmem case, and requested further investigation.
Show original comment 
Hi Johannes,

On Wed, May 27, 2026 at 04:45:07PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
>This is version 5 of switching the THP shrinker to list_lru.
>
>Core of the new version is the list_lru/set_shrinker_bit fix up front,
>which minimally affects later patches; and a rebase onto the latest
>mm-unstable - replaced alloc_swap_folio() with __swap_cache_alloc().
>
>The changes seemed small enough that *I chose to keep the review tags
>from v4*. Please shout if you object to this!
>
>Changes in v5:
>- patch 1 is a new fix for a very old, pre-existing set_shrinker_bit()
>  problem in list_lru, where the bit can be set on a dying child memcg
>  instead of the ancestor that actually received the item. Pointed out
>  by Usama Arif and Sashiko; fix it first to make it minimally
>  backportable and so the conversion is safe.
>- patches 6 and 9 adapt to that fix's new memcg-by-reference
>  lock_list_lru_of_memcg() signature
>- collapse_huge_page(): propagate folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() failure
>  as SCAN_ALLOC_HUGE_PAGE_FAIL instead of leaking SCAN_SUCCEED and
>  falsely reporting a successful MADV_COLLAPSE (Usama Arif, Sashiko)
>- deferred_split_isolate(): fix a UAF by reading folio state before
>  list_lru_isolate(); once removed, a racing folio_put() frees the
>  folio via the lockless list_empty() check while we still touch its
>  flags and stats (Sashiko)
>- rebased to mm-unstable of 2026-05-27, which simplifies the flatten
>  prep patch (now anon-only, as alloc_swap_folio() was folded into the
>  new __swap_cache_alloc()) and moves the swap-side
>  folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() hook into __swap_cache_alloc(). Kairui,
>  I would appreciate an eyeball on that.
>
>Changes in v4:
>- guard folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() with mem_cgroup_disabled() to fix
>  NULL deref in __memcg_list_lru_alloc() when booting with
>  cgroup_disable=memory (e.g., kdump capture kernel) -- reported and
>  tested by Mikhail Zaslonko on s390 and x86
>- flatten if (folio) branches in alloc_swap_folio() and alloc_anon_folio()
>  in a prep patch so the list_lru allocation additions are a clean minimal
>  diff (Lorenzo)
>- folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() moved out of alloc_charge_folio() into the
>  anon-only collapse_huge_page() path; collapse_file() shares that helper
>  but its pages don't go on the THP shrinker queue (David)
>- guard folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() with order > 1; mTHPs below order-2
>  can't be queued on the deferred split list (David)
>- make deferred_split_lru static, hide behind folio_memcg_alloc_deferred()
>  wrapper with GFP_KERNEL (Lorenzo)
>- rename l -> lru throughout huge_memory.c (Lorenzo)
>- kdoc for folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() (Lorenzo)
>- list_lru_lock_irq()/unlock_irq()/add_irq() irq-disabling variants;
>  use list_lru_add_irq() in deferred_split_scan() (Lorenzo)
>- reorder shrinker_free() before list_lru_destroy() (Lorenzo)
>
>Changes in v3:
>- dedicated lockdep_key for irqsafe deferred_split_lru.lock (syzbot)
>- conditional list_lru ops in __folio_freeze_and_split_unmapped() (syzbot)
>- annotate runs of inscrutable false, NULL, false function arguments (David)
>- rename to folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() (David)
>
>Changes in v2:
>- explicit rcu_read_lock() in __folio_freeze_and_split_unmapped() (Usama)
>- split out list_lru prep bits (Dave)
>
>The open-coded deferred split queue has issues. It's not NUMA-aware
>(when cgroup is enabled), and it's more complicated in the callsites
>interacting with it. Switching to list_lru fixes the NUMA problem and
>streamlines things. It also simplifies planned shrinker work.
>
>Patch 1 fixes a pre-existing list_lru bug where the shrinker bit is
>set on the caller's memcg rather than the ancestor whose sublist the
>item actually lands on after a walk-up. Standalone, backportable; the
>rest of the series depends on it.
>
>Patches 2-5 are cleanups and small refactors in list_lru code. They're
>basically independent, but make the THP shrinker conversion easier.
>
>Patch 6 extends the list_lru API to allow the caller to control the
>locking scope. The THP shrinker has private state it needs to keep
>synchronized with the LRU state.
>
>Patch 7 extends the list_lru API with a convenience helper to do
>list_lru head allocation (memcg_list_lru_alloc) when coming from a
>folio. Anon THPs are instantiated in several places, and with the
>folio reparenting patches pending, folio_memcg() access is now a more
>delicate dance. This avoids having to replicate that dance everywhere.
>
>Patch 8 flattens the alloc_anon_folio() retry loop so the next patch's
>list_lru hook lands as a clean addition rather than nested deep inside
>an if (folio) block.
>
>Patch 9 finally switches the deferred_split_queue to list_lru.

As the changelog above says, the old queue is per-memcg only, rather
than per-memcg-per-node. So reclaim on one node can still walk the whole
memcg queue and split underused THPs from other nodes in the same memcg.

But I think the new one can lose reclaim in the cgroup.memory=nokmem
case ...

With nokmem, the deferred shrinker can still run from memcg reclaim,
because it is SHRINKER_NONSLAB. But the list_lru is no longer per-memcg:

__list_lru_init() clears memcg_aware,

	if (mem_cgroup_kmem_disabled())
		memcg_aware = false;

so list_lru_from_memcg_idx() falls back to the shared node list:

static inline struct list_lru_one *
list_lru_from_memcg_idx(struct list_lru *lru, int nid, int idx)
{
	if (list_lru_memcg_aware(lru) && idx >= 0) {
[...]
	}
	return &lru->node[nid].lru;
}

That makes the shrinker bit unreliable. __list_lru_add() still sets the
bit on the memcg passed in, but only when the list goes from empty to
non-empty:

bool __list_lru_add(struct list_lru *lru, struct list_lru_one *l,
		    struct list_head *item, int nid,
		    struct mem_cgroup *memcg)
{
	if (list_empty(item)) {
[...]
		if (!l->nr_items++)
			set_shrinker_bit(memcg, nid, lru_shrinker_id(lru));
[...]
		return true;
	}
	return false;
}

If memcg A adds the first folio, A gets the bit. If memcg B later adds a
folio to the same shared list, B does not get a bit, because the list
was already non-empty.

So in the A-first/B-later case, reclaim from B may not call the deferred
shrinker at all. The shared list is scanned from memcg reclaim only if
reclaim runs from the memcg that has the bit, such as A here, or from
global reclaim :)

Anyway, only after the shared list is emptied does the next memcg to add
a folio get to be the one with the bit, IIUC :)

Hopefully I didn't miss somthing important ...

Cheers, Lance

---

On Wed, May 27, 2026 at 04:45:16PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
[...]
>diff --git a/mm/memory.c b/mm/memory.c
>index 135f5c0f57bd..f22e61d8c8de 100644
>--- a/mm/memory.c
>+++ b/mm/memory.c
>@@ -5222,6 +5222,10 @@ static struct folio *alloc_anon_folio(struct vm_fault *vmf)
> 			folio_put(folio);
> 			goto next;
> 		}
>+		if (order > 1 && folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
>+			folio_put(folio);

Missing a MTHP_STAT_ANON_FAULT_FALLBACK bump here?

Since we jump straight to fallback and end up at order-0 :)

>+			goto fallback;
>+		}
> 		folio_throttle_swaprate(folio, gfp);
> 		/*
> 		 * When a folio is not zeroed during allocation
[...]

Cheers, Lance

---

> 
> On Wed, May 27, 2026 at 04:45:16PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> [...]
>> diff --git a/mm/memory.c b/mm/memory.c

>> --- a/mm/memory.c
>> +++ b/mm/memory.c
>> @@ -5222,6 +5222,10 @@ static struct folio *alloc_anon_folio(struct vm_fault *vmf)
>> 			folio_put(folio);
>> 			goto next;
>> 		}
>> +		if (order > 1 && folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
>> +			folio_put(folio);
> 
> Missing a MTHP_STAT_ANON_FAULT_FALLBACK bump here?
> 
> Since we jump straight to fallback and end up at order-0 :)

Sorry for the noise, I missed the earlier discussion. Never mind this 
one, please.

> 
>> +			goto fallback;
>> +		}
>> 		folio_throttle_swaprate(folio, gfp);
>> 		/*
>> 		 * When a folio is not zeroed during allocation
