---

On Wed, May 27, 2026 at 04:45:16PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
[...]
>diff --git a/mm/swap_state.c b/mm/swap_state.c
>index 04f5ce992401..9c3a5cf99778 100644
>--- a/mm/swap_state.c
>+++ b/mm/swap_state.c
>@@ -465,6 +465,16 @@ static struct folio *__swap_cache_alloc(struct swap_cluster_info *ci,
> 		return ERR_PTR(-ENOMEM);
> 	}
> 

Shouldn't this be limited to anon swapin?

e.g. vmf && vma_is_anonymous(vmf->vma)

>+	if (order > 1 && folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {

__swap_cache_alloc() is also used by shmem direct swapin, so shmem can
get here too when handling a large swap entry:

shmem_get_folio_gfp()
  shmem_swapin_folio()
    shmem_swap_alloc_folio()
      swapin_sync()
        swap_cache_alloc_folio()
          __swap_cache_alloc()
            folio_memcg_alloc_deferred()

@Baolin please correct me if I got it wrong :)

folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() itself doesn't filter shmem out either; it
only allocates the memcg list_lru metadata for deferred_split_lru:

int folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(struct folio *folio)
{
	if (mem_cgroup_disabled())
		return 0;
	return folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(folio, &deferred_split_lru, GFP_KERNEL);
}

Since deferred_split_lru only queues anon large folios, doing this for
shmem swapin doesn't buy us anything :)

Maybe I'm missing something, just wanted to raise it.

>+		spin_lock(&ci->lock);
>+		__swap_cache_do_del_folio(ci, folio, entry, shadow);
>+		spin_unlock(&ci->lock);
>+		folio_unlock(folio);
>+		/* nr_pages refs from swap cache, 1 from allocation */
>+		folio_put_refs(folio, nr_pages + 1);
>+		return ERR_PTR(-ENOMEM);
>+	}
>+
> 	/* memsw uncharges swap when folio is added to swap cache */
> 	memcg1_swapin(folio);
> 	if (shadow)
>-- 
>2.54.0
>
>
View on lore ↗
Signals: potential issue, further investigation needed
Lance Yang Inline Review Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Lance Yang pointed out that vma_is_anonymous() is not sufficient to filter shmem swapin, as a file-backed VMA can be mapped privately and still be anonymous; instead, he suggested using !vma_is_shmem(vma) to correctly limit the allocation of deferred split metadata to anon swapin
Show original comment 
On 2026/6/9 15:35, Lorenzo Stoakes wrote:
> On Tue, Jun 09, 2026 at 11:20:58AM +0800, Lance Yang wrote:
>>
>> On Mon, Jun 01, 2026 at 02:17:28PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
>>> On Mon, Jun 01, 2026 at 09:21:35PM +0800, Lance Yang wrote:
>>>>
>>>> On Wed, May 27, 2026 at 04:45:16PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
>>>> [...]
>>>>> diff --git a/mm/swap_state.c b/mm/swap_state.c
>>>>> index 04f5ce992401..9c3a5cf99778 100644
>>>>> --- a/mm/swap_state.c
>>>>> +++ b/mm/swap_state.c
>>>>> @@ -465,6 +465,16 @@ static struct folio *__swap_cache_alloc(struct swap_cluster_info *ci,
>>>>> 		return ERR_PTR(-ENOMEM);
>>>>> 	}
>>>>>
>>>>
>>>> Shouldn't this be limited to anon swapin?
>>>>
>>>> e.g. vmf && vma_is_anonymous(vmf->vma)
>>>>
>>>>> +	if (order > 1 && folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
>>>>
>>>> __swap_cache_alloc() is also used by shmem direct swapin, so shmem can
>>>> get here too when handling a large swap entry:
>>>>
>>>> shmem_get_folio_gfp()
>>>>    shmem_swapin_folio()
>>>>      shmem_swap_alloc_folio()
>>>>        swapin_sync()
>>>>          swap_cache_alloc_folio()
>>>>            __swap_cache_alloc()
>>>>              folio_memcg_alloc_deferred()
>>>
>>> Good catch, I think you're right. I shouldn't have dismissed that
>>> branch due to "/* Direct swapin skipping swap cache & readahead */"
>>>
>>>> @Baolin please correct me if I got it wrong :)
>>>>
>>>> folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() itself doesn't filter shmem out either; it
>>>> only allocates the memcg list_lru metadata for deferred_split_lru:
>>>>
>>>> int folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(struct folio *folio)
>>>> {
>>>> 	if (mem_cgroup_disabled())
>>>> 		return 0;
>>>> 	return folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(folio, &deferred_split_lru, GFP_KERNEL);
>>>> }
>>>>
>>>> Since deferred_split_lru only queues anon large folios, doing this for
>>>> shmem swapin doesn't buy us anything :)
>>>
>>> Yes, agreed. I don't think it's a big deal / show stopper in terms of
>>> user-visible effect, but of course still worth fixing.
>>>
>>> I'll send a follow-up patch.
>>
>> Thanks.
>>
>> Looks like this has already landed in mm-stable. If you're okay with it,
>> I can send the follow-up.
>>
>> From: Lance Yang <lance.yang@linux.dev>
>> Date: Tue, 9 Jun 2026 10:56:45 +0800
>> Subject: [PATCH] mm: prepare deferred split metadata only for anon swapin
>>
>> __swap_cache_alloc() prepares deferred split metadata for large swapcache
>> folios.
>>
>> That also covers shmem swapin, because shmem_swap_alloc_folio() can call
>> swapin_sync() with a large order[1]. But shmem folios are not queued on
>> the deferred split queue, so preparing the metadata doesn't buy us
>> anything there.
>>
>> So let's limit it to anon swapin.
>>
>> [1] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260601132135.14272-1-lance.yang@linux.dev/
>>
>> Signed-off-by: Lance Yang <lance.yang@linux.dev>
>> ---
>>   mm/swap_state.c | 3 ++-
>>   1 file changed, 2 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)
>>
>> diff --git a/mm/swap_state.c b/mm/swap_state.c
>> index 9c3a5cf99778..7adac957c2b8 100644
>> --- a/mm/swap_state.c
>> +++ b/mm/swap_state.c
>> @@ -465,7 +465,8 @@ static struct folio *__swap_cache_alloc(struct swap_cluster_info *ci,
>>   		return ERR_PTR(-ENOMEM);
>>   	}
>>
>> -	if (order > 1 && folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
>> +	if (order > 1 && vma && vma_is_anonymous(vma) &&
> 
> A folio can be anon for a non-shmem file-backed VMA though?
> E.g. MAP_PRIVATE-mapped file-backed mappings?

Ah, good point! vma_is_anonymous() is too strong here ...

Maybe use !vma_is_shmem(vma) instead?

> Not sure if that's something that'd be a factor here/meaningful though.
> 
>> +	    folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
>>   		spin_lock(&ci->lock);
>>   		__swap_cache_do_del_folio(ci, folio, entry, shadow);
>>   		spin_unlock(&ci->lock);
>> --
>> 2.39.3 (Apple Git-146)
> 
> Cheers, Lorenzo
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes
Lorenzo Stoakes Inline Review Reviewed-by Neutral LLM
The reviewer pointed out that the current implementation of folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() does not filter shmem swapin, which is not a problem because shmem folios are not queued on the deferred split queue. However, they noted that a folio can be anon for a non-shmem file-backed VMA, and suggested adding vma_is_anonymous(vmf->vma) to the condition.
Show original comment 
On Tue, Jun 09, 2026 at 11:20:58AM +0800, Lance Yang wrote:
>
> On Mon, Jun 01, 2026 at 02:17:28PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> >On Mon, Jun 01, 2026 at 09:21:35PM +0800, Lance Yang wrote:
> >>
> >> On Wed, May 27, 2026 at 04:45:16PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> >> [...]
> >> >diff --git a/mm/swap_state.c b/mm/swap_state.c
> >> >index 04f5ce992401..9c3a5cf99778 100644
> >> >--- a/mm/swap_state.c
> >> >+++ b/mm/swap_state.c
> >> >@@ -465,6 +465,16 @@ static struct folio *__swap_cache_alloc(struct swap_cluster_info *ci,
> >> > 		return ERR_PTR(-ENOMEM);
> >> > 	}
> >> >
> >>
> >> Shouldn't this be limited to anon swapin?
> >>
> >> e.g. vmf && vma_is_anonymous(vmf->vma)
> >>
> >> >+	if (order > 1 && folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {

> >> get here too when handling a large swap entry:

> >>   shmem_swapin_folio()
> >>     shmem_swap_alloc_folio()
> >>       swapin_sync()
> >>         swap_cache_alloc_folio()
> >>           __swap_cache_alloc()
> >>             folio_memcg_alloc_deferred()
> >
> >Good catch, I think you're right. I shouldn't have dismissed that
> >branch due to "/* Direct swapin skipping swap cache & readahead */"
> >
> >> @Baolin please correct me if I got it wrong :)
> >>
> >> folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() itself doesn't filter shmem out either; it
> >> only allocates the memcg list_lru metadata for deferred_split_lru:
> >>

> >> {
> >> 	if (mem_cgroup_disabled())
> >> 		return 0;
> >> 	return folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(folio, &deferred_split_lru, GFP_KERNEL);
> >> }
> >>
> >> Since deferred_split_lru only queues anon large folios, doing this for
> >> shmem swapin doesn't buy us anything :)
> >
> >Yes, agreed. I don't think it's a big deal / show stopper in terms of
> >user-visible effect, but of course still worth fixing.
> >
> >I'll send a follow-up patch.
>
> Thanks.
>
> Looks like this has already landed in mm-stable. If you're okay with it,
> I can send the follow-up.
>
> From: Lance Yang <lance.yang@linux.dev>
> Date: Tue, 9 Jun 2026 10:56:45 +0800
> Subject: [PATCH] mm: prepare deferred split metadata only for anon swapin
>
> __swap_cache_alloc() prepares deferred split metadata for large swapcache
> folios.
>
> That also covers shmem swapin, because shmem_swap_alloc_folio() can call
> swapin_sync() with a large order[1]. But shmem folios are not queued on
> the deferred split queue, so preparing the metadata doesn't buy us
> anything there.
>
> So let's limit it to anon swapin.
>
> [1] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260601132135.14272-1-lance.yang@linux.dev/
>
> Signed-off-by: Lance Yang <lance.yang@linux.dev>
> ---
>  mm/swap_state.c | 3 ++-
>  1 file changed, 2 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)
>
> diff --git a/mm/swap_state.c b/mm/swap_state.c
> index 9c3a5cf99778..7adac957c2b8 100644
> --- a/mm/swap_state.c
> +++ b/mm/swap_state.c
> @@ -465,7 +465,8 @@ static struct folio *__swap_cache_alloc(struct swap_cluster_info *ci,
>  		return ERR_PTR(-ENOMEM);
>  	}
>
> -	if (order > 1 && folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
> +	if (order > 1 && vma && vma_is_anonymous(vma) &&

A folio can be anon for a non-shmem file-backed VMA though?
E.g. MAP_PRIVATE-mapped file-backed mappings?

Not sure if that's something that'd be a factor here/meaningful though.

> +	    folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
>  		spin_lock(&ci->lock);
>  		__swap_cache_do_del_folio(ci, folio, entry, shadow);

JP Kobryn

8 items
Patches Submitted (3)
[PATCH v2] mm/lruvec: trace LRU add drains Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies

This patch adds a new tracepoint called mm_lru_add_drain to report when an LRU add batch is drained, including the CPU and number of folios added. This allows for better tracing and attribution of full drains versus partial drains, which can help identify sources of contention on the LRU lock. The patch introduces a new function lru_add_drain_cpu that calls the tracepoint when draining an LRU add batch. The change is made to improve debugging and performance analysis capabilities in Linux.

Last comment 2026-06-09 [PATCH] mm/lruvec: trace LRU add drains and drain-all queuing Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch adds two new tracepoints to the Linux kernel's memory management (mm) subsystem: mm_lru_add_drain and mm_lru_drain_all_queue. The first tracepoint reports when an LRU add batch is drained, allowing tracing of partial drains and attribution to calling stacks. The second tracepoint captures when per-CPU drain work is queued for remote drain operations, including the requester stack and target CPU. These additions improve debugging capabilities by providing more detailed information about memory management events.

4 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Barry Song ↳ replying to JP Kobryn Reviewed-by Neutral LLM
Reviewer Barry Song questioned whether tracing each CPU individually is necessary, suggesting that tracing the entire __lru_add_drain_all() invocation would be sufficient, and also asked about tracing this_gen and lru_drain_gen.
Show original comment 
Do you need tracing on each CPU individually, or is tracing the
entire __lru_add_drain_all() invocation sufficient?

Do you also need this_gen and lru_drain_gen to be traced?

By the way, I'm not sure drain_all_queue is the best name here.
Why not simply use add_drain_all()? It would match the existing
function name better.

Best Regards
Barry
View on lore ↗
Signals: questioning, request for clarification
JP Kobryn (author) ↳ replying to Barry Song Needs Work LLM
Author agrees that using the mm_lru_add_drain tracepoint to show kworker stacks is sufficient and no further changes are needed. Author acknowledged that trace parameters for mm_lru_drain_all_queue do not provide meaningful information about who is making requests to drain all, but plans to move the trace call earlier in the function to still capture requestors.
Show original comment 
I think the latter would be fine. The remote work will invoke the
mm_lru_add_drain tracepoint, which will show up as kworker stacks. Since
the event already has the CPU, we could see where queued drains actually
ran.

---

As trace parameters, I don't think they give meaningful info on who is
making requests to drain all. But since I'm going to move the trace call
from within the CPU loop to earlier in the function we can still see
requestors even if the function exits early because a parallel drain
generation already satisfied the request.
View on lore ↗
Signals: agreement, no further action planned, acknowledged a limitation
JP Kobryn (author) Needs Work LLM
Author considered the feedback that a tracepoint may not be needed for the mm_lru_drain_all_queue event, as other probes can be installed on function invocation and read the single argument; agreed to drop this from v2 and keep the single mm_lru_add_drain tracepoint.
Show original comment 
Actually if it's just a single invocation and the only event data is the
force flag, a tracepoint may not even be needed. Other probes can be
installed on function invocation and read the single argument. I can
drop this from v2 and keep the single mm_lru_add_drain tracepoint.
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledged a fix is needed
Shakeel Butt ↳ replying to JP Kobryn Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer Shakeel Butt noted that tracing only lru_add_drain_cpu() would not provide information on the requester of the drain, and suggested instead tracing the whole __lru_add_drain_all() function to capture this context.
Show original comment 
No we do want to trace the callers requesting to drain from all the CPUs. If you
trace just lru_add_drain_cpu() then you will only see that the drain is
requested for a given CPU but no information on the requester. 

Also as Barry said, I think single trace for whole __lru_add_drain_all() is good
enough.
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes
[PATCH v4] mm/lruvec: trace LRU add drains and drain-all requests Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies

This patch adds two new tracepoints to the Linux kernel's mm/lruvec module: mm_lru_add_drain and mm_lru_add_drain_all. The first tracepoint reports when an LRU add batch is drained, including the CPU and number of folios in the batch. This allows tracing to distinguish between full drains and partial drains. The second tracepoint captures callers of __lru_add_drain_all and whether they set the force flag for all CPUs. These additions help attribute LRU lock contention to specific callers and improve debugging capabilities.

Discussions / RFCs (0)
No activity

This patch adds two new tracepoints to the Linux kernel's memory management (mm) subsystem: mm_lru_add_drain and mm_lru_drain_all_queue. The first tracepoint reports when an LRU add batch is drained, allowing tracing of partial drains and attribution to calling stacks. The second tracepoint captures when per-CPU drain work is queued for remote drain operations, including the requester stack and target CPU. These additions improve debugging capabilities by providing more detailed information about memory management events.

JP Kobryn (author) ↳ replying to Shakeel Butt Neutral LLM
Author questioned the necessity of adding a dedicated tracepoint, suggesting that existing tracing mechanisms like fentry or kprobe could be used instead.

Right, but couldn't that already be done with fentry or kprobe? If we

JP Kobryn (author) Needs Work LLM

Signals: acknowledged need for fix, agreed to restructure
Shakeel Butt ↳ replying to JP Kobryn Neutral LLM
Reviewer noted that kprobes and fentries may be inlined by the compiler, potentially making them unreliable for tracing purposes.
Show original comment 
Thanks. BTW even without inline keyword, compiler can still decide to inline
a function, so kprobe/fentry are not always reliable.

Signals: potential issue, unreliability

The first tracepoint reports when an LRU add batch is drained, allowing tracing of partial drains and attribution to calling stacks. Last comment 2026-06-10 Re: [PATCH] mm/lruvec: trace LRU add drains and drain-all queuing Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch adds a new tracepoint to track LRU add drains and drain-all queuing in the Linux kernel's memory management system, specifically targeting the lruvec module. The motivation behind this change is to provide more detailed tracing capabilities without relying on existing mechanisms like fentry or kprobe. This allows for better debugging and analysis of memory-related issues.

Re: [PATCH] mm/swap_state: remove unnecessary lru_add_drain() from readahead Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies

Kiryl Shutsemau

14 items
Patches Submitted (7)
[PATCH v2 0/3] arm64: cross-CPU NMI via SDEI Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies

This patch series adds support for cross-CPU NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) on arm64 using the SDEI (System Description Event Infrastructure) mechanism. This is necessary because current mechanisms, such as pseudo-NMI and FEAT_NMI, have significant performance costs or are not available in deployed silicon. The new SDEI-based approach allows for interrupting a CPU with its interrupts locally masked without impacting performance, enabling more accurate debugging and observability features like backtraces and crash dumps.

[PATCH v2 1/3] firmware: arm_sdei: add SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL support Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies

This patch adds a new function called sdei_event_signal() to the Linux kernel's firmware/arm_sdei.c file. This function is a thin wrapper over the SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL call, which signals the software-signalled event (event 0) to another processor element (PE). The function takes two parameters: event_num and mpidr, where mpidr is the target PE's MPIDR affinity. The patch also updates the include/linux/arm_sdei.h file to add a declaration for sdei_event_signal(), and adds a new enum value SDEI_1_0_FN_SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL to the include/uapi/linux/arm_sdei.h file. This change is intended to make it safe to call from NMI or crash context, as it does not take any locks.

[PATCH v2 2/3] drivers/firmware: add SDEI cross-CPU NMI service for arm64 Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies

This patch adds a feature to deliver NMI-like events to interrupt-masked CPUs on arm64 architecture using the SDEI software-signalled event mechanism, which is more efficient than the pseudo-NMI hot-path cost. The first user of this feature is the arch_trigger_cpumask_backtrace() function, which can now reach masked CPUs without relying on IPIs. A future watchdog backend will also be able to use this feature. The patch introduces a new SDEI cross-CPU NMI service and modifies several files to support it.

[PATCH v2 3/3] arm64: escalate smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI as a last resort Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies

This patch adds an escalation mechanism to the smp_send_stop() function on arm64 architecture, which is triggered when a CPU is wedged and ignores interrupts. The escalation sends an SDEI NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) as a last resort to stop the CPU. This ensures that the system can still perform a kdump or reboot even if one of the CPUs is stuck. The patch introduces a new function arm64_nmi_cpu_stop() in smp.c, which handles the SDEI NMI and distinguishes between a kdump crash stop and a plain stop. The CPU acknowledges the stop request by marking itself offline.

Ongoing Submitted 2026-06-03 Last comment 2026-06-05 ⏱ No recent activity [PATCH 3/4] arm64: wire SDEI NMI into the hardlockup watchdog Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch adds support for the SDEI (System Description Event Interface) NMI to the hardlockup watchdog on arm64 architectures. It introduces a new driver that uses SDEI to deliver cross-CPU NMIs, which can be used by various kernel features such as arch_trigger_cpumask_backtrace and the hardlockup watchdog backend. The patch also selects HAVE_HARDLOCKUP_DETECTOR_ARCH in Kconfig to enable this feature. If the firmware provides SDEI, it is used; otherwise, a perf-NMI counter fallback is employed.

Doug Anderson ↳ replying to Kiryl Shutsemau Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the hardlockup watchdog backend is selected based on whether SDEI firmware is provided, but the fallback to a perf-NMI counter should be reconsidered because it may not work correctly in all cases. Reviewer Doug Anderson questioned the necessity of the patch, suggesting that it duplicates functionality already present in the existing buddy hardlockup detector and patches #1 and #2 in the series.
Show original comment 
Hi,

On Wed, Jun 3, 2026 at 7:36 AM Kiryl Shutsemau <kirill@shutemov.name> wrote:

---

I'm a little confused about this patch. We already have a buddy
hardlockup detector using the hrtimer, and it's even been improved
recently to trigger in a smaller time bound. It looks as if you're
duplicating bits of the perf and buddy detector here?

I don't think you need this patch at all. The existing buddy detector
+ patches #1 and #2 in your series should be sufficient.

Did I misunderstand?

-Doug
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes, duplicated functionality, unnecessary patch
Kiryl Shutsemau (author) ↳ replying to Doug Anderson Needs Work LLM
Author acknowledged that the patch adds boot-time source selection, which is out of scope for this patchset, and agreed to drop it in v2.
Show original comment 
You're mostly right.

Buddy + #2 covers the console case (the remote branch triggers the
culprit's backtrace, which #2 makes deliverable), and #4 gets the wedged
CPU's registers into the vmcore.

The one thing this patch adds that a config can't is boot-time source
selection: PERF-compiled kernels have no detector on a pseudo_nmi=0
boot, and PREFER_BUDDY costs the pseudo-NMI machines perf
self-detection. But that's arguably out of scope for the patchset.

I'll drop this patch in v2 and run PREFER_BUDDY here. If a runtime
perf->buddy fallback ever materializes, the gap closes entirely.

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

Signals: acknowledged a limitation, agreed to restructure
Doug Anderson ↳ replying to Kiryl Shutsemau Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer noted that the SDEI NMI handler is not properly synchronized, and requested the use of a spinlock to protect access to the hrtimer Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the hardlockup watchdog backend, when using SDEI NMI, has a limitation where it cannot detect simultaneous lockups of all CPUs and suggested an alternative solution to use a hardware watchdog timer's 'bark' interrupt as a NMI source.
Show original comment 
Hi,

On Fri, Jun 5, 2026 at 2:12 PM Kiryl Shutsemau <kirill@shutemov.name> wrote:

---

Sure. If you're interested in trying to make pref vs. buddy coexist,
that should be done in a platform-agnostic way. Feel free to post
patches for that. I know we discussed this previously. Ah, here they
are:

https://lore.kernel.org/r/20250916145122.416128-1-wangjinchao600@gmail.com

I think those got bikeshedded to death and nobody cared enough to keep pushing.

FWIW, my belief is that the buddy detector is superior in every way
except that it can't detect when all CPUs lock up simultaneously.
...though I wonder if a nicer way to solve the "all CPUs locked up" is
to just NMI-enable the "bark" interrupt of a hardware watchdog timer.
That ought to be quite easy...

-Doug

Signals: synchronization issue, requested changes
Ongoing Submitted 2026-06-03 Last comment 2026-06-09 ⏱ No recent activity [PATCH 4/4] arm64: route crash_smp_send_stop() last resort through SDEI Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch adds the SDEI (System Description Event Interface) as a last resort to stop CPUs in an arm64 system when normal methods fail. It signals event 0 at still-online CPUs, which runs crash_save_cpu() on the wedged context and parks them. This approach is chosen because an SDEI-stopped CPU never completes its event, making it less recoverable than a normal stop. The patch adds functionality to handle this scenario and includes changes to various kernel files.

3 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Doug Anderson ↳ replying to Kiryl Shutsemau Inline Review Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the SDEI cross-CPU NMI handler captures crash_save_cpu() state from the wedged context before parking the CPU, but this may not be sufficient to ensure recoverability since an SDEI-stopped CPU never completes its event. Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the patch adds SDEI as a last resort for crash_smp_send_stop(), but not for regular smp_send_stop(), and suggested modifying smp_send_stop() to fall back to SDEI if sending the NMI failed, instead of adding this separate path. Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that passing a cpumask_t to the stack in crash_smp_send_stop() can lead to issues due to its potential size under certain CONFIG options, and suggested avoiding this by defining it as static.
Show original comment 
Hi,

On Wed, Jun 3, 2026 at 7:36 AM Kiryl Shutsemau <kirill@shutemov.name> wrote:

---

It feels weird to me that you're adding SDEI for "crash stop" but not
for regular "stop". It feels like you should modify smp_send_stop() to
fall back to SDEI if sending the NMI failed, instead of adding this
separate path.

---

The above will probably get you a yell. Putting "cpumask_t" on the
stack is a no-no since it can be quite large under certain CONFIG
options. This is why it's nearly always defined as "static".

-Doug

Signals: recoverability, concerns about SDEI, requested changes

The author acknowledged a concern about the function being non-static and promised to make it static in v2, indicating that a fix is planned.
Show original comment 
Fair. A wedged CPU ignores the reboot-path stop just the same, and the
escalation logic already lives in smp.c, so I'll restructure in v2.

One thing to sort out there: this patch parks the stopped CPU inside
its SDEI handler without completing the event, which is fine for the
crash case (nothing expects the CPU back before reset), but a generic
stop path probably wants SDEI_EVENT_COMPLETE_AND_RESUME into a parking
stub instead, so that e.g. a regular kexec can bring all CPUs back up
in the new kernel. I'll look into that as part of the rework.

---

Yes -- falls out of the above. I will look into this.

Maybe pull the save/offline/park body into a shared helper that both the
IPI handler and the SDEI handler call.

---

Doh! Will make it static in v2 -- safe here since the path is serialized
by the crash_stop guard.

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

Kiryl Shutsemau (author) Needs Work LLM
Author acknowledged that crash kernel cannot reuse wedged CPU and explained why C&R alone is insufficient, but admitted to still struggling with implementing the necessary steps to get the CPU to CPU_OFF.
Show original comment 
Regular kexec takes different path and offlines CPU normally. So the
next kernel can start them. But crash kernel cannot re-use wedged CPU.

C&R alone doesn't buy us anything. We need to get the CPU to CPU_OFF.

I am trying to do this, but so far no luck. Crash kernel fails to start
at all if try to do C&R and then CPU_OFF. C&R alone works, but CPU is
still unreachable by the next kernel, as expected.

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

Signals: acknowledging a limitation, admitting difficulty in implementation
Ongoing Submitted 2026-05-29 Last comment 2026-06-02 ⏱ No recent activity [PATCH 0/6] userfaultfd/pagemap: pre-existing fixes 6 patches
Show 6 individual patches
  • [1/6] fs/proc/task_mmu: fix make_uffd_wp_huge_pte() prot-update race Neutral Submitted 2026-05-29Last comment 2026-06-02 Lorenzo Stoakes Dev Jain
  • [2/6] fs/proc/task_mmu: use huge_page_size() in pagemap_scan_hugetlb_entry() Neutral Submitted 2026-05-29Last comment 2026-06-02 Lorenzo Stoakes Dev Jain
  • [3/6] fs/proc/task_mmu: fix hugetlb self-deadlock in pagemap_scan_pte_hole() Submitted 2026-05-29
  • [4/6] mm/huge_memory: preserve pmd_swp_uffd_wp on device-private PMD downgrade Neutral Submitted 2026-05-29Last comment 2026-06-01 Balbir Singh
  • [5/6] userfaultfd: gate must_wait writability check on pte_present() Neutral Submitted 2026-05-29Last comment 2026-06-02 Lorenzo Stoakes Mike Rapoport
  • [6/6] userfaultfd: build __VMA_UFFD_FLAGS from config-gated masks Neutral Submitted 2026-05-29Last comment 2026-06-02 Lorenzo Stoakes Mike Rapoport
Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch series addresses six pre-existing bugs in the userfaultfd and pagemap code, which were previously carried as part of a larger feature series. The issues include missing updates to hardware Dirty/Accessed bits, incorrect handling of gigantic hugetlb pages, self-deadlocks when scanning unpopulated hugetlb ranges, loss of uffd-wp markers during PMD permission downgrades, and out-of-bounds writes in the vma_flags_t structure. Each bug is fixed independently and applies to the mm-new code directly, with some patches also carrying Cc: stable@ for backporting. The approach taken involves correcting specific functions and data structures to ensure accurate and safe handling of userfaultfd and pagemap operations.

Balbir Singh Inline Review Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer noted that change_non_present_huge_pmd() does not carry pmd_swp_uffd_wp() across when rewriting a writable device-private PMD swap entry into a readable one, which can cause a UFFD_WP-marked THP to lose the bit and bypass the trap on swap-in. The reviewer suggested mirroring the behavior of PTE-level change_softleaf_pte(), matching what copy_huge_pmd() does for the fork path.
Show original comment 
On 5/30/26 03:23, Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) wrote:
> change_non_present_huge_pmd() rewrites a writable device-private PMD
> swap entry into a readable one without carrying pmd_swp_uffd_wp()
> across. The PTE-level change_softleaf_pte() does this correctly;
> mirror that here, matching what copy_huge_pmd() does for the fork
> path. Without the carry, a plain mprotect() over a UFFD_WP-marked
> device-private THP strips the bit and the trap is bypassed on
> swap-in.
> 
> Fixes: 368076f52ebe ("mm/huge_memory: add device-private THP support to PMD operations")
> Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
> Reported-by: Sashiko AI review <sashiko-bot@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau <kas@kernel.org>
> ---
>  mm/huge_memory.c | 2 ++
>  1 file changed, 2 insertions(+)
> 
> diff --git a/mm/huge_memory.c b/mm/huge_memory.c
> index 42b86e8ab7c0..b7c895b1d366 100644
> --- a/mm/huge_memory.c
> +++ b/mm/huge_memory.c
> @@ -2663,6 +2663,8 @@ static void change_non_present_huge_pmd(struct mm_struct *mm,
>  	} else if (softleaf_is_device_private_write(entry)) {
>  		entry = make_readable_device_private_entry(swp_offset(entry));
>  		newpmd = swp_entry_to_pmd(entry);
> +		if (pmd_swp_uffd_wp(*pmd))
> +			newpmd = pmd_swp_mkuffd_wp(newpmd);
>  	} else {
>  		newpmd = *pmd;
>  	}

Reviewed-by: Balbir Singh <balbirs@nvidia.com>

Balbir

---

On Tue, Jun 02, 2026 at 11:32:54AM +0300, Mike Rapoport wrote:
> On Fri, May 29, 2026 at 06:23:30PM +0100, Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) wrote:
> > The VMA flags bitmap is a single word today: NUM_VMA_FLAG_BITS is
> > BITS_PER_LONG, so on 32-bit vma_flags_t holds only 32 bits. (The bitmap
> > type exists so this can grow past BITS_PER_LONG later; until it does,
> > anything declared above the first word is out of range on 32-bit.) The bit
> > enum nevertheless declares some bits unconditionally above BITS_PER_LONG --
> > VMA_UFFD_MINOR_BIT is 41, with VM_UFFD_MINOR == VM_NONE on 32-bit so no VMA
> > actually carries the bit.
> > 
> > __VMA_UFFD_FLAGS feeds VMA_UFFD_MINOR_BIT to mk_vma_flags() unconditionally.
> > On 32-bit that becomes __set_bit(41, &one_long), a write one word past the
> > end of the single-word bitmap. The compiler folds the out-of-bounds store
> > with wraparound (1UL << (41 % 32) == bit 9) into the first word; bit 9 is
> > already in __VMA_UFFD_FLAGS so the mask happens to come out right today, but
> > it is an out-of-bounds write all the same, and any high-numbered bit whose
> > mod-BITS_PER_LONG position is otherwise unused would silently OR an extra
> > bit into the mask.
> > 
> > Rather than feed bit numbers that may not exist on the current build to
> > mk_vma_flags(), build the mask from whole per-mode masks that collapse to
> > EMPTY_VMA_FLAGS when their feature is unavailable. Add
> > mk_vma_flags_from_masks() for that, and define VMA_UFFD_MISSING / _WP /
> > _MINOR alongside the VM_UFFD_* flags, gating VMA_UFFD_MINOR on the same
> > config as VM_UFFD_MINOR (which implies 64BIT, where bit 41 fits). An
> > out-of-range bit is then never materialised, on any arch, and the in-range
> > fast path stays a compile-time constant.
> > 
> > Fixes: 9ea35a25d51b ("mm: introduce VMA flags bitmap type")
> > Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
> > Reported-by: Sashiko AI review <sashiko-bot@kernel.org>
> > Suggested-by: Lorenzo Stoakes <ljs@kernel.org>
> > Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau <kas@kernel.org>
> > Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-8
> 
> Can you ask claude to produce more concise changelogs and better split it
> to paragraphs?

Will do!

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

---

On Fri, May 29, 2026 at 05:34:48PM -0700, Andrew Morton wrote:
> On Fri, 29 May 2026 18:23:24 +0100 "Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta)" <kas@kernel.org> wrote:

> > All six were flagged by the Sashiko AI review of the RWP series and
> > carry Reported-by: Sashiko AI review <sashiko-bot@kernel.org>. They are

> And...  this made Sashiko point at other stuff:
> 	https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260529172331.356655-1-kas@kernel.org
>

> would a full scan of mm/ tell us?  Gulp.

Yeah, but I also kinda wish it didn't do this :) It's similar to sending a
series, and somebody replying saying 'well this is totally unrelated but <blah
blah blah>' and expecting a reply :>)

You'd want that person to just submit a patch for the issue or a [DISCUSSION] or
[BUG] thread or something, but interrupting, possibly delaying + taking people's
(usually very limited) time on something unrelated is just unhelpful I think.

I'd prefer Sashiko had a passive mode that checked for existing stuff elsewhere,
and it saved the tokens doing it for a new series :)

Just my 2 pence!

Cheers, Lorenzo

---

On Fri, May 29, 2026 at 06:23:25PM +0100, Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) wrote:
> make_uffd_wp_huge_pte() arms the UFFD_WP bit on a present HugeTLB PTE by
> calling huge_ptep_modify_prot_commit() with a ptent snapshot that was
> fetched without the corresponding huge_ptep_modify_prot_start().

The
> start helper is what atomically clears the entry so the kernel-owned
> snapshot stays consistent until the commit; without it, the hardware
> may set Dirty or Accessed in the live PTE between the original read
> and the commit, and huge_ptep_modify_prot_commit() (whose generic
> implementation just calls set_huge_pte_at()) then writes the stale
> snapshot back over the live hardware bits, losing the update.

Very pedantic nit - this is a mammoth paragraph :P maybe add a linebreak
where I did above?

>
> The non-hugetlb sibling make_uffd_wp_pte() does this correctly via
> ptep_modify_prot_start() / ptep_modify_prot_commit(). Mirror that
> pattern for the present-PTE branch. The migration case stays as-is --
> migration entries are non-present, so there's no hardware update to
> race against.
>
> Fixes: 52526ca7fdb9 ("fs/proc/task_mmu: implement IOCTL to get and optionally clear info about PTEs")

I wonder if better as a separate patch? Not sure what Andrew thinks about
it, I do recall him complaining about separate-hotfixes-as-part-of-a-series :>)

> Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
> Reported-by: Sashiko AI review <sashiko-bot@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau <kas@kernel.org>

LGTM, so:

Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes <ljs@kernel.org>

> ---
>  fs/proc/task_mmu.c | 12 ++++++++----
>  1 file changed, 8 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)
>
> diff --git a/fs/proc/task_mmu.c b/fs/proc/task_mmu.c
> index 1e3a15bf46f4..e21a38ac745b 100644
> --- a/fs/proc/task_mmu.c
> +++ b/fs/proc/task_mmu.c
> @@ -2610,12 +2610,16 @@ static void make_uffd_wp_huge_pte(struct vm_area_struct *vma,
>  	if (softleaf_is_hwpoison(entry) || softleaf_is_marker(entry))
>  		return;
>
> -	if (softleaf_is_migration(entry))
> +	if (softleaf_is_migration(entry)) {
>  		set_huge_pte_at(vma->vm_mm, addr, ptep,
>  				pte_swp_mkuffd_wp(ptent), psize);
> -	else
> -		huge_ptep_modify_prot_commit(vma, addr, ptep, ptent,
> -					     huge_pte_mkuffd_wp(ptent));
> +	} else {
> +		pte_t old_pte, new_pte;
> +
> +		old_pte = huge_ptep_modify_prot_start(vma, addr, ptep);
> +		new_pte = huge_pte_mkuffd_wp(old_pte);
> +		huge_ptep_modify_prot_commit(vma, addr, ptep, old_pte, new_pte);
> +	}
>  }
>  #endif /* CONFIG_HUGETLB_PAGE */
>
> --
> 2.54.0
>

Cheers, Lorenzo

---

On Mon, Jun 01, 2026 at 06:55:43PM +0100, Lorenzo Stoakes wrote:
> On Fri, May 29, 2026 at 06:23:25PM +0100, Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) wrote:
> > Fixes: 52526ca7fdb9 ("fs/proc/task_mmu: implement IOCTL to get and optionally clear info about PTEs")
>
> I wonder if better as a separate patch? Not sure what Andrew thinks about
> it, I do recall him complaining about separate-hotfixes-as-part-of-a-series :>)

Ahhh it's a series of Fixes: :) ok ignore this then!

Cheers, Lorenzo

---

On Fri, May 29, 2026 at 06:23:26PM +0100, Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) wrote:
> The partial-page check compares against HPAGE_SIZE (PMD_SIZE), which
> is wrong for gigantic hugetlb hstates (e.g. 1G). The walker hands the
> callback a huge_page_size()-sized range, never start + HPAGE_SIZE, so
> the comparison always declares it partial and aborts the WP. Compare
> against the actual hstate's page size.
>
> Fixes: 52526ca7fdb9 ("fs/proc/task_mmu: implement IOCTL to get and optionally clear info about PTEs")
> Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
> Reported-by: Sashiko AI review <sashiko-bot@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau <kas@kernel.org>

LGTM, so:

Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes <ljs@kernel.org>

> ---
>  fs/proc/task_mmu.c | 2 +-
>  1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 1 deletion(-)
>
> diff --git a/fs/proc/task_mmu.c b/fs/proc/task_mmu.c
> index e21a38ac745b..1489c67e88f7 100644
> --- a/fs/proc/task_mmu.c
> +++ b/fs/proc/task_mmu.c
> @@ -2960,7 +2960,7 @@ static int pagemap_scan_hugetlb_entry(pte_t *ptep, unsigned long hmask,
>  	if (~categories & PAGE_IS_WRITTEN)
>  		goto out_unlock;
>
> -	if (end != start + HPAGE_SIZE) {
> +	if (end != start + huge_page_size(hstate_vma(vma))) {
>  		/* Partial HugeTLB page WP isn't possible. */
>  		pagemap_scan_backout_range(p, start, end);
>  		p->arg.walk_end = start;
> --
> 2.54.0
>

---

On Fri, May 29, 2026 at 06:23:29PM +0100, Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) wrote:
> userfaultfd_must_wait() and userfaultfd_huge_must_wait() read the PTE
> without taking the page table lock and then apply pte_write() /
> huge_pte_write() to it. Those accessors decode bits from the present
> encoding only; on a swap or migration entry they read the offset bits
> that happen to share the same position and return an undefined result.
>
> The intent of the check is "is this fault still WP-blocked?". A
> non-marker swap entry means the page is in transit -- the userfault
> context the original fault delivered against is no longer the same,
> and the swap-in or migration completion path will re-deliver a fresh
> fault if userspace still needs to handle it. Worst case under the
> current code the garbage write bit says "wait", and the thread stays
> asleep until a UFFDIO_WAKE that may never arrive.
>
> Gate the writability check on pte_present() so the lockless re-check
> only inspects present-PTE bits when the entry is actually present.
> The non-present, non-marker case returns "don't wait" and lets the
> fault path retry.
>
> Fixes: 369cd2121be4 ("userfaultfd: hugetlbfs: userfaultfd_huge_must_wait for hugepmd ranges")
> Fixes: 63b2d4174c4a ("userfaultfd: wp: add the writeprotect API to userfaultfd ioctl")
> Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
> Reported-by: Sashiko AI review <sashiko-bot@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau <kas@kernel.org>

One tiny nit is maybe could mention softleaf :P but it's not important!

LGTM, so:

Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes <ljs@kernel.org>

> ---
>  mm/userfaultfd.c | 20 ++++++++++++++++++++
>  1 file changed, 20 insertions(+)
>
> diff --git a/mm/userfaultfd.c b/mm/userfaultfd.c
> index 35b206cc9aa6..f6d2a1c67019 100644
> --- a/mm/userfaultfd.c
> +++ b/mm/userfaultfd.c
> @@ -2535,6 +2535,15 @@ static inline bool userfaultfd_huge_must_wait(struct userfaultfd_ctx *ctx,
>  	/* UFFD PTE markers require userspace to resolve the fault. */
>  	if (pte_is_uffd_marker(pte))
>  		return true;
> +	/*
> +	 * Concurrent migration may have replaced the present PTE with a
> +	 * non-marker swap entry between fault delivery and this lockless
> +	 * re-check. huge_pte_write() on a swap entry decodes random offset
> +	 * bits, so gate it on pte_present(). The migration completion path
> +	 * will re-deliver the fault if it still needs userspace.
> +	 */
> +	if (!pte_present(pte))
> +		return false;
>  	/*
>  	 * If VMA has UFFD WP faults enabled and WP fault, wait for userspace to
>  	 * resolve the fault.
> @@ -2621,6 +2630,17 @@ static inline bool userfaultfd_must_wait(struct userfaultfd_ctx *ctx,
>  	/* UFFD PTE markers require userspace to resolve the fault. */
>  	if (pte_is_uffd_marker(ptent))
>  		goto out;
> +	/*
> +	 * Concurrent swap-out / migration may have replaced the present PTE
> +	 * with a non-marker swap entry between fault delivery and this
> +	 * lockless re-check. pte_write() on a swap entry decodes random
> +	 * offset bits, so gate it on pte_present(). The page-in path will
> +	 * re-deliver the fault if it still needs userspace.
> +	 */
> +	if (!pte_present(ptent)) {
> +		ret = false;
> +		goto out;
> +	}
>  	/*
>  	 * If VMA has UFFD WP faults enabled and WP fault, wait for userspace to
>  	 * resolve the fault.
> --
> 2.54.0
>

---

On Fri, May 29, 2026 at 06:23:30PM +0100, Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) wrote:
> The VMA flags bitmap is a single word today: NUM_VMA_FLAG_BITS is
> BITS_PER_LONG, so on 32-bit vma_flags_t holds only 32 bits. (The bitmap
> type exists so this can grow past BITS_PER_LONG later; until it does,
> anything declared above the first word is out of range on 32-bit.) The bit
> enum nevertheless declares some bits unconditionally above BITS_PER_LONG --
> VMA_UFFD_MINOR_BIT is 41, with VM_UFFD_MINOR == VM_NONE on 32-bit so no VMA
> actually carries the bit.
>
> __VMA_UFFD_FLAGS feeds VMA_UFFD_MINOR_BIT to mk_vma_flags() unconditionally.
> On 32-bit that becomes __set_bit(41, &one_long), a write one word past the
> end of the single-word bitmap. The compiler folds the out-of-bounds store
> with wraparound (1UL << (41 % 32) == bit 9) into the first word; bit 9 is
> already in __VMA_UFFD_FLAGS so the mask happens to come out right today, but
> it is an out-of-bounds write all the same, and any high-numbered bit whose
> mod-BITS_PER_LONG position is otherwise unused would silently OR an extra
> bit into the mask.
>
> Rather than feed bit numbers that may not exist on the current build to
> mk_vma_flags(), build the mask from whole per-mode masks that collapse to
> EMPTY_VMA_FLAGS when their feature is unavailable. Add
> mk_vma_flags_from_masks() for that, and define VMA_UFFD_MISSING / _WP /
> _MINOR alongside the VM_UFFD_* flags, gating VMA_UFFD_MINOR on the same
> config as VM_UFFD_MINOR (which implies 64BIT, where bit 41 fits). An
> out-of-range bit is then never materialised, on any arch, and the in-range
> fast path stays a compile-time constant.
>
> Fixes: 9ea35a25d51b ("mm: introduce VMA flags bitmap type")

Hmm, I think commit a06eb2f8279e ("mm/vma: convert
vma_modify_flags[_uffd]() to use vma_flags_t") is more appropriate isn't
it?

As that's the commit that added __VMA_UFFD_FLAGS :)

> Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
> Reported-by: Sashiko AI review <sashiko-bot@kernel.org>
> Suggested-by: Lorenzo Stoakes <ljs@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau <kas@kernel.org>

The change LGTM, so:

Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes <ljs@kernel.org>

> Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-8
> ---
>  include/linux/mm.h            | 39 +++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
>  include/linux/userfaultfd_k.h |  4 ++--
>  2 files changed, 41 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)
>
> diff --git a/include/linux/mm.h b/include/linux/mm.h
> index 0f2612a70fb1..485df9c2dbdd 100644
> --- a/include/linux/mm.h
> +++ b/include/linux/mm.h
> @@ -496,6 +496,21 @@ enum {
>  #else
>  #define VM_UFFD_MINOR	VM_NONE
>  #endif
> +
> +/*
> + * vma_flags_t masks for the userfaultfd VMA flags. VMA_UFFD_MINOR is gated on
> + * the same config as VM_UFFD_MINOR -- which implies 64BIT, where the bit fits
> + * -- so an out-of-range bit is never fed to mk_vma_flags() on a build whose
> + * bitmap cannot hold it.
> + */
> +#define VMA_UFFD_MISSING	mk_vma_flags(VMA_UFFD_MISSING_BIT)
> +#define VMA_UFFD_WP		mk_vma_flags(VMA_UFFD_WP_BIT)
> +#ifdef CONFIG_HAVE_ARCH_USERFAULTFD_MINOR
> +#define VMA_UFFD_MINOR		mk_vma_flags(VMA_UFFD_MINOR_BIT)
> +#else
> +#define VMA_UFFD_MINOR		EMPTY_VMA_FLAGS
> +#endif
> +
>  #ifdef CONFIG_64BIT
>  #define VM_ALLOW_ANY_UNCACHED	INIT_VM_FLAG(ALLOW_ANY_UNCACHED)
>  #define VM_SEALED		INIT_VM_FLAG(SEALED)
> @@ -1238,6 +1253,30 @@ static __always_inline void vma_flags_set_mask(vma_flags_t *flags,
>  #define vma_flags_set(flags, ...) \
>  	vma_flags_set_mask(flags, mk_vma_flags(__VA_ARGS__))
>
> +static __always_inline vma_flags_t __mk_vma_flags_from_masks(size_t count,
> +		const vma_flags_t *masks)
> +{
> +	vma_flags_t flags = EMPTY_VMA_FLAGS;
> +	size_t i;
> +
> +	for (i = 0; i < count; i++)
> +		vma_flags_set_mask(&flags, masks[i]);
> +	return flags;
> +}
> +
> +/*
> + * Combine pre-computed vma_flags_t masks into one value, e.g.:
> + *
> + * vma_flags_t flags = mk_vma_flags_from_masks(VMA_UFFD_WP, VMA_UFFD_MINOR);
> + *
> + * Unlike mk_vma_flags(), which takes bit numbers, this takes whole masks --
> + * each of which may be EMPTY_VMA_FLAGS when its feature is unavailable -- so a
> + * bit that does not exist on the current build is never materialised.
> + */
> +#define mk_vma_flags_from_masks(...)					\
> +	__mk_vma_flags_from_masks(COUNT_ARGS(__VA_ARGS__),		\
> +		(const vma_flags_t []){__VA_ARGS__})
> +
>  /* Clear all of the to-clear flags in flags, non-atomically. */
>  static __always_inline void vma_flags_clear_mask(vma_flags_t *flags,
>  		vma_flags_t to_clear)
> diff --git a/include/linux/userfaultfd_k.h b/include/linux/userfaultfd_k.h
> index 3ec8e1071673..68edac4dcd78 100644
> --- a/include/linux/userfaultfd_k.h
> +++ b/include/linux/userfaultfd_k.h
> @@ -23,8 +23,8 @@
>  /* The set of all possible UFFD-related VM flags. */
>  #define __VM_UFFD_FLAGS (VM_UFFD_MISSING | VM_UFFD_WP | VM_UFFD_MINOR)
>
> -#define __VMA_UFFD_FLAGS mk_vma_flags(VMA_UFFD_MISSING_BIT, VMA_UFFD_WP_BIT, \
> -				      VMA_UFFD_MINOR_BIT)
> +#define __VMA_UFFD_FLAGS mk_vma_flags_from_masks(VMA_UFFD_MISSING, VMA_UFFD_WP, \
> +						 VMA_UFFD_MINOR)
>
>  /*
>   * CAREFUL: Check include/uapi/asm-generic/fcntl.h when defining
> --
> 2.54.0
>
View on lore ↗
Signals: concern, suggestion
Dev Jain Inline Review Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
The reviewer noted that the make_uffd_wp_huge_pte() function does not properly handle huge page updates by calling huge_ptep_modify_prot_commit() without first calling huge_ptep_modify_prot_start(), which can lead to stale hardware updates being written back. The reviewer suggested mirroring the pattern used in the non-hugetlb sibling function, make_uffd_wp_pte().
Show original comment 
On 29/05/26 10:53 pm, Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) wrote:
> make_uffd_wp_huge_pte() arms the UFFD_WP bit on a present HugeTLB PTE by
> calling huge_ptep_modify_prot_commit() with a ptent snapshot that was
> fetched without the corresponding huge_ptep_modify_prot_start(). The
> start helper is what atomically clears the entry so the kernel-owned
> snapshot stays consistent until the commit; without it, the hardware
> may set Dirty or Accessed in the live PTE between the original read
> and the commit, and huge_ptep_modify_prot_commit() (whose generic
> implementation just calls set_huge_pte_at()) then writes the stale
> snapshot back over the live hardware bits, losing the update.
> 
> The non-hugetlb sibling make_uffd_wp_pte() does this correctly via
> ptep_modify_prot_start() / ptep_modify_prot_commit(). Mirror that
> pattern for the present-PTE branch. The migration case stays as-is --
> migration entries are non-present, so there's no hardware update to
> race against.
> 
> Fixes: 52526ca7fdb9 ("fs/proc/task_mmu: implement IOCTL to get and optionally clear info about PTEs")
> Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
> Reported-by: Sashiko AI review <sashiko-bot@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau <kas@kernel.org>
> ---

LGTM

Reviewed-by: Dev Jain <dev.jain@arm.com>


>  fs/proc/task_mmu.c | 12 ++++++++----
>  1 file changed, 8 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/proc/task_mmu.c b/fs/proc/task_mmu.c
> index 1e3a15bf46f4..e21a38ac745b 100644
> --- a/fs/proc/task_mmu.c
> +++ b/fs/proc/task_mmu.c
> @@ -2610,12 +2610,16 @@ static void make_uffd_wp_huge_pte(struct vm_area_struct *vma,
>  	if (softleaf_is_hwpoison(entry) || softleaf_is_marker(entry))
>  		return;
>  
> -	if (softleaf_is_migration(entry))
> +	if (softleaf_is_migration(entry)) {
>  		set_huge_pte_at(vma->vm_mm, addr, ptep,
>  				pte_swp_mkuffd_wp(ptent), psize);
> -	else
> -		huge_ptep_modify_prot_commit(vma, addr, ptep, ptent,
> -					     huge_pte_mkuffd_wp(ptent));
> +	} else {
> +		pte_t old_pte, new_pte;
> +
> +		old_pte = huge_ptep_modify_prot_start(vma, addr, ptep);
> +		new_pte = huge_pte_mkuffd_wp(old_pte);
> +		huge_ptep_modify_prot_commit(vma, addr, ptep, old_pte, new_pte);
> +	}
>  }
>  #endif /* CONFIG_HUGETLB_PAGE */
>

---

On 29/05/26 10:53 pm, Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) wrote:
> The partial-page check compares against HPAGE_SIZE (PMD_SIZE), which
> is wrong for gigantic hugetlb hstates (e.g. 1G). The walker hands the
> callback a huge_page_size()-sized range, never start + HPAGE_SIZE, so
> the comparison always declares it partial and aborts the WP. Compare
> against the actual hstate's page size.
> 
> Fixes: 52526ca7fdb9 ("fs/proc/task_mmu: implement IOCTL to get and optionally clear info about PTEs")
> Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
> Reported-by: Sashiko AI review <sashiko-bot@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau <kas@kernel.org>
> ---

Reviewed-by: Dev Jain <dev.jain@arm.com>


>  fs/proc/task_mmu.c | 2 +-
>  1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 1 deletion(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/proc/task_mmu.c b/fs/proc/task_mmu.c
> index e21a38ac745b..1489c67e88f7 100644
> --- a/fs/proc/task_mmu.c
> +++ b/fs/proc/task_mmu.c
> @@ -2960,7 +2960,7 @@ static int pagemap_scan_hugetlb_entry(pte_t *ptep, unsigned long hmask,
>  	if (~categories & PAGE_IS_WRITTEN)
>  		goto out_unlock;
>  
> -	if (end != start + HPAGE_SIZE) {
> +	if (end != start + huge_page_size(hstate_vma(vma))) {
>  		/* Partial HugeTLB page WP isn't possible. */
>  		pagemap_scan_backout_range(p, start, end);
>  		p->arg.walk_end = start;
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes
Mike Rapoport Reviewed-by Positive Heuristic
Gave Reviewed-by
View on lore ↗
Discussions / RFCs (0)
No activity
Reviews Given (5)
Re: [PATCH v2 3/3] arm64: escalate smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI as a last resort Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies
Re: [PATCH 4/4] arm64: route crash_smp_send_stop() last resort through SDEI Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies
Last comment 2026-06-12 Re: [PATCH v1] kasan: Fix false-positive wild-memory-access on x86 under 5-level paging Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch fixes a false-positive wild-memory-access error in the KASAN (Kernel Address Sanitizer) on x86 systems using 5-level paging. The issue occurs because the `cpu_feature_enabled()` function reads the live bits of CPU features before alternatives are patched, leading to incorrect results in interrupt context. To fix this, the patch modifies the `identify_cpu()` function to save and restore interrupts around the code that populates the `x86_capability` structure, ensuring that the correct values are read even in interrupt context. This change is specific to x86 systems and does not affect other architectures.

1 reviewer comment — 0 participants
0 participants
Ihor Solodrai ↳ replying to Kiryl Shutsemau Inline Review Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer Ihor Solodrai expressed uncertainty about the fix proposed by Kiryl Shutsemau, questioning whether disabling IRQs in identify_cpu() is a complete solution and potentially introducing undesirable consequences, such as NMI handling issues. Reviewer Ihor Solodrai expressed doubts about disabling IRQs in identify_cpu() as the solution to prevent a window where boot_cpu_data.x86_capability reads back zero, suggesting that this approach may not be correct because it only protects a portion of the capabilities array and could lead to issues with mcheck_cpu_init() which performs kmalloc(GFP_KERNEL) during this time. Reviewer Ihor Solodrai suggested defining a static key for LA57 feature in KASAN code to avoid performance issues and make the code x86-specific, but noted that this would not address the underlying issue of capabilities being temporarily cleared on boot.
Show original comment 
Hi Kiryl, thank you for testing.

I thought of disabling IRQs here too, and AI bot flagged this as well [1].

I wasn't sure it'd be a good fix, because I don't know if it has any
undesirable consequences, and whether it's actually a complete fix.
For example, can a NMI hit this?

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/bpf/7b6d449f-c70c-4e8e-bfc4-a2f75517395c@linux.dev/

---

If we scope this narrowly, then it remains LA57-specific, because we protect
only a portion of the capabilities array.
IIUC to ensure x86_capability is "ready", a proper scope should be
entire identify_cpu(). And this may be a problem, because mcheck_cpu_init()
does kmalloc(GFP_KERNEL) [2], for example.

So I have doubts disabling IRQ is the right approach.

[2] https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux.git/tree/arch/x86/kernel/cpu/mce/genpool.c?h=v7.1-rc7#n119

---

What if we define a static key for LA57 feature for KASAN?
This will avoid the performance hit we get with USE_EARLY_PGTABLE_L5.
And KASAN won't care about x86_capability being cleared.
Is that feasible/acceptable?

That will be a KASAN-specific LA57-specific fix however.
The underlying "capabilities are temporarily cleared on boot"
issue would remain. Although it wasn't a problem until this bug surfaced.

Something like this:

diff --git a/arch/x86/include/asm/kasan.h b/arch/x86/include/asm/kasan.h
index d7e33c7f096b..6c1ebcb4a417 100644
--- a/arch/x86/include/asm/kasan.h
+++ b/arch/x86/include/asm/kasan.h
@@ -36,6 +36,35 @@ static inline void kasan_populate_shadow_for_vaddr(void *va, size_t size,
                                                   int nid) { }
 #endif
 
+#ifdef CONFIG_KASAN_GENERIC
+#include <linux/jump_label.h>
+
+DECLARE_STATIC_KEY_TRUE(kasan_la57_enabled);
+
+#define addr_has_metadata addr_has_metadata
+static __always_inline bool addr_has_metadata(const void *addr)
+{
+       unsigned int shift = static_branch_likely(&kasan_la57_enabled) ? 56 : 47;
+
+       return (unsigned long)addr >= (-1UL << shift);
+}
+#endif /* CONFIG_KASAN_GENERIC */
+
 #endif
 
 #endif
diff --git a/arch/x86/kernel/cpu/common.c b/arch/x86/kernel/cpu/common.c
index a4268c47f2bc..79bad61f4891 100644
--- a/arch/x86/kernel/cpu/common.c
+++ b/arch/x86/kernel/cpu/common.c
@@ -28,6 +28,7 @@
 #include <linux/stackprotector.h>
 #include <linux/utsname.h>
 #include <linux/efi.h>
+#include <linux/kasan.h>
 
 #include <asm/alternative.h>
 #include <asm/cmdline.h>
@@ -2581,6 +2582,19 @@ void __init arch_cpu_finalize_init(void)
 {
        struct cpuinfo_x86 *c = this_cpu_ptr(&cpu_info);
 
+#ifdef CONFIG_KASAN_GENERIC
+       if (!__pgtable_l5_enabled)
+               static_branch_disable(&kasan_la57_enabled);
+#endif
+
        identify_boot_cpu();
 
        select_idle_routine();
diff --git a/arch/x86/mm/kasan_init_64.c b/arch/x86/mm/kasan_init_64.c
index 998b6010d6d3..3cb20b4d8799 100644
--- a/arch/x86/mm/kasan_init_64.c
+++ b/arch/x86/mm/kasan_init_64.c
@@ -11,6 +11,7 @@
 #include <linux/sched.h>
 #include <linux/sched/task.h>
 #include <linux/vmalloc.h>
+#include <linux/jump_label.h>
 
 #include <asm/e820/types.h>
 #include <asm/pgalloc.h>
@@ -20,6 +21,12 @@
 
 extern struct range pfn_mapped[E820_MAX_ENTRIES];
 
+DEFINE_STATIC_KEY_TRUE(kasan_la57_enabled);
+
 static p4d_t tmp_p4d_table[MAX_PTRS_PER_P4D] __initdata __aligned(PAGE_SIZE);
 
 static __init void *early_alloc(size_t size, int nid, bool should_panic)

Nhat Pham

13 items
Patches Submitted (1)
[RFC PATCH v2 0/7] mm, swap: Virtual Swap Space (Swap Table Edition) 7 patches
Show 7 individual patches
  • [1/7] mm, swap: add virtual swap device infrastructure
  • [2/7] mm, swap: support zswap and zeroswap as vswap backends
  • [3/7] mm, swap: support physical swap as a vswap backend
  • [4/7] mm, swap: only charge physical swap entries
  • [5/7] mm, swap: add debugfs counters for vswap
  • [6/7] mm, swap: defer memcg_table allocation on physical clusters
  • [7/7] mm, swap: widen swap_info_struct max/pages to unsigned long
Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies

This patch series introduces Virtual Swap Space (Swap Table Edition), a new approach to swap management that decouples swap entries from physical slots in the backing swap device. This is motivated by the inefficiencies of current design, which occupies physical space for pages that are never expected to hit disk, such as compressed and zero-filled pages. The patch series addresses these issues by introducing a virtual swap table, deferring memcg_table allocation until first vswap-backing slot installs, and widening swap_info_struct->max and ->pages to support up to 8 PB of swap space.

Discussions / RFCs (0)
No activity
Reviews Given (8)
Last comment 2026-06-10 Re: [zswap?] BUG: sleeping function called from invalid context at kernel/cgroup/rstat.c:421 Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch addresses a bug where the function shrinker_debugfs_count_show() is calling zswap_shrinker_count() from an invalid context, potentially causing it to sleep. The issue arises because zswap_shrinker_count() triggers stats flushing, which can lead to sleeping. To fix this, the patch suggests removing unnecessary RCU locks and considering a long-term solution of eliminating the stats flushing altogether.

4 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Nhat Pham (author) Needs Work LLM
Author acknowledged that the code should check for SHRINKER_MEMCG_AWARE flag before entering the loop and inducing overhead, agreed to restructure in a specific way
Show original comment 
Also, looking at the code a bit closer - if (!shrinker->flags &
SHRINKER_MEMCG_AWARE), we shouldn't be getting into this loop at all
and inducing all the memcg-related overhead at all...

The code really should be structure as:

if (shrinker->flags & SHRINKER_MEMCG_AWARE) {
    total = shrinker_count_objects(shrinker, NULL, count_per_node);
    if (total)
      ...
} else {
   memcg = mem_cgroup_iter(NULL, NULL, NULL);
   do {
   } while ((memcg = mem_cgroup_iter(NULL, memcg, NULL)) != NULL);
}
...
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledged fix needed, agreed to restructure
Shakeel Butt ↳ replying to Nhat Pham Inline Review Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer noted that shrinker_debugfs_count_show() invokes zswap_shrinker_count(), which might sleep due to stats flushing, and suggested removing the rcu_read_(un)lock() calls as they are not necessary for safety Reviewer Shakeel Butt noted that since mem_cgroup_iter() already pins the memory cgroup, it's unnecessary to use rcu_read_section() in shrinker_debugfs_count_show(), and suggested removing the rcu_read_(un)lock() calls. Reviewer Shakeel Butt pointed out that the caller should already hold a reference to the shrinker, and questioned whether the debugfs file entry holds a reference to the shrinker being given; he also noted that the rcu_read_section() is against the call_rcu() in shrinker_free(), and suggested using shrinker_try_get() as previously mentioned
Show original comment 
+Roman, Qi

On Wed, Jun 10, 2026 at 09:38:03AM -0700, Nhat Pham wrote:

---

mem_cgroup_iter() handles the lifetime of memcg, so there is no need for rcu
read section for memcg.

---

Shouldn't the caller already holds the reference to the shrinker which it is
giving to this function? Does debugfs file entry holds a reference to the
shrinker which it is giving.

After looking at shrinker_free(), it has call_rcu(&shrinker->rcu,
shrinker_free_rcu_cb), so this rcu read section is against that.

I think we can simply use shrinker_try_get() here as Nhat said.
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes
Nhat Pham (author) ↳ replying to Shakeel Butt Neutral LLM
Author questioned the safety of accessing a shrinker within an RCU read section, pointing out that the shrinker is not freed until after the callback is registered.
Show original comment 
Hmm, so is this unsafe even with the current rcu shennanigans? What's
stopping shrinker to be freed by that callback before we enter
rcu_read_section()?

Seems like this is just implicitly correct - shrinker_debugfs_detach()
and shrinker_debugfs_remove() happens before call_rcu(&shrinker->rcu,
shrinker_free_rcu_cb);, so if you're reading this file, then it's
before shrinker_free_rcu_cb() is even registered?

Do we still need rcu or shrinker_try_get() here?
View on lore ↗
Signals: questioning, uncertainty
Shakeel Butt ↳ replying to Nhat Pham Inline Review Needs Work LLM
Reviewer noted that shrinker_debugfs_count_show() can sleep while holding a debugfs file reader lock, creating a deadlock scenario when parallel calls to shrinker_debugfs_remove() are made.
Show original comment 
I think you are right that we don't need rcu or shrinker_try_get() but it is
more about an active debugfs file reader. Suppose we are sleeping within rstat
flush from shrinker_debugfs_count_show() and there is a parallel
shrinker_debugfs_remove() call.

shrinker_debugfs_remove calls debugfs_remove_recursive and deep in the stack
there is a call wait_for_completion(&fsd->active_users_drained) which will wait
for active users, one of which is sleeping within rstat flush.

So, let's simply remove rcu read here.
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes

Shakeel Butt ↳ replying to Nhat Pham Neutral LLM
Agreed with Nhat's assessment that RCU or shrinker_try_get() is not necessary, but pointed out a potential issue with parallel debugfs file removal and sleeping within rstat flush.
Show original comment 
On Wed, Jun 10, 2026 at 11:38:29AM -0700, Nhat Pham wrote:
> On Wed, Jun 10, 2026 at 10:31\u202fAM Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev> wrote:
> >
> > +Roman, Qi
> >
> > On Wed, Jun 10, 2026 at 09:38:03AM -0700, Nhat Pham wrote:
> > > On Wed, Jun 10, 2026 at 9:05\u202fAM Zenghui Yu <zenghui.yu@linux.dev> wrote:
> > >
> > > Thanks for reporting, Zenghui.
> > >
> > >
> > > >
> > > > Hi all,
> > > >
> > > > The following splat was triggered on the mainline kernel:
> > > >
> > > >  BUG: sleeping function called from invalid context at kernel/cgroup/rstat.c:421
> > > >  in_atomic(): 0, irqs_disabled(): 0, non_block: 0, pid: 1126, name: cat
> > > >  preempt_count: 0, expected: 0
> > > >  RCU nest depth: 1, expected: 0
> > > >  CPU: 7 UID: 0 PID: 1126 Comm: cat Kdump: loaded Not tainted 7.1.0-rc7-00056-gacb7500801e9-dirty #304 PREEMPT
> > > >  Hardware name: QEMU QEMU Virtual Machine, BIOS edk2-stable202408-prebuilt.qemu.org 08/13/2024
> > > >  Call trace:
> > > >   show_stack+0x18/0x24 (C)
> > > >   dump_stack_lvl+0x78/0x90
> > > >   dump_stack+0x18/0x24
> > > >   __might_resched+0x114/0x170
> > > >   __might_sleep+0x48/0x98
> > > >   css_rstat_flush+0x54/0x564
> > > >   mem_cgroup_flush_stats+0x9c/0xb0
> > > >   zswap_shrinker_count+0xe4/0x1e4
> > > >   shrinker_debugfs_count_show+0xd8/0x268
> > >
> > > Ah, this seems a bit tricky.
> > >
> > > Seems like shrinker_debugfs_count_show() is invoking
> > > zswap_shrinker_count() in rcu_read_section(). zswap_shrinker_count()
> > > triggers a stats flushing, which might sleep. Not ideal.
> > >
> > > Is the rcu_read_section() here to protect memcg or shrinker? For
> > > memcg, i dont think it's necessary, no? mem_cgroup_iter() pins the
> > > memcg before returning.
> > >
> > > (memcg maintainers please fact check me).
> >
> > mem_cgroup_iter() handles the lifetime of memcg, so there is no need for rcu
> > read section for memcg.
> >
> > >
> > > If this is for the shrinker think this needs to follow shrink_slab()'s pattern.:
> > >
> > > rcu_read_lock();
> > > list_for_each_entry_rcu(shrinker, &shrinker_list, list)
> > > {
> > >     if (!shrinker_try_get(shrinker))
> > >         continue;
> > >     rcu_read_unlock();
> > > }
> > >
> > > But OTOH, doesn't seem like rcu_read_section() is what keeping it safe:
> >
> > Shouldn't the caller already holds the reference to the shrinker which it is
> > giving to this function? Does debugfs file entry holds a reference to the
> > shrinker which it is giving.
> >
> > After looking at shrinker_free(), it has call_rcu(&shrinker->rcu,
> > shrinker_free_rcu_cb), so this rcu read section is against that.
> >
> > I think we can simply use shrinker_try_get() here as Nhat said.
> 
> Hmm, so is this unsafe even with the current rcu shennanigans? What's
> stopping shrinker to be freed by that callback before we enter
> rcu_read_section()?
> 
> Seems like this is just implicitly correct - shrinker_debugfs_detach()
> and shrinker_debugfs_remove() happens before call_rcu(&shrinker->rcu,
> shrinker_free_rcu_cb);, so if you're reading this file, then it's
> before shrinker_free_rcu_cb() is even registered?
> 
> Do we still need rcu or shrinker_try_get() here?

I think you are right that we don't need rcu or shrinker_try_get() but it is
more about an active debugfs file reader. Suppose we are sleeping within rstat
flush from shrinker_debugfs_count_show() and there is a parallel
shrinker_debugfs_remove() call.

shrinker_debugfs_remove calls debugfs_remove_recursive and deep in the stack
there is a call wait_for_completion(&fsd->active_users_drained) which will wait
for active users, one of which is sleeping within rstat flush.

So, let's simply remove rcu read here.

>

> > >

> > > list_for_each_entry_rcu(shrinker, &shrinker_list, list)
> > > {

> > >         continue;
> > >     rcu_read_unlock();

> >

> > giving to this function? Does debugfs file entry holds a reference to the
> > shrinker which it is giving.
> >
> > After looking at shrinker_free(), it has call_rcu(&shrinker->rcu,
> > shrinker_free_rcu_cb), so this rcu read section is against that.
> >
> > I think we can simply use shrinker_try_get() here as Nhat said.
> 
> Hmm, so is this unsafe even with the current rcu shennanigans? What's
> stopping shrinker to be freed by that callback before we enter
> rcu_read_section()?
> 
> Seems like this is just implicitly correct - shrinker_debugfs_detach()
> and shrinker_debugfs_remove() happens before call_rcu(&shrinker->rcu,
> shrinker_free_rcu_cb);, so if you're reading this file, then it's

> 
> Do we still need rcu or shrinker_try_get() here?

I think you are right that we don't need rcu or shrinker_try_get() but it is
more about an active debugfs file reader. Suppose we are sleeping within rstat
flush from shrinker_debugfs_count_show() and there is a parallel
shrinker_debugfs_remove() call.

shrinker_debugfs_remove calls debugfs_remove_recursive and deep in the stack
there is a call wait_for_completion(&fsd->active_users_drained) which will wait
for active users, one of which is sleeping within rstat flush.

So, let's simply remove rcu read here.

>

Last comment 2026-06-10 Re: [PATCH v4 1/4] mm/zsmalloc: encode class index in obj value for lockless class lookup Neutral LLM

The patch proposes to encode class index in obj value for lockless class lookup in mm/zsmalloc.

2 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Nhat Pham Neutral LLM
Raised concerns about using log2 macro and suggested renaming macros to improve clarity.
Signals: Naming conventions
Wenchao Hao ↳ replying to Nhat Pham Neutral LLM
Clarified the purpose of log2 macro and suggested manual cascade due to its limitations. Also proposed renaming macros for better naming.
Show original comment 
On Wed, Jun 10, 2026 at 1:56 AM Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> On Tue, Jun 9, 2026 at 4:35 AM Wenchao Hao <haowenchao22@gmail.com> wrote:
> >
> > From: Wenchao Hao <haowenchao@xiaomi.com>
> >
> > + * ceil(log2(ZS_MAX_PAGES_PER_ZSPAGE)) at preprocessor time, for use
> > + * in #if below.  Kconfig restricts ZSMALLOC_CHAIN_SIZE to [4, 16].
> > + */
> > +#if ZS_MAX_PAGES_PER_ZSPAGE <= 4
> > +#define ZS_CHAIN_LOG2          2
> > +#elif ZS_MAX_PAGES_PER_ZSPAGE <= 8
> > +#define ZS_CHAIN_LOG2          3
> > +#elif ZS_MAX_PAGES_PER_ZSPAGE <= 16
> > +#define ZS_CHAIN_LOG2          4
>
> Do we not have log2 macro to use here?

const_ilog2() in <linux/log2.h> wraps everything in __builtin_constant_p(),
which is not a preprocessor expression and so cannot be used in an
#if condition.  Since we need this value at the preprocessor stage
(to decide ZS_OBJ_CLASS_BITS in the next #if), the manual cascade
seems unavoidable.

>
> > +#else
> > +#error "ZSMALLOC_CHAIN_SIZE out of expected range [4,16]"
> > +#endif
> > +
> > +/* PAGE_SHIFT - 5 = log2(PAGE_SIZE / 32); 32 = ZS_MIN_ALLOC_SIZE floor. */
> > +#define ZS_MAX_OBJ_PER_PAGE_LOG2       (PAGE_SHIFT - 5)
> > +
> > +/*
> > + * obj_idx width that keeps ZS_MIN_ALLOC_SIZE at its 32-byte floor.
> > + * Below this, ZS_MIN_ALLOC_SIZE is auto-raised by the MAX(32, ...)
> > + * formula -- still correct, but objects are coarser.
> > + */
> > +#define ZS_OBJ_IDX_DENSE_BITS  (ZS_CHAIN_LOG2 + ZS_MAX_OBJ_PER_PAGE_LOG2)
>
> ZS_CHAIN_LOG2 is (roughly) number of bits to represent the zpage index
> in the zspage, and ZS_MAX_OBJ_PER_PAGE_LOG2 is roughly the number of
> bits to represent the object index within the zpage, correct?
>

Exactly.  Both are really "how many bits are needed to represent X",
just expressed as log2(X):

  ZS_CHAIN_LOG2
    = ceil(log2(ZS_MAX_PAGES_PER_ZSPAGE))
    = bits to index a page within a zspage.

  ZS_MAX_OBJ_PER_PAGE_LOG2
    = log2(PAGE_SIZE / 32)
    = bits to index an object within a single page, at the smallest
      possible object size (32 bytes).

The "_LOG2" suffix names the math but hides the intent.  How about
renaming them as:

  ZS_CHAIN_LOG2            -> ZS_MAX_BITS_PAGES_PER_ZSPAGE
  ZS_MAX_OBJ_PER_PAGE_LOG2 -> ZS_MAX_BITS_OBJS_PER_PAGE
  ZS_OBJ_IDX_DENSE_BITS    -> ZS_MAX_BITS_OBJS_PER_ZSPAGE

Then the third one reads as the sum of the first two: "bits to index
any object in the densest zspage", and the ZS_OBJ_CLASS_BITS predicate
gets easier to follow.

Does that work for you?

Wenchao
Signals: Naming conventions
Last comment 2026-06-12 Re: [PATCH v4 1/4] mm/zsmalloc: encode class index in obj value for lockless class lookup Neutral LLM

Patch to encode class index in obj value for lockless class lookup was positively received by reviewers.

Wenchao Hao ↳ replying to Nhat Pham Reviewed-by Neutral LLM

Show original comment 
On Thu, Jun 11, 2026 at 10:31 PM Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> On Wed, Jun 10, 2026 at 8:15 PM Wenchao Hao <haowenchao22@gmail.com> wrote:
> >
> > On Thu, Jun 11, 2026 at 5:28 AM Barry Song <baohua@kernel.org> wrote:
> >
> > Agreed, will follow the convention and drop MAX_:
> >
> >   ZS_CHAIN_LOG2            -> ZS_PAGES_PER_ZSPAGE_BITS
> >   ZS_MAX_OBJ_PER_PAGE_LOG2 -> ZS_OBJS_PER_PAGE_BITS
> >   ZS_OBJ_IDX_DENSE_BITS    -> ZS_OBJS_PER_ZSPAGE_BITS
> >
> > Nhat -- sorry for the churn; this supersedes the names I proposed
> > earlier.  Let me know if the new naming still reads well to you.
>
> No worries at all. I like this a lot :)

Thanks!  Since the rest of 1/4 is unchanged from v4 apart from the
rename, would you be open to re-applying your Reviewed-by on the
next spin?

Wenchao
Last comment 2026-06-09 Re: [PATCH v3 1/4] mm/zswap: Make shrink_worker writeback cursor per-memcg Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch modifies the zswap shrink worker to writeback its cursor on a per-memcg basis. This change is necessary because proactive reclaim schemes can target cgroups and their children separately, but this can lead to inefficient reclaim if hotter memory in one cgroup is exhausted while colder memory in another cgroup is spared. The approach taken is to make the shrink worker's writeback cursor dependent on the memcg it is operating within.

4 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Yosry Ahmed ↳ replying to Nhat Pham Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer Yosry Ahmed noted that the unfairness of the per-memcg cursor is limited to the batch size per-invocation, which is relatively low even at its highest setting (0.5 MB), and suggested that reclaiming in larger steps would make shares more even. Reviewer Yosry Ahmed noted that the current implementation of proactive reclaim in zswap falls back to reclaiming in batches from each child cgroup because it doesn't accurately track coldest pages at the parent level, and suggested that ideally fairness shouldn't be a factor and instead reclaim the absolute coldest memory at the parent level. Reviewer Yosry Ahmed expressed concern about code complexity and unnecessary space overhead due to the introduction of per-memcg writeback cursor in the zswap shrink worker, specifically mentioning the zombie memcg cleanup as a contributing factor.
Show original comment 
Yes, the per-memcg cursor is more fair, and you can synthesize
scenarios that show that. However, I don't think this is a problem in
practice:

1. The unfairness is limited to the batch size per-invocation. If the
batch size is 128 pages (your highest one here), that's 0.5 MB (on
x86), which is fairly low? If the batch size is 32, it's even less.

2. Realistically, if you have a parent cgroup with with >10G of
memory, you wouldn't be reclaiming in steps of 1M. If you want to
reclaim 200MB, why are you doing it over 200 invocations? If you do it
in a single one (or over a few retries) the shares should become much
more even.

We're trying to fix a practical use case, not finding reasons why a
simple implementation won't work -- right?

More below (to Nhat's point).

---

I would go a bit farther and claim that ideally fairness shouldn't
even be a factor. If you invoke proactive reclaim on a parent cgroup
with 100MB, you want to reclaim the coldest 100MB in that parent, no
matter what child they reside in. If one child cgroup has 100% hot
memory and one child cgroup has 100% cold memory, ideally you'd
reclaim all the cold memory from the second child.

However, the implementation of the LRUs and the coldness tracking
doesn't allow for doing this, so we "fallback" to reclaiming in
batches from each child because we don't really know where the coldest
pages overall are. If that changes in the future (somehow), I argue
that the correct thing to do is reclaim the absolute coldest memory at
the parent level.

If you want to reclaim evenly among the children, you can do that and
directly reclaim from the children.

---

Mostly the complexity (e.g. the zombie memcg cleanup) and a tiny bit
the unnecessary space (8 bytes is not a lot, but these things add up).
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes, suggested improvements, suggested alternative approach
Yosry Ahmed ↳ replying to Nhat Pham Reviewed-by Neutral LLM
Yosry Ahmed noted that the unfairness in the patch is limited to the batch size per-invocation, and suggested that it should be fine as long as the amount is not divided over a large number of iterations.
Show original comment 
Forgot to respond to this part, the unfairness is limited to the batch
size per-invocation, so it should be fine as long as you don't divide
the amount over 100 iterations for some reason. Also yes, all memory
in zswap is cold, the relative coldness is not that important (e.g.
compared to relative coldness during reclaim).
View on lore ↗

Nhat Pham (author) ↳ replying to Yosry Ahmed Needs Work LLM

Show original comment 
Ok then yeah, I think we should shelve per-memcg cursor for the next
version. Down the line, if we have more data that unfairness is an
issue, we can always fix it. One step at a time :)
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledged reviewer's concern, agreed to shelve feature
Hao Jia ↳ replying to Nhat Pham Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer Hao Jia noted that the per-memcg cursor approach may lead to inefficient reclaim, where hotter memory of one cgroup is exhausted while colder memory of another cgroup is spared, and suggested dropping the per-memcg cursor in favor of reusing the root cgroup cursor logic.
Show original comment 
Thanks a lot to Yosry, Nhat, and Shakeel for the great suggestions!

Let me summarize what I plan to do in the next version to make sure we 
are on the same page:

  - Drop the per-memcg cursor and keep the root cgroup cursor 
(zswap_next_shrink) logic intact.
  - Stick to using the zswap_writeback_only key, and change the 
proactive writeback size to use the compressed size.
  - Consolidate and reuse the logic between shrink_worker() and 
shrink_memcg(). Enable batch writeback in the shrink_worker() path, 
while keeping the writeback behavior in the zswap_store() path unchanged.

Please let me know if I missed or misunderstood anything. Thanks again 
for clearing things up!

Thanks\uff0c
Hao

Summarized the plan for the next version, which includes dropping per-memcg cursor and consolidating logic between shrink_worker() and shrink_memcg().
Show original comment 

On 2026/6/9 02:01, Nhat Pham wrote:
> On Mon, Jun 8, 2026 at 9:48\u202fAM Yosry Ahmed <yosry@kernel.org> wrote:
>>
>>> But OTOH, this does seem like a recipe for inefficient reclaim. We
>>> might exhaust hotter memory of a cgroup while sparing colder memory of
>>> another cgroup... But maybe if they're all cold anyway, then who
>>> cares, and eventually you'll get to the cold stuff of other child?
>>
>> Forgot to respond to this part, the unfairness is limited to the batch
>> size per-invocation, so it should be fine as long as you don't divide

Thanks a lot to Yosry, Nhat, and Shakeel for the great suggestions!

Let me summarize what I plan to do in the next version to make sure we 
are on the same page:

  - Drop the per-memcg cursor and keep the root cgroup cursor 
(zswap_next_shrink) logic intact.
  - Stick to using the zswap_writeback_only key, and change the 
proactive writeback size to use the compressed size.
  - Consolidate and reuse the logic between shrink_worker() and 
shrink_memcg(). Enable batch writeback in the shrink_worker() path, 
while keeping the writeback behavior in the zswap_store() path unchanged.

Please let me know if I missed or misunderstood anything. Thanks again 
for clearing things up!

Thanks\uff0c
