Patches Submitted (3)
Ongoing Submitted 2026-06-03 Last comment 2026-06-09 ⏱ No recent activity [PATCH v3 0/3] iova: use maple tree for O(log n) allocation 3 patches
Show 3 individual patches
  • [1/3] iova: convert from rbtree to maple tree Submitted 2026-06-03
  • [2/3] iova: add KUnit test suite Submitted 2026-06-03
  • [3/3] iova: defer maple tree erase on GFP_ATOMIC failure Neutral Submitted 2026-06-03Last comment 2026-06-09 Jason Gunthorpe
Neutral LLM

The patch series uses a maple tree to index iova ranges, allowing alloc_iova() to have O(log n) complexity while keeping memory usage similar to the original code. The patches also add self-tests for the iova code and were tested on an AMD Bergamo system with IOMMU enabled.

1 reviewer comment — 0 participants
0 participants
Jason Gunthorpe ↳ replying to Rik van Riel Neutral LLM
Raised a question about the use of mas_store(NULL) instead of mas_erase() in the iova alloc/free code, suggesting that pair-wise allocation is required.
Show original comment 
On Tue, Jun 02, 2026 at 11:35:48PM -0400, Rik van Riel wrote:
> +/*
> + * Remove an IOVA entry from the maple tree. Returns true on success.
> + * On failure (maple tree node allocation under GFP_ATOMIC failed),
> + * returns false \u2014 the entry remains in the tree and the caller must
> + * not free the struct iova.
> + */
> +static bool remove_iova(struct iova_domain *iovad, struct iova *iova)
>  {
>  	MA_STATE(mas, &iovad->mtree, iova->pfn_lo, iova->pfn_hi);
>  
> @@ -165,7 +175,36 @@ static void remove_iova(struct iova_domain *iovad, struct iova *iova)
>  	if (iova->pfn_lo < iovad->dma_32bit_pfn)
>  		iovad->max32_alloc_size = iovad->dma_32bit_pfn;
>  
> -	mas_store_gfp(&mas, NULL, GFP_ATOMIC);
> +	if (mas_store_gfp(&mas, NULL, GFP_ATOMIC))
> +		return false;

But why does it use mas_store(NULL) instead of mas_erase()? I thought
the iova alloc/free has to be pair wise, we don't split allocations?

Jason
Signals: QUESTION
[PATCH] sched/fair: clamp rescaled vlag in reweight_entity() to bound entity_key() Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies

This patch fixes a scheduler bug that caused a hard lockup on a large CPU machine running an EEVDF kernel. The issue was due to the rescaled vlag in reweight_entity() not being clamped to the per-entity lag limit, leading to an overflow and subsequent deadlock. The patch adds a clamp operation to reweight_entity() to prevent this overflow and ensures that the vruntime accounting remains correct even after large weight changes.

Last comment 2026-06-12 [PATCH 0/9] sched: make WARN_ON under rq->lock deadlock-safe (SCHED_WARN_ON) 9 patches
Show 9 individual patches
  • [1/9] sched: add SCHED_WARN_ON()/SCHED_WARN_ON_ONCE()/SCHED_WARN()/SCHED_WARN_ONCE()
  • [2/9] sched/core: defer WARN console output under rq->lock
  • [3/9] sched/fair: defer WARN console output under rq->lock
  • [4/9] sched/deadline: defer WARN console output under rq->lock
  • [5/9] sched/rt: defer WARN console output under rq->lock
  • [6/9] sched_ext: defer WARN console output under rq->lock
  • [7/9] sched/core_sched: defer WARN console output under rq->lock
  • [8/9] sched/deadline: defer WARN console output under rq->lock
  • [9/9] sched/rt: defer WARN console output under rq->lock
Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch series addresses a deadlock issue in the Linux kernel's scheduler, where WARN_ON() calls under rq->lock or ->pi_lock can cause recursive locking and deadlocks. To fix this, new macros SCHED_WARN_ON(), SCHED_WARN_ON_ONCE(), SCHED_WARN(), and SCHED_WARN_ONCE() are introduced, which use printk_deferred to defer console output to irq_work instead of emitting it synchronously. The patch series converts 134 WARN*() calls in the scheduler code to use these new macros, making them deadlock-safe.

5 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Peter Zijlstra ↳ replying to Rik Riel Contentious LLM
Peter Zijlstra strongly disagrees with using printk_deferred(), arguing it can prevent output from being seen in case of a machine malfunction, and instead suggests fixing printk() itself.
Show original comment 
Sorry, no. I've said it before and I'll stick with it. Just no.

printk_deferred() is an abomination, it means that if you mess up the
machine properly you'll *NEVER* see the output.

As per always, printk() is the one that needs fixing, and IIRC they were
very close to getting there.
View on lore ↗
Signals: strong disagreement, NACK
Rik Riel (author) ↳ replying to Peter Zijlstra Neutral LLM
Author acknowledged a potential deadlock issue in the printk code when waking up the printk worker thread and holding the runqueue lock, but is unsure if using an irq work to wake up the worker would make things more reliable.
Show original comment 
Printk to certain console types is always deferred,
by default, because trying to synchronously print
everything to a slow serial console can lead to a
system softlockup panic.

In fact, this particular lockup is due to the
printk being passed off to a worker thread, and
the kernel deadlocking when the wakeup code
tries to grab the runqueue lock its CPU already
holds.

You are right that this could be fixed in the
printk code, but the solution there will by
necessity continue to contain some deferring.


I suppose the printk code could use something
like an irq work to wake up the printk worker,
and avoid the scheduler deadlock that way?

I'm not sure that would make things more
reliable, though...

-- 
All Rights Reversed.
View on lore ↗
Signals: uncertainty, open-ended question
Peter Zijlstra ↳ replying to Rik Riel Inline Review Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
The reviewer, Peter Zijlstra, pointed out that the current implementation of SCHED_WARN_ON() still has a deadlock issue due to the use of console_sem, which wakes up console kthreads from printk() call context while holding scheduler locks. He suggested reworking printk() to handle message buffering and printing in a lockless manner. The reviewer expressed disagreement over default settings for the serial console and printk, preferring to use earlycon and force printk to be early_printk() instead of relying on the legacy direct path.
Show original comment 
The non-atomic consoles will always need a buffer, but atomic consoles
can (and should IMO) push out the messages immediately.

Anyway, the thing that keeps tripping is that console_sem thing, that
should just entirely go away. That thing ends up doing a wakeup from
printk() call context, which obviously doesn't work when inside the
scheduler locks.

So printk should:

 - stick msg in buffer (lockless)
 - print to atomic consoles (lockless)
 - use irq_work to wake console kthreads (lockless)
 - each kthread then tries to flush buffer to its own non-atomic console
   in non-atomic context.

---

disagreement between printk people an me is about defaults IIRC. I want
to have the serial console default to atomic, they want it to be an
option. But whatever, as long as I can specify on the kernel cmdline
that my serial should be atomic I'm good.

Myself, I almost exclusively run with earlycon serial and force printk
to be early_printk() (effectively not using printk at all). This works
perfectly fine and is the most reliable thing ever. I can push out
characters to the UART from any context.

And sure, sometimes its gets a little scrambled, but meh.

I have this working with real actual serial, IPMI/serial-over-lan and
AMT/serial-over-lan.

And if a machine doesn't have serial, its a paperweight ;-)

Now, of course I also use trace_printk() a lot, but for those moments
when the machine goes down hard, nothing beats serial.
View on lore ↗
Signals: deadlock issue, lockless handling, disagreement
Rik Riel (author) ↳ replying to Peter Zijlstra Neutral LLM
The author acknowledges a concern about the impact of deferred console output on system stability and agrees that making it an option could be beneficial for different scenarios.
Show original comment 
Great for development, not so good for production.

When something like an OOM kill happens, the amount
of data printed by the kernel can take a long time
to get flushed out a serial port.

In fact, it can take long enough to cause things
like RCU stalls and soft lockups, and break
workloads that way.

Having the serial console deferred allows those
systems to survive.

If the system crashes before the messages were sent
out the serial port, the dmesg buffer will be in
the vmcore, and get extracted by the crash analysis
tooling.

It sounds like the printk people may be right with
making that behavior an option, since different
scenarios require different behavior.

-- 
All Rights Reversed.
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledges a concern, agrees with alternative approach
Peter Zijlstra ↳ replying to Rik Riel Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
The reviewer suggested a different approach to handle WARN/BUG messages, proposing to create a threshold and route ERR-level messages directly to the atomic console while lower-priority messages are buffered and spooled out later.
Show original comment 
I've not hit OOM in a decade or so; I've no idea how much silly nonsense
it spouts. The example the printk people have used for a long while is
booting with a million block devices, apparently that generates insane
amount of boot noise -- another problem I don't have :-)

Anyway, one solution there is to create a threshold and say ERR and
above go directly to atomic console while everything below goes into the
buffer and gets spooled out later on the non-atomic variant.

This way all your WARN/BUG and other useful bits actually hit the wire
when you need them, and the useless verbiage goes into the bin for
later.

Anyway, all this is stuff that seems trivially solvable.
View on lore ↗

Re: [PATCH v3 3/3] iova: defer maple tree erase on GFP_ATOMIC failure Neutral Heuristic
Last comment 2026-06-12 Re: [PATCH v3 3/3] iova: defer maple tree erase on GFP_ATOMIC failure Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch defers the erase operation of a maple tree in memory allocation failure scenarios to prevent potential deadlocks. The issue arises from the mas_erase function calling mas_nomem while holding a spinlock, which is not safe. The solution involves modifying the remove_iova function to defer the erase operation when GFP_ATOMIC fails.

4 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Jason Gunthorpe ↳ replying to Rik Riel Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer questioned whether mtree_erase can fail with ENOMEM and if it's safe to call in an atomic context, suggesting the issue might be better addressed by Liam.
Show original comment 
Oh, the kdoc doesn't say that, it doesn't return any error code if it
can't allocate memory, and not a single caller checks for erase
failures.

I assumed internally it "somehow worked out" even though there are
allocations in the callchains..

This is probably a better question for Liam? Can mtree_erase actually
fail ENOMEM? Is it safe to call it in an atomic context?

Jason
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested clarification on error handling, suggested seeking input from another developer
Rik Riel (author) ↳ replying to Jason Gunthorpe Neutral LLM
Rik Riel acknowledged Jason Gunthorpe's feedback that mas_erase() function calls mas_nomem(mas, GFP_KERNEL) which is not safe to call while holding a spinlock, and proposed two possible solutions: always asynchronously freeing iovas using __free_iova and friends or reverting to the augmented rbtree if asynchronous freeing breaks the system.
Show original comment 
Yes, it can fail.

When it does, __free_iova and friends fall back to
asynchronously freeing the iova from a worker.

If we are ok with always asynchronously freeing
iovas, we might be able to simplify the code by
always going through that helper.

If there are cases where asynchronously freeing
the iova breaks the system, we cannot use the
maple tree, but need the augmented rbtree, instead.

I do still have a cleaned up version of the augmented
rbtree, if asynchronous freeing is a real concern.

-- 
All Rights Reversed.
View on lore ↗
Signals: clarification, explanation
Jason Gunthorpe ↳ replying to Rik Riel Inline Review Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer noted that mas_erase() function does not propagate ENOMEM errors to the caller, potentially leading to unexpected behavior in atomic contexts, and suggested reverting to a previous option of handling memory allocation failures with mas_store_gfp()
Show original comment 
Currently it never returns a failure to the caller. Look at mas_erase():

	entry = mas_state_walk(mas);
	if (!entry)
		return NULL;
[..]
	if (mas_is_err(mas))
		goto out;
[..]
out:
	mas_destroy(mas);
	return entry;

There is no propogation of ENOMEM, it returns success. No caller
checks for any error here either.

So I think the intention is that it cannot fail, yet it does have the
memory allocations and busted failure path. Hence asking Liam what it
should be, and what about an atomic context.

Perhaps this might be relying on the modern kernels "small allocations
never fail", meaning mas_erase never fails, but then you can't
call it from an atomic context..

In any case, it does look like you can't use mas_erase from an atomic
context anyhow so your prior option with the mas_store_gfp() and
failure handling seems reasonable.

Jason
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes
Liam Howlett ↳ replying to Jason Gunthorpe Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer noted that mas_erase() calls mas_nomem(mas, GFP_KERNEL) while holding a spinlock in remove_iova(), which is not safe and requested an alternative solution to avoid this issue. Reviewer noted that mas_erase() function calls mas_nomem(mas, GFP_KERNEL) which is not safe to call while holding a spinlock, and suggested either making maple tree code uglier or reverting to the augmented rbtree Reviewer Liam Howlett noted that the patch does not propagate GFP_ATOMIC failure to the caller, which could lead to silent failures and requested changes to return -ENOMEM on failure, update documentation, and audit callers. Reviewer Liam Howlett pointed out that using mas_store_gfp() could lead to unnecessary internal node changes and rebalancing in the maple tree, suggesting instead writing XA_ZERO_ENTRY over each entry as it is dealt with and then doing a single mas_store_gfp(NULL) over the whole range at the end.
Show original comment 
+Cc maple-tree list.

On 26/06/12 03:03PM, Jason Gunthorpe wrote:

---

At one point this was considered to be impossible to fail, and it is
documented to return the entry or null.

---

It _can_ fail, but right now that error will not be propagated to the
caller.  The caller could infer the failure due to the return of NULL...
if that's what would happen, so there's very much an issue on failure
that I need to investigate and fix.

It is possible that it fails with -ENOMEM.  And the spinlock can be
dropped if you are not using an external lock and the gfp flag allows
blocking.  On failure to allocate and the lock is dropped, we retry the
operation from the start in case there was a race with another writer.

I think I should probably change this to return -ENOMEM, fix the docs on
it, and probably audit the callers (most use external locks or are
early-boot-so-don't-worry-about-it).  Any issue here should also be
caught by lockdep pretty quickly.

---

mas_store_gfp() works if you know the range of your entry.  You could
also write the XA_ZERO_ENTRY over the entry so that there is no internal
node changes - just a value swap.  If you do this, you have to be
careful when reading things back when using the mas_ interface.

I think, in your case, hitting an XA_ZERO_ENTRY would be necessary to
indicate that we cannot reuse this particular location until it is
correctly dealt with?  Or is the maple tree the only reason it is
considered unusable?

One thing to remember is that each write can cause allocations to occur,
so if you have a list of items being overwritten then you are causing
the tree to do each write and potentially rebalancing (as you shrink the
data beyond the lower limit of the node).

One way around that is to write XA_ZERO_ENTRY over each one as you
deal with your entry.  Then, when you are done you do a single
mas_store_gfp() of NULL over the whole range.  It will be a larger tree
operation, but smaller than the incremental steps.

Thanks,
Liam
View on lore ↗
Signals: locking, safety, requested changes

On Fri, Jun 12, 2026 at 01:23:58PM -0400, Rik van Riel wrote:
> On Fri, 2026-06-12 at 13:48 -0300, Jason Gunthorpe wrote:

> > can't allocate memory, and not a single caller checks for erase
> > failures.
> > 
> > I assumed internally it "somehow worked out" even though there are
> > allocations in the callchains..
> > 
> > This is probably a better question for Liam? Can mtree_erase actually
> > fail ENOMEM? Is it safe to call it in an atomic context?
> 
> Yes, it can fail.

Currently it never returns a failure to the caller. Look at mas_erase():

	entry = mas_state_walk(mas);
	if (!entry)
		return NULL;
[..]
	if (mas_is_err(mas))
		goto out;
[..]
out:
	mas_destroy(mas);
	return entry;

There is no propogation of ENOMEM, it returns success. No caller
checks for any error here either.

So I think the intention is that it cannot fail, yet it does have the
memory allocations and busted failure path. Hence asking Liam what it
should be, and what about an atomic context.

Perhaps this might be relying on the modern kernels "small allocations
never fail", meaning mas_erase never fails, but then you can't
call it from an atomic context..

In any case, it does look like you can't use mas_erase from an atomic
context anyhow so your prior option with the mas_store_gfp() and
failure handling seems reasonable.

Jason

Disagreed with Rik's suggestion, proposing an alternative solution involving a threshold for ERR and above messages to be sent directly to the atomic console.
Show original comment 
On Thu, Jun 11, 2026 at 12:20:27PM -0400, Rik van Riel wrote:
> On Thu, 2026-06-11 at 09:43 +0200, Peter Zijlstra wrote:
> > 
> > Sorry, no. I've said it before and I'll stick with it. Just no.
> > 
> > printk_deferred() is an abomination, it means that if you mess up the
> > machine properly you'll *NEVER* see the output.
> > 
> > As per always, printk() is the one that needs fixing, and IIRC they
> > were
> > very close to getting there.
> 
> Printk to certain console types is always deferred,
> by default, because trying to synchronously print
> everything to a slow serial console can lead to a
> system softlockup panic.
> 
> In fact, this particular lockup is due to the
> printk being passed off to a worker thread, and
> the kernel deadlocking when the wakeup code
> tries to grab the runqueue lock its CPU already
> holds.
> 
> You are right that this could be fixed in the
> printk code, but the solution there will by
> necessity continue to contain some deferring.
> 
> 
> I suppose the printk code could use something
> like an irq work to wake up the printk worker,
> and avoid the scheduler deadlock that way?
> 
> I'm not sure that would make things more
> reliable, though...

The non-atomic consoles will always need a buffer, but atomic consoles
can (and should IMO) push out the messages immediately.

Anyway, the thing that keeps tripping is that console_sem thing, that
should just entirely go away. That thing ends up doing a wakeup from
printk() call context, which obviously doesn't work when inside the
scheduler locks.

So printk should:

 - stick msg in buffer (lockless)
 - print to atomic consoles (lockless)
 - use irq_work to wake console kthreads (lockless)
 - each kthread then tries to flush buffer to its own non-atomic console
   in non-atomic context.

>From what I understand, we're very close to having this work. The only
disagreement between printk people an me is about defaults IIRC. I want
to have the serial console default to atomic, they want it to be an
option. But whatever, as long as I can specify on the kernel cmdline
that my serial should be atomic I'm good.

Myself, I almost exclusively run with earlycon serial and force printk
to be early_printk() (effectively not using printk at all). This works
perfectly fine and is the most reliable thing ever. I can push out
characters to the UART from any context.

And sure, sometimes its gets a little scrambled, but meh.

I have this working with real actual serial, IPMI/serial-over-lan and
AMT/serial-over-lan.

And if a machine doesn't have serial, its a paperweight ;-)

Now, of course I also use trace_printk() a lot, but for those moments
when the machine goes down hard, nothing beats serial.
Signals: CONTROVERSIAL
Rik van Riel ↳ replying to Peter Zijlstra Neutral LLM
Acknowledged Peter's point about the potential issue with OOM kills and serial console output, but emphasized the importance of having a deferred mechanism for handling printk messages in production environments.
Signals: NEEDS_WORK
Last comment 2026-06-12 Re: [PATCH 0/9] sched: make WARN_ON under rq->lock deadlock-safe (SCHED_WARN_ON) Neutral LLM

The patch aims to make WARN_ON under rq->lock deadlock-safe, but some reviewers question its impact on production systems and suggest alternative solutions.

2 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Rik van Riel Needs Work LLM
Expressed concerns about the patch's impact on production systems, particularly in scenarios where OOM kills occur. Suggested alternative solutions, such as creating a threshold for ERR messages.
Signals: CONTENTIOUS
Peter Zijlstra ↳ replying to Rik van Riel Neutral LLM
Responded to Rik's concerns, stating that he has not encountered OOM issues in a decade and suggested an alternative solution involving a threshold for ERR messages.
Show original comment 
On Thu, Jun 11, 2026 at 09:53:45PM -0400, Rik van Riel wrote:
> On Thu, 2026-06-11 at 21:19 +0200, Peter Zijlstra wrote:
> > 
> > Myself, I almost exclusively run with earlycon serial and force
> > printk
> > to be early_printk() (effectively not using printk at all). This
> > works
> > perfectly fine and is the most reliable thing ever. I can push out
> > characters to the UART from any context.
> > 
> Great for development, not so good for production.
> 
> When something like an OOM kill happens, the amount
> of data printed by the kernel can take a long time
> to get flushed out a serial port.
> 
> In fact, it can take long enough to cause things
> like RCU stalls and soft lockups, and break
> workloads that way.
> 
> Having the serial console deferred allows those
> systems to survive.
> 
> If the system crashes before the messages were sent
> out the serial port, the dmesg buffer will be in
> the vmcore, and get extracted by the crash analysis
> tooling.
> 
> It sounds like the printk people may be right with
> making that behavior an option, since different
> scenarios require different behavior.

I've not hit OOM in a decade or so; I've no idea how much silly nonsense
it spouts. The example the printk people have used for a long while is
booting with a million block devices, apparently that generates insane
amount of boot noise -- another problem I don't have :-)

Anyway, one solution there is to create a threshold and say ERR and
above go directly to atomic console while everything below goes into the
buffer and gets spooled out later on the non-atomic variant.

This way all your WARN/BUG and other useful bits actually hit the wire
when you need them, and the useless verbiage goes into the bin for
later.

Anyway, all this is stuff that seems trivially solvable.
Acks / Tags Given (0)
No activity

Shakeel Butt

23 items
Patches Submitted (2)
Last comment 2026-06-11 [PATCH] mm/shrinker: do not hold RCU lock in shrinker_debugfs_count_show() Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch fixes a bug in the Linux kernel where the shrinker_debugfs_count_show function was holding an RCU lock while sleeping, which is not allowed. The function invokes the ->count_objects callback under rcu_read_lock, but this is unnecessary because mem_cgroup_iter takes care of RCU internally. The patch removes the unnecessary RCU lock and unlock calls, fixing the bug and preventing potential crashes. This fix addresses a problem that was introduced in commit 5035ebc644ae ('mm: shrinkers: introduce debugfs interface for memory shrinkers') and was reported by Zenghui Yu.

Last comment 2026-06-12 [swap tier discussion] Re: [PATCH v3 2/4] mm/zswap: Implement proactive writeback Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch proposes a new interface for swap tiering, similar to zswap, which allows setting limits on memory usage. The initial implementation will support only two values: 0 and max, effectively mirroring the existing behavior. This change aims to provide more fine-grained control over swap tiering.

1 reviewer comment — 0 participants
0 participants
Yosry Ahmed ↳ replying to Shakeel Butt Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer Yosry Ahmed suggested renaming the memory tiering interface to a swap tiering interface, proposing 'memory.swap.tiers.max' as a configuration option and recommending support for only two values (0/max) initially, with plans to expand incrementally.
Show original comment 
If we will have one interface for all the tiers for memory tiering,
I'd rather we do the swap for swap tiering. So maybe
memory.swap.tiers.max or memory.swap.tiered max?

The file can show the limits for all tiers when read, and maybe write
something like "echo 'tierX max' > memory.swap.tiers.max" to it to set
a new limit. We can support only 0/max for now to enable/disable
tiers. In the future, we can also allow something like "auto" to
automatically scale the limit based on the swapfile size and
memory.swap.max, similar to the direction memory tiering is heading
in.

I think we can start with just this interface for now, and expand
incrementally. For proactive zswap writeback, we can add
memory.swap.tiers.demote or something, and only support zswap
initially?

Reviews Given (16)
Last comment 2026-06-10 Re: [zswap?] BUG: sleeping function called from invalid context at kernel/cgroup/rstat.c:421 Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch addresses a bug where the function shrinker_debugfs_count_show() is calling zswap_shrinker_count() from an invalid context, potentially causing it to sleep. The issue arises because zswap_shrinker_count() triggers stats flushing, which can lead to sleeping. To fix this, the patch suggests removing unnecessary RCU locks and considering a long-term solution of eliminating the stats flushing altogether.

2 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Nhat Pham (author) ↳ replying to Shakeel Butt Neutral LLM
Author questioned the safety of accessing a shrinker within an RCU read section, pointing out that the shrinker is not freed until after the callback is registered.
Show original comment 
Hmm, so is this unsafe even with the current rcu shennanigans? What's
stopping shrinker to be freed by that callback before we enter
rcu_read_section()?

Seems like this is just implicitly correct - shrinker_debugfs_detach()
and shrinker_debugfs_remove() happens before call_rcu(&shrinker->rcu,
shrinker_free_rcu_cb);, so if you're reading this file, then it's
before shrinker_free_rcu_cb() is even registered?

Do we still need rcu or shrinker_try_get() here?
View on lore ↗
Signals: questioning, uncertainty
Shakeel Butt ↳ replying to Nhat Pham Inline Review Needs Work LLM
Reviewer noted that shrinker_debugfs_count_show() can sleep while holding a debugfs file reader lock, creating a deadlock scenario when parallel calls to shrinker_debugfs_remove() are made.
Show original comment 
I think you are right that we don't need rcu or shrinker_try_get() but it is
more about an active debugfs file reader. Suppose we are sleeping within rstat
flush from shrinker_debugfs_count_show() and there is a parallel
shrinker_debugfs_remove() call.

shrinker_debugfs_remove calls debugfs_remove_recursive and deep in the stack
there is a call wait_for_completion(&fsd->active_users_drained) which will wait
for active users, one of which is sleeping within rstat flush.

So, let's simply remove rcu read here.
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes
Re: [zswap?] BUG: sleeping function called from invalid context at kernel/cgroup/rstat.c:421 Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies
Re: [zswap?] BUG: sleeping function called from invalid context at kernel/cgroup/rstat.c:421 Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch resolves a bug where a sleeping function is called from an invalid context in the kernel's cgroup/rstat.c file, likely due to incorrect locking or synchronization. The problem was identified and addressed by another developer, Shakeel Butt, who sent a patch that has already been applied by Andrew. This message simply acknowledges receipt of the patch and thanks Shakeel for his contribution.

Last comment 2026-06-10 Re: [PATCH] mm/lruvec: trace LRU add drains and drain-all queuing Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch adds two new tracepoints to the Linux kernel's memory management (mm) subsystem: mm_lru_add_drain and mm_lru_drain_all_queue. The first tracepoint reports when an LRU add batch is drained, allowing tracing of partial drains and attribution to calling stacks. The second tracepoint captures when per-CPU drain work is queued for remote drain operations, including the requester stack and target CPU. These additions improve debugging capabilities by providing more detailed information about memory management events.

3 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants

don't see a strong need for a dedicated tracepoint.

a function, so kprobe/fentry are not always reliable.

Last comment 2026-06-08 Re: [PATCH 0/2] mm/vmpressure: reduce CPU, memory and code overhead on cgroup v2 Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

The patch aims to reduce CPU, memory, and code overhead associated with cgroup v2 by considering a change in the vmpressure implementation to make it memcg-v1 only.

3 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Usama Arif ↳ replying to Shakeel Butt Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer noted that vmpressure and PSI do not work out to be the same, and suggested a transition plan to switch socket pressure to PSI while keeping vmpressure around as an opt-in feature gated by a defconfig, to avoid potential regressions with increased memory usage or networking performance.
Show original comment 
I think there might be a transition needed? Because vmpressure and PSI
do not work out to be the same and people might notice a regression with
increased memory usage or a hit in networking performance and might want to
opt out? A solution might be to switch socket pressure to PSI while
keeping vmpressure around gated by a defconfig. And then in a few releases
remove it completely for cgroup v2 if no one complaints. If we go down that
path, we would need patch 2 for the medium term.
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes, suggested alternative approach
Shakeel Butt (author) ↳ replying to Usama Arif Needs Work LLM
Author acknowledged that transitioning cgroup v2 away from vmpressure would require multiple iterations and agreed to update the commit message with a midterm or transition plan.
Show original comment 
Yeah the reasoning that PSI is not an exact replacement for vmpressure makes
sense and it will take couple of iterations to transition v2 (networking) away
from vmpressure. Can you please update your commit message with this and about
the midterm or transition plan.

I assume eventually we will just have vmpressure-v1.c file which will be behind
MEMCG_V1 flag, correct?
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledged a fix is needed, agreed to restructure
Usama Arif ↳ replying to Shakeel Butt Reviewed-by Neutral LLM
Reviewer suggested rephrasing the commit message to clarify the long-term plan for vmpressure, specifically mentioning that it will be made CONFIG_MEMCG_V1 only after introducing a PSI-based socket-pressure path and keeping vmpressure available as an opt-out behind a defconfig.
Show original comment 
Yes.

How about something like below in the commit message? :

This split is the first step toward eventually making vmpressure
CONFIG_MEMCG_V1 only. The v2 in-kernel socket pressure path
(tree=false) cannot be removed today immediately: PSI is not an
exact replacement for vmpressure, and switching networking socket-buffer
back-off to PSI may regress networking performance or increase memory pressure
in workloads that today rely on vmpressure's hysteresis. The medium-term plan is
to introduce a PSI-based socket-pressure path, keep vmpressure available for
v2 behind a defconfig as an opt-out for several releases, and only then
drop the tree=false path entirely, at which point everything that
remains in mm/vmpressure-v1.c is the whole subsystem.
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested rephrasing of commit message

Re: [PATCH 0/2] mm/vmpressure: reduce CPU, memory and code overhead on cgroup v2 Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies
Last comment 2026-06-09 Re: [PATCH v3 2/4] mm/zswap: Implement proactive writeback Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch modifies the zswap shrink worker to writeback its cursor on a per-memcg basis. This change is necessary because proactive reclaim schemes can target cgroups and their children separately, but this can lead to inefficient reclaim if hotter memory in one cgroup is exhausted while colder memory in another cgroup is spared. The approach taken is to make the shrink worker's writeback cursor dependent on the memcg it is operating within.

5 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Shakeel Butt (author) Neutral LLM
Author acknowledged that the key option for zswap writeback trigger is preferred over an explicit interface and explained its advantages in terms of future proofing. Author acknowledged that future-proofing the zswap writeback trigger is necessary, considering potential additions/changes/new-features for this interface, such as triggering from a specific node or lowest memory tier. Author asked for input on whether zswap should be considered a top swap tier or something different.
Show original comment 
+Youngjun

On Mon, Jun 08, 2026 at 11:30:30AM -0700, Shakeel Butt wrote:

---

Youngjun is working on swap tiers. At the moment he is more interested in
allowing a specific swap device to a memcg or not. I can imagine in future there
will be use-cases where there will be a need to demote data on higher tier swap
to lower tier swap. What would be the appropriate interface?

BTW does zswap folks think of zswap as a top swap tier or something different?
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledged, explained, clarifying question
Yosry Ahmed ↳ replying to Shakeel Butt Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer noted that zswap writeback is similar to proactive reclaim and suggested applying the same semantics to memory.reclaim, including node-specific and age-based reclaim, which could be overloading a single interface Reviewer expressed uncertainty about the placement of zswap in the swap hierarchy, considering it as a top swap tier but acknowledging potential future complexity with memory tiers and compressed memory nodes.
Show original comment 
The way I see it, zswap writeback is just a "special" type of
proactive reclaim, but the goal is still proactively freeing cold
memory. In that regard, I think it makes sense to have things like
node-specific reclaim. Not sure about other extensions, but Hao
initially suggested making this age-based, so I think the answer is
yes.

For both of these examples (node-specific reclaim, age-based reclaim),
I think the same semantics could apply to memory.reclaim in general,
which is why I suggested making it a part of memory.reclaim. I also
like the idea of having a single proactive reclaim interface in
general, but maybe we don't want to overload it too much.

---

I haven't been following the swap tiers work closely, but personally I
do think of zswap as a top swap tier. Things will probably get more
blurry with memory tiers and compressed memory nodes though.
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes, suggested improvements, uncertainty
Shakeel Butt (author) ↳ replying to Yosry Ahmed Neutral LLM
Author agrees that the key option for zswap writeback trigger is more clean and future-proof, but expresses concern about potential duplication in setting limits for swap tiers. Author responded to Yosry Ahmed's feedback about the zswap writeback interface, stating that even if a key is used (zswap_writeback_only), there will still be a distinction between byte addressable and fault on access devices.
Show original comment 
Same for me though I imagine swap tiers would introduce some duplication i.e.
different way (interface) to set limits for swap tiers for a given memcg.

---

I think there will still be distinction between byte addressable and fault on
access devices.
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledges feedback, raises new concern, no clear resolution signal
Yosry Ahmed ↳ replying to Shakeel Butt Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer noted that the distinction between byte-addressable devices and fault-on-access devices is still maintained in this patch, but expressed concern about potential duplication of interface for setting limits on swap tiers within a given memcg.
Show original comment 
Yeah, I think it makes sense to define "swap" as fault on access
(zswap, SSD, etc), and memory tiers as byte-addressable (even if you
put an SSD behind CXL and make it byte-addressable). But I also
remember seeing discussions about unifying memory tiers and swap in a
way, and it makes sense from a reclaim perspective (swap or demote
first?).
View on lore ↗
Signals: potential duplication, interface for setting limits
YoungJun Park ↳ replying to Yosry Ahmed Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer noted that swap tiers would introduce duplication of setting limits for a given memcg and requested a different interface Reviewer YoungJun Park suggested integrating swap tiers into the existing zswap writeback interface, proposing to assign a target tier and allowing users to configure active tiers via memory.swap.tiers. He also proposed extending user-triggered demotion between swap tiers. Reviewer YoungJun Park agreed that zswap should be defined as the default, top-most tier in swap_tier and referenced his own early RFC where he allocated a separate tier for zswap Reviewer YoungJun Park noted that the proposed interface may introduce duplication with existing swap.max control, and suggested considering an alias for memory.zswap.writeback to set 'zswap only' as a tier
Show original comment 
On Mon, Jun 08, 2026 at 03:27:07PM -0700, Yosry Ahmed wrote:

+Chris +Kairui +Baoquan

Hello

Thanks for inviting me to the discussion, Shakeel.

---

Speaking of my work on swap tiers, I recently submitted a patch and am
currently considering memcg integration:
https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20260527062247.3440692-1-youngjun.park@lge.com/

The future use-cases imagined above seem to align with this
direction. (BTW, I am currently waiting for reviews/feedback from the memcg
folks on this patch. Any reviews would be highly appreciated!)

We could potentially assign a target tier
for writeback within the existing memory.zswap.writeback interface. 

For instance, '0' could mean disabled, while non-zero values could represent
specific tiers, which would maintain backward compatibility with the current
version. Alternatively, if zswap is treated as the default top tier, 
the `memory.swap.tiers` interface could potentially replace `memory.zswap.writeback`.

Furthermore, this could be expanded so that each swap tier can demote data
user-triggered demotion between swap tiers.

Based on the current patch's ideas combined with my swap tiers concept:

Assuming a hierarchy like:
zswap -> tier1 (SSD swap) -> tier2 (HDD swap) -> tier3 (Network swap)

We could configure the active tiers via a setting like `memory.swap.tiers`
(tier2 enabled, tier3 enabled).

For example, the concept of `echo "100M zswap_writeback_only > memory.reclaim"`
could be extended. A user could run `echo "100M tier2 > memory.reclaim"`
to explicitly trigger demotion from tier2 to tier3.
(BTW, if we combine these features, my personal preference for the keyword
format would be `<size> <demote_prefix><tier_name>`. I think it would be
better to explicitly indicate that it is a swap demotion by using a specific
prefix followed by the tier name. 
Or make demote prefix another key is also possible)

So, the whole picture would look something like this:
- memory.swap.tiers		: Interface for configuring the tier mask.
- memory.reclaim                : Entry point for user-triggered demotion.

---

Regarding zswap's position, I agree it needs to be defined as the default,
top-most tier in swap_tier. In my early RFC, I allocated a separate tier
specifically for zswap:
(https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20251109124947.1101520-3-youngjun.park@lge.com/)

---

I also agree with the concern about interface duplication. We will eventually
need a mechanism to control swap amounts per tier, which requires thinking
about its relationship with swap.max. (I raised this as an open question in
my early RFC).
https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20251109124947.1101520-1-youngjun.park@lge.com/
(Further Discussion and Open Questions Part)
However, since this feature is necessary anyway, wouldn't the proposed
interface be acceptable without causing conflicts at this early stage?

Additionally, `memory.zswap.writeback` seems redundant. Restricting a cgroup
to only use the zswap tier (assuming it's the first tier) is practically
identical to disabling `memory.zswap.writeback` (correct me if I'm wrong).
But there is no problem to integrate it as I think
e.g  `memory.zswap.writeback` could internally act as an alias for setting `memory.swap.tier` to 'zswap only'.

BR,
Youngjun Park
View on lore ↗
Signals: duplication, interface, requested changes
Last comment 2026-06-09 Re: [PATCH v3 2/4] mm/zswap: Implement proactive writeback Neutral LLM

This patch modifies the zswap shrink worker to writeback its cursor on a per-memcg basis. This change is necessary because proactive reclaim schemes can target cgroups and their children separately, but this can lead to inefficient reclaim if hotter memory in one cgroup is exhausted while colder memory in another cgroup is spared. The approach taken is to make the shrink worker's writeback cursor dependent on the memcg it is operating within.

4 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Yosry Ahmed ↳ replying to Nhat Pham Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer Yosry Ahmed noted that the unfairness of the per-memcg cursor is limited to the batch size per-invocation, which is relatively low even at its highest setting (0.5 MB), and suggested that reclaiming in larger steps would make shares more even. Reviewer Yosry Ahmed noted that the current implementation of proactive reclaim in zswap falls back to reclaiming in batches from each child cgroup because it doesn't accurately track coldest pages at the parent level, and suggested that ideally fairness shouldn't be a factor and instead reclaim the absolute coldest memory at the parent level. Reviewer Yosry Ahmed expressed concern about code complexity and unnecessary space overhead due to the introduction of per-memcg writeback cursor in the zswap shrink worker, specifically mentioning the zombie memcg cleanup as a contributing factor.
Show original comment 
Yes, the per-memcg cursor is more fair, and you can synthesize
scenarios that show that. However, I don't think this is a problem in
practice:

1. The unfairness is limited to the batch size per-invocation. If the
batch size is 128 pages (your highest one here), that's 0.5 MB (on
x86), which is fairly low? If the batch size is 32, it's even less.

2. Realistically, if you have a parent cgroup with with >10G of
memory, you wouldn't be reclaiming in steps of 1M. If you want to
reclaim 200MB, why are you doing it over 200 invocations? If you do it
in a single one (or over a few retries) the shares should become much
more even.

We're trying to fix a practical use case, not finding reasons why a
simple implementation won't work -- right?

More below (to Nhat's point).

---

I would go a bit farther and claim that ideally fairness shouldn't
even be a factor. If you invoke proactive reclaim on a parent cgroup
with 100MB, you want to reclaim the coldest 100MB in that parent, no
matter what child they reside in. If one child cgroup has 100% hot
memory and one child cgroup has 100% cold memory, ideally you'd
reclaim all the cold memory from the second child.

However, the implementation of the LRUs and the coldness tracking
doesn't allow for doing this, so we "fallback" to reclaiming in
batches from each child because we don't really know where the coldest
pages overall are. If that changes in the future (somehow), I argue
that the correct thing to do is reclaim the absolute coldest memory at
the parent level.

If you want to reclaim evenly among the children, you can do that and
directly reclaim from the children.

---

Mostly the complexity (e.g. the zombie memcg cleanup) and a tiny bit
the unnecessary space (8 bytes is not a lot, but these things add up).
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes, suggested improvements, suggested alternative approach
Yosry Ahmed ↳ replying to Nhat Pham Reviewed-by Neutral LLM
Yosry Ahmed noted that the unfairness in the patch is limited to the batch size per-invocation, and suggested that it should be fine as long as the amount is not divided over a large number of iterations.
Show original comment 
Forgot to respond to this part, the unfairness is limited to the batch
size per-invocation, so it should be fine as long as you don't divide
the amount over 100 iterations for some reason. Also yes, all memory
in zswap is cold, the relative coldness is not that important (e.g.
compared to relative coldness during reclaim).
View on lore ↗
Signals: concern about fairness, clarification on cold memory
Nhat Pham (author) ↳ replying to Yosry Ahmed Needs Work LLM
Author acknowledged reviewer's point about potential inefficiency in reclaim and agreed to shelve per-memcg cursor for the next version, indicating that a fix is planned but not immediate.
Show original comment 
Ok then yeah, I think we should shelve per-memcg cursor for the next
version. Down the line, if we have more data that unfairness is an
issue, we can always fix it. One step at a time :)
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledged reviewer's concern, agreed to shelve feature
Hao Jia ↳ replying to Nhat Pham Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer Hao Jia noted that the per-memcg cursor approach may lead to inefficient reclaim, where hotter memory of one cgroup is exhausted while colder memory of another cgroup is spared, and suggested dropping the per-memcg cursor in favor of reusing the root cgroup cursor logic.
Show original comment 
Thanks a lot to Yosry, Nhat, and Shakeel for the great suggestions!

Let me summarize what I plan to do in the next version to make sure we 
are on the same page:

  - Drop the per-memcg cursor and keep the root cgroup cursor 
(zswap_next_shrink) logic intact.
  - Stick to using the zswap_writeback_only key, and change the 
proactive writeback size to use the compressed size.
  - Consolidate and reuse the logic between shrink_worker() and 
shrink_memcg(). Enable batch writeback in the shrink_worker() path, 
while keeping the writeback behavior in the zswap_store() path unchanged.

Please let me know if I missed or misunderstood anything. Thanks again 
for clearing things up!

Thanks\uff0c
Hao
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes
Last comment 2026-06-09 Re: [PATCH v3 2/4] mm/zswap: Implement proactive writeback Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch modifies the zswap shrink worker to writeback its cursor on a per-memcg basis. This change is necessary because proactive reclaim schemes can target cgroups and their children separately, but this can lead to inefficient reclaim if hotter memory in one cgroup is exhausted while colder memory in another cgroup is spared. The approach taken is to make the shrink worker's writeback cursor dependent on the memcg it is operating within.

(zswap, SSD, etc), and memory tiers as byte-addressable (even if you
put an SSD behind CXL and make it byte-addressable). But I also
remember seeing discussions about unifying memory tiers and swap in a

+Chris +Kairui +Baoquan

Hello

Thanks for inviting me to the discussion, Shakeel.

---

Speaking of my work on swap tiers, I recently submitted a patch and am
currently considering memcg integration:
https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20260527062247.3440692-1-youngjun.park@lge.com/

The future use-cases imagined above seem to align with this
direction. (BTW, I am currently waiting for reviews/feedback from the memcg
folks on this patch. Any reviews would be highly appreciated!)

We could potentially assign a target tier
for writeback within the existing memory.zswap.writeback interface. 

For instance, '0' could mean disabled, while non-zero values could represent
specific tiers, which would maintain backward compatibility with the current
version. Alternatively, if zswap is treated as the default top tier, 
the `memory.swap.tiers` interface could potentially replace `memory.zswap.writeback`.

Furthermore, this could be expanded so that each swap tier can demote data
user-triggered demotion between swap tiers.

Based on the current patch's ideas combined with my swap tiers concept:

Assuming a hierarchy like:
zswap -> tier1 (SSD swap) -> tier2 (HDD swap) -> tier3 (Network swap)

We could configure the active tiers via a setting like `memory.swap.tiers`
(tier2 enabled, tier3 enabled).

For example, the concept of `echo "100M zswap_writeback_only > memory.reclaim"`
could be extended. A user could run `echo "100M tier2 > memory.reclaim"`
to explicitly trigger demotion from tier2 to tier3.
(BTW, if we combine these features, my personal preference for the keyword
format would be `<size> <demote_prefix><tier_name>`. I think it would be
better to explicitly indicate that it is a swap demotion by using a specific
prefix followed by the tier name. 
Or make demote prefix another key is also possible)

So, the whole picture would look something like this:
- memory.swap.tiers		: Interface for configuring the tier mask.
- memory.reclaim                : Entry point for user-triggered demotion.

---

Regarding zswap's position, I agree it needs to be defined as the default,
top-most tier in swap_tier. In my early RFC, I allocated a separate tier
specifically for zswap:
(https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20251109124947.1101520-3-youngjun.park@lge.com/)

---

I also agree with the concern about interface duplication. We will eventually
need a mechanism to control swap amounts per tier, which requires thinking
about its relationship with swap.max. (I raised this as an open question in
my early RFC).

(Further Discussion and Open Questions Part)
However, since this feature is necessary anyway, wouldn't the proposed
interface be acceptable without causing conflicts at this early stage?

Additionally, `memory.zswap.writeback` seems redundant. Restricting a cgroup
to only use the zswap tier (assuming it's the first tier) is practically
identical to disabling `memory.zswap.writeback` (correct me if I'm wrong).
But there is no problem to integrate it as I think
e.g  `memory.zswap.writeback` could internally act as an alias for setting `memory.swap.tier` to 'zswap only'.

BR,
Youngjun Park

Signals: duplication, interface, requested changes
Last comment 2026-06-12 Re: [PATCH v3 2/4] mm/zswap: Implement proactive writeback Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch implements proactive writeback for zswap, which is a key component in Linux's memory management system. The goal is to improve the performance of zswap by reducing the time it takes to swap out pages from RAM. This is achieved through a new mechanism that anticipates and writes back dirty pages before they are actually swapped out.

1 reviewer comment — 0 participants
0 participants
YoungJun Park ↳ replying to Shakeel Butt Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer YoungJun Park suggested merging the proposed swap_tier interface, proposing two alternatives if consensus is difficult: making zswap the first tier and handling memory.zswap.writeback internally or merging the swap_tier infrastructure itself first.
Show original comment 
I am looking forward to the discussion on this interface!

To help boost the discussion and progress, I would like to share a few of my thoughts.
We could either introduce a new interface to trigger demotion/promotion,
or we could reuse the existing one (using tier just internally)

Based on the memcg interface currently proposed in swap_tier
(memory.swap.tiers, memory.swap.tiers.effective), I think it aligns well
with the current direction. It provides a foundation for selectively
targeting devices in tier order.

To summarize the discussions so far, the following points align well.

- Per-cgroup swap control, as I suggested.
- Proactive zswap writeback (Hao's usecase)
- Swap device target demotion(if it wants selective, then it is more better), as you mentioned:
  https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/aicZ-5GX9De3MAU7@linux.dev/
- Virtual Swap on/off in the future, as Nhat mentioned:
  https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20260528212955.1912856-1-nphamcs@gmail.com/
- The memory.zswap.writeback alternative (no hierarchy model conflict)
- zswap is first swap tier.
- Promotion. (Also better for selectve usage)
- tier based swap policy (e.g round-robin...)

To accelerate this work, I believe we should reach a consensus and
merge the currently proposed swap_tier interface :)

If the above approach is difficult, I would like to suggest an
alternative for progress with the memcg interfaces removed:

1) We could make zswap the first tier and create
a use case where memory.zswap.writeback internally is handled by tier logic.

2) Or simply merge the swap_tier infrastructure itself first.

This would allow the swap_tier infrastructure to be merged and discussed
more easily.

If it takes longer to adopt swap_tier anyway, by doing so we progress next step
as a experimental feature.

- Apply per-cgroup swap as an experimental (debugfs) feature.
- Apply Hao's use case experimentally or as it is as Yosry suggested.
(future migration to swap tier)

How do you think?

(FYI: My emails to kernel.org are failing due to internal server issues.)

Thank you 
Youngjun Park
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes, suggested alternatives

Signals: WAITING_FOR_REVIEW
Last comment 2026-06-12 Re: [RFC PATCH 1/3] mm: avoid unnecessary lru drain for wp_can_reuse_anon_folio() Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch optimizes the handling of anonymous folios by reusing them directly from the LRU add cache under certain conditions, avoiding unnecessary LRU drain operations.

6 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Shakeel Butt (author) Neutral LLM
Author responded to a question about the effectiveness of their patch by explaining that they could optimize the check to reuse the folio directly from the LRU cache if certain conditions are met.
Show original comment 
Actually if we can peek into lru_add cache and folio_ref_count(folio) is exactly
equal to (2 + folio_test_swapcache(folio)) and folio is not on LRU then we can
just reuse folio if it is in lru_add cache without draining, right?
View on lore ↗
Signals: clarification, explanation
Baoquan He ↳ replying to Shakeel Butt Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
reviewer suggested that instead of reusing a folio when it's not on LRU, we should only touch the wanted folio if it exists in pvec, which can improve efficiency more than the current patch
Show original comment 
Sounds more reasonable if we can only touch the wanted folio if it
exists in pvec. Believe it can improve efficiency more than the
current patch.
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes, suggested improvement
Barry Song ↳ replying to Shakeel Butt Inline Review Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
reviewer noted that !folio_test_lru(folio) does not guarantee the folio is in lru_cache, and suggested adding a separate flag to indicate lru_cache membership or scanning the cache instead
Show original comment 
The real problem is that !folio_test_lru(folio) does not tell us
whether the folio is still sitting in lru_cache, and determining that
is unlikely to be cheap. We could either scan the lru_cache or add a
separate flag to indicate lru_cache membership, but neither option
sounds particularly appealing.

Thanks
Barry
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes
Barry Song ↳ replying to Baoquan He Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer noted that !folio_test_lru(folio) does not distinguish between a folio being removed from LRU or still sitting in the lru_cache, making it difficult to determine if a folio is in the lru_add cache without scanning the entire cache. He suggested either adding a dedicated folio flag (PG_lru-like) to indicate lru_cache membership or dropping the drain altogether and releasing the folio later.
Show original comment 
Technically yes, but in practice it seems quite hard. As I explained
to Shakeel, we don't have an easy way to determine whether a folio is
sitting in the lru_cache. !folio_test_lru(folio) only tells us that
the folio may either have been removed from the LRU or still be
sitting in the lru_cache; it does not distinguish between the two.
Unless we want to scan the lru_cache to look up the folio, we may need
a dedicated folio flag, similar to PG_lru, to indicate lru_cache
membership -
I guess people would be quite unhappy about adding a new flag?

On the other hand, the folio may be sitting in the lru_cache of
another CPU, which the current drain cannot flush. As things stand
today, the drain only succeeds if the folio happens to be queued on
the current CPU's lru_cache, giving it roughly a 1/nr_cpus chance of
working. drain_all would clearly be too expensive to avoid.

So another possibility is to drop this drain as well. The folio can
be released later, at the cost of missing some opportunities for
reuse.

Best Regards
Barry
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes, alternative solutions
Baoquan He ↳ replying to Barry Song Reviewed-by Neutral LLM
Reviewer Baoquan He noted that BAM sitting in another CPU's pvec is a barrier to reusing the folio, but agreed that the current solution is optimal when refcount equals (2 + folio_test_swapcache(folio))
Show original comment 
BAM, sitting in other CPU's pvec is a real barrier. If it's in local
cpu, it definitely deserves a try as Shakeel said, when refcount = (2 +
folio_test_swapcache(folio)). 

Then the current solution is optimal at present.
View on lore ↗
Signals: agreed with Shakeel's suggestion, considered current solution optimal
Barry Song ↳ replying to Shakeel Butt Neutral LLM
Reviewer Barry Song analyzed the patch's effectiveness by booting the system into Ubuntu and observing a significant reduction in LRU drains, specifically noting that wp_can_reuse_anon_folio() still skips a substantial number of drains even under heavy swap-in workloads.
Show original comment 
This is a very good question. I booted the system into Ubuntu, and after
boot completed I observed:

wp_reuse_skipped_drain: 5542
do_swap_skipped_drain: 0

Then I built the kernel in a 1GB memcg using zRAM swap, and observed:

wp_reuse_skipped_drain: 25017
do_swap_skipped_drain: 43595

So in summary, even without swap-in we can save a significant number of
drains in wp_can_reuse_anon_folio(). With heavy swap-in workloads, most
of the savings come from avoiding drains in do_swap_page(), while we
still see a substantial number of skipped drains in wp_can_reuse_anon_folio().

Best Regards
Barry
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested additional data, provided analysis

Show original comment 
The real problem is that !folio_test_lru(folio) does not tell us
whether the folio is still sitting in lru_cache, and determining that
is unlikely to be cheap. We could either scan the lru_cache or add a
separate flag to indicate lru_cache membership, but neither option
sounds particularly appealing.

Thanks
Barry
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes
Barry Song ↳ replying to Baoquan He Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer noted that !folio_test_lru(folio) does not distinguish between a folio being removed from LRU or still sitting in the lru_cache, making it difficult to determine if a folio is in the lru_add cache without scanning the entire cache. He suggested either adding a dedicated folio flag (PG_lru-like) to indicate lru_cache membership or dropping the drain altogether and releasing the folio later.
Show original comment 
Technically yes, but in practice it seems quite hard. As I explained
to Shakeel, we don't have an easy way to determine whether a folio is
sitting in the lru_cache. !folio_test_lru(folio) only tells us that
the folio may either have been removed from the LRU or still be
sitting in the lru_cache; it does not distinguish between the two.
Unless we want to scan the lru_cache to look up the folio, we may need
a dedicated folio flag, similar to PG_lru, to indicate lru_cache
membership -
I guess people would be quite unhappy about adding a new flag?

On the other hand, the folio may be sitting in the lru_cache of
another CPU, which the current drain cannot flush. As things stand
today, the drain only succeeds if the folio happens to be queued on
the current CPU's lru_cache, giving it roughly a 1/nr_cpus chance of
working. drain_all would clearly be too expensive to avoid.

So another possibility is to drop this drain as well. The folio can
be released later, at the cost of missing some opportunities for
reuse.

Best Regards
Barry
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes, alternative solutions
Baoquan He ↳ replying to Barry Song Reviewed-by Neutral LLM
Reviewer Baoquan He noted that BAM sitting in another CPU's pvec is a barrier to reusing the folio, but agreed that the current solution is optimal when refcount equals (2 + folio_test_swapcache(folio))
Show original comment 
BAM, sitting in other CPU's pvec is a real barrier. If it's in local
cpu, it definitely deserves a try as Shakeel said, when refcount = (2 +
folio_test_swapcache(folio)). 

Then the current solution is optimal at present.
View on lore ↗
Signals: agreed with Shakeel's suggestion, considered current solution optimal
Acks / Tags Given (5)
Re: [PATCH v3] mm/lruvec: trace LRU add drains and drain-all requests Acked-by Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies
Last comment 2026-06-08 Re: [PATCH] mm/swap_state: remove unnecessary lru_add_drain() from readahead Acked-by Neutral LLM

Patch to remove unnecessary lru_add_drain() from readahead, initially acknowledged by Shakeel Butt.

2 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Shakeel Butt Acked-by Neutral LLM
Acknowledged the patch and suggested adding tracepoints to track lru_add_drain_all() callers, but did not review the code itself.
Signals: ACKNOWLEDGMENT
JP Kobryn ↳ replying to Shakeel Butt Positive LLM
Confirmed that he will send a patch to add tracepoints for lru_add_drain_all() callers.
Show original comment 
On 6/8/26 9:06 AM, Shakeel Butt wrote:
> On Mon, Jun 08, 2026 at 07:32:42AM -0700, Usama Arif wrote:
>> swap_cluster_readahead() and swap_vma_readahead() end the readahead
>> loop with an explicit lru_add_drain() call. That drain is a leftover
>> from 2.6.12 era code and serves no functional purpose for the callers:
>>
>> - do_swap_page() ignores LRU residency for the readahead folios;
>>   it only needs the target folio it called swapin_readahead() for,
>>   and if the write-fault path needs the target folio on the LRU to count
>>   references accurately, it runs its own lru_add_drain() at the
>>   wp_can_reuse_anon_folio() and do_swap_page() sites.
>>
>> - shmem_swapin_cluster() immediately locks the  returned folio, waits
>>   for writeback, then operates on it - LRU residency of either the target
>>   or the readahead folios is irrelevant.
>>
>> - try_to_unuse() likewise locks the folio and calls unuse_pte() without
>>   depending on LRU presence.
>>
>> Folios newly added to the swap cache by the readahead loop sit in
>> the per-CPU LRU folio_batch and will be drained naturally as the
>> batch fills (FOLIO_BATCH_SIZE),by the next reclaim/compaction
>> lru_add_drain_all() and so on.  The unconditional drain only
>> synchronously flushes a partial batch and forces contention on
>> lruvec_lock.
>>
>> On a 176-CPU production host running a memory-pressured workload, this
>> path was observed to call folio_batch_move_lru() from
>> swap_cluster_readahead() ~28K/min, a very large source of LRU lock
>> traffic.
>>
>> This is a direct continuation of the cleanup started in commit
>> 1aa43598c03b ("mm: remove unnecessary calls to lru_add_drain") which
>> removed the equivalent drain from free_pages_and_swap_cache() with
>> the same rationale. A detailed reasoning for this is present in [1].
>>
>> Remove both drains.
>>
>> [1] https://lore.kernel.org/all/dca2824e8e88e826c6b260a831d79089b5b9c79d.camel@surriel.com/T/#u
>>
>> Signed-off-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
> 
> Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>
> 
> Thanks for pushing this. JP was also looking into LRU lock contention sources.
> Particularly we lack visibiluty into the lru_add_drain_all() callers. The idea
> was to add tracepoints to tracks such callers. (Just nudging you towards it :P)

Yup, I'll send a patch for that one.
Signals: AGREEMENT
Re: [PATCH 1/2] mm/vmpressure: skip tree=true accounting on cgroup v2 Acked-by Neutral LLM
Re: [RFC PATCH 2/3] mm: drop stale folio_ref_count()==1 check in do_swap_page reuse logic Acked-by Neutral LLM
Re: [RFC PATCH 3/3] mm: entirely remove lru_add_drain in do_swap_page Acked-by Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies

Usama Arif

22 items
Patches Submitted (6)
Last comment 2026-06-12 [PATCH] block: invalidate cached plug timestamp after task switch Neutral LLM

The patch addresses a bug in the block layer where cached plug timestamps are not invalidated after task switches, leading to stale vnow values and incorrect throttle decisions. It moves the invalidation logic from sched_update_worker() to finish_task_switch(), which is called after every context switch.

1 reviewer comment — 0 participants
0 participants
Peter Zijlstra ↳ replying to Usama Arif Neutral LLM
Suggested simplifying the code by moving the branch into the blk_plug_invalidate_ts() function and making it __always_inline.
Show original comment 
On Thu, Jun 11, 2026 at 04:14:28PM -0700, Usama Arif wrote:
> blk_time_get_ns() caches ktime_get_ns() in current->plug->cur_ktime
> and marks the task with PF_BLOCK_TS. That cache is only valid while the
> task keeps running; if the task is switched out, wall-clock time
> advances and the cached value must not be reused when the task runs again.
> 
> The existing invalidation covers explicit plug flushes through
> __blk_flush_plug(), and the schedule() / rtmutex paths through
> sched_update_worker(). It does not cover in-kernel preemption paths such
> as preempt_schedule(), preempt_schedule_notrace(), and
> preempt_schedule_irq(), which enter __schedule(SM_PREEMPT) directly and
> return without calling sched_update_worker().
> 
> As a result, a task preempted while holding a plug with PF_BLOCK_TS set
> can reuse a stale plug->cur_ktime after it is scheduled back in. blk-iocost
> then consumes that stale timestamp through ioc_now(), producing stale vnow
> values for throttle decisions, and through ioc_rqos_done(), inflating
> on-queue time and feeding false missed-QoS samples into vrate
> adjustment.
> 
> Move the schedule-side invalidation to finish_task_switch(), which runs
> for the scheduled-in task after every actual context switch regardless
> of which schedule entry point was used. Keep __blk_flush_plug() as the
> explicit flush/finish-plug invalidation path, and remove only the
> PF_BLOCK_TS handling from sched_update_worker().
> 
> Fixes: 06b23f92af87 ("block: update cached timestamp post schedule/preemption")
> Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
> Signed-off-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
> ---
>  kernel/sched/core.c | 13 +++++++++----
>  1 file changed, 9 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/kernel/sched/core.c b/kernel/sched/core.c
> index 8b791e9e9f67..bf024ca115ff 100644
> --- a/kernel/sched/core.c
> +++ b/kernel/sched/core.c
> @@ -5368,6 +5368,13 @@ static struct rq *finish_task_switch(struct task_struct *prev)
>  	 */
>  	kmap_local_sched_in();
>  
> +	/*
> +	 * Any cached block-layer timestamp (plug->cur_ktime) is stale now,
> +	 * invalidate it.
> +	 */
> +	if (unlikely(current->flags & PF_BLOCK_TS))
> +		blk_plug_invalidate_ts(current);

Can you make that just blk_plug_invalidate_ts() and move the branch into
the function itself, which is already inline anyway (but perhaps upgrade
it to __always_inline).
Signals: NEEDS_WORK
Last comment 2026-06-12 [PATCH v2] block: invalidate cached plug timestamp after task switch Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch fixes a bug where the cached plug timestamp is not invalidated after a task switch, leading to stale vnow values and incorrect throttle decisions in blk-iocost. The existing invalidation mechanism only covers explicit plug flushes and schedule/preemption paths, but not in-kernel preemption paths like preempt_schedule(). To fix this, the patch moves the invalidation to finish_task_switch(), which runs for the scheduled-in task after every actual context switch. This ensures that the cached timestamp is always up-to-date and accurate.

3 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Peter Zijlstra ↳ replying to Usama Arif Needs Work LLM
Reviewer Peter Zijlstra suggested reducing the code in blk_plug_invalidate_ts() by assuming that PF_BLOCK_TS is always set when current->plug exists, but noted this assumption requires a guarantee that PF_BLOCK_TS is only ever set under these conditions.
Show original comment 
If you can guarantee PF_BLOCK_TS is only ever set when current->plug,
this can be reduced further.
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes
Usama Arif (author) ↳ replying to Peter Zijlstra Needs Work LLM
Author acknowledged that the copy_process() function doesn't strip PF_BLOCK_TS from inherited flags, and provided a patch to fix this issue by adding PF_BLOCK_TS to the flags cleared in copy_process().
Show original comment 
Thanks for the reviews!

The invariant holds at set time (the only set in blk_time_get_ns() is
gated by if (!plug)) and through the only legitimate plug clear in
blk_finish_plug() (which goes through __blk_flush_plug() that clears
PF_BLOCK_TS first).

However, copy_process() sets p->plug = NULL for the child but doesn't
strip PF_BLOCK_TS from the inherited flags.

I think the if(plug) is a good defensive check, but can also do the below
if you prefer?

diff --git a/include/linux/blkdev.h b/include/linux/blkdev.h
index 1c1fd31ce187..c285a4d9837d 100644
--- a/include/linux/blkdev.h
+++ b/include/linux/blkdev.h
@@ -1219,10 +1219,7 @@ static inline void blk_flush_plug(struct blk_plug *plug, bool async)
 static __always_inline void blk_plug_invalidate_ts(void)
 {
        if (unlikely(current->flags & PF_BLOCK_TS)) {
-               struct blk_plug *plug = current->plug;
-
-               if (plug)
-                       plug->cur_ktime = 0;
+               current->plug->cur_ktime = 0;
                current->flags &= ~PF_BLOCK_TS;
        }
 }
diff --git a/kernel/fork.c b/kernel/fork.c
index 892a95214c54..9a062149e0d8 100644
--- a/kernel/fork.c
+++ b/kernel/fork.c
@@ -2167,7 +2167,8 @@ __latent_entropy struct task_struct *copy_process(
                goto bad_fork_cleanup_count;
 
        delayacct_tsk_init(p);  /* Must remain after dup_task_struct() */
-       p->flags &= ~(PF_SUPERPRIV | PF_WQ_WORKER | PF_IDLE | PF_NO_SETAFFINITY);
+       p->flags &= ~(PF_SUPERPRIV | PF_WQ_WORKER | PF_IDLE | PF_NO_SETAFFINITY |
+                     PF_BLOCK_TS);
        p->flags |= PF_FORKNOEXEC;
        INIT_LIST_HEAD(&p->children);
        INIT_LIST_HEAD(&p->sibling);
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledged a problem, provided a fix
Usama Arif (author) ↳ replying to Peter Zijlstra Neutral LLM
Author clarifies that they misunderstood reviewer's feedback, but now agrees to simplify the code by removing the plug check and clearing the PF_BLOCK_TS flag in fork() instead.
Show original comment 
ah sorry didnt understand you completely, by extra lines do you mean keep
the if(plug) or get rid of it and clear the flag in fork?

I though more about it and I think its much nicer to get rid of the if(plug)
and clear the flag in fork.
View on lore ↗
Signals: clarification, reconsideration
Last comment 2026-06-09 [PATCH] fs/super: skip non-memcg-aware nr_cached_objects in memcg slab shrink Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch modifies the Linux kernel's super_block shrinker to skip non-memcg-aware nr_cached_objects hooks during per-memcg slab reclaim. The issue arises when memcg-heavy slab reclaim is performed, causing hundreds of duplicate work items to be queued and resulting in wasted CPU cycles. To address this, the patch restricts the invocation of these global fs-specific hooks to only occur on the root/global shrink path, ensuring that the actual reclaim happens on the global path rather than per-memcg.

2 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Jan Kara ↳ replying to Usama Arif Reviewed-by Neutral LLM
Reviewer Jan Kara expressed surprise that the XFS inode shrinker is not memcg-aware, but agreed with the patch change; he also suggested notifying the XFS developers about this.
Show original comment 
To me this makes sense. However I'm bit surprised that XFS inode shrinker
(which is what gets counted in nr_cached_objects for XFS) isn't memcg
aware. I guess since these inodes are on their way to a relatively quick
destruction, nobody really bothered. So I'm fine with the change, just
I'd like to make sure XFS folks are aware and don't plan anything in this
area.

								Honza
View on lore ↗
Signals: agreement, request for notification
Usama Arif (author) ↳ replying to Jan Kara Needs Work LLM
Author acknowledged that feedback on the patch is needed from xfs and btrfs maintainers, so they can raise any objections before a potential revision.
Show original comment 
Thanks for the review!

Yes I am hoping that if there are any objections from xfs or btrfs, it gets
raised. Have cc'ed the btrfs and xfs maintainers and reviewers.

Thanks!
Usama
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledged need for further review, cc'ed relevant maintainers
Ongoing Submitted 2026-06-02 Last comment 2026-06-08 ⏱ No recent activity [PATCH] mm/mincore: handle non-swap entries before !CONFIG_SWAP guard Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch fixes a bug in the Linux kernel's mincore function where it incorrectly reports non-swap entries as non-resident on systems without swap enabled. The fix involves moving the check for !CONFIG_SWAP below the early return for non-swap entries, ensuring that only true swap entries trigger warnings and non-shmem pages follow the existing 'uptodate / non-shmem' path.

9 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants

Lorenzo Stoakes ↳ replying to Usama Arif Inline Review Reviewed-by Neutral LLM
reviewer noted that the original patch moved the !IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_SWAP) guard below the non-swap-entry check as intended to prevent spurious WARNs on !CONFIG_SWAP kernels The reviewer noted that the patch's change is unnecessary because the condition in mincore_swap() will already be false for !CONFIG_SWAP builds, and thus the WARN_ON(1) and return statement are redundant.
Show original comment 
Logic LGTM so:

Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes <ljs@kernel.org>

Small note below but I think not a problem.

Thanks, Lorenzo

---

I guess fine to do this after software_is_swap() because for !CONFIG_SWAP this
will always evaluate false?

Not a big deal therefore I think.
View on lore ↗
Signals: LGTM, requested changes
Usama Arif (author) ↳ replying to Lorenzo Stoakes Neutral LLM
Author acknowledged that the softleaf_is_swap() function isn't structurally gated by CONFIG_SWAP, but its callers are, and thus it will always return false on !CONFIG_SWAP builds.
Show original comment 
Yes, thats right. In softleaf_is_swap(), softleaf_type() returns SOFTLEAF_SWAP
iff type_num < MAX_SWAPFILES. MAX_SWAPFILES is a compile-time constant independent
of CONFIG_SWAP, so softleaf_is_swap() isn't structurally gated, but every producer
of real swap entries lives under CONFIG_SWAP.
With !CONFIG_SWAP none of those run, so softleaf_is_swap() is always false.
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledged a technical detail, provided an explanation
Johannes Weiner ↳ replying to Usama Arif Acked-by Positive Heuristic
Gave Acked-by

reviewer noted that the patch requires formal Acked-by tag from the stable maintainers, as it contains a Fixes: line and -stable maintainers have been asked not to auto-accept such patches
Show original comment 
It's required for MM patches because we've asked the -stable
maintainers to not auto-pick everything which has a Fixes:.
